topics.title: Introduction to Linear Programming Problems and Convex Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Linear Programming Problems and Convex Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linear Programming Problem (LPP) — Formal Academic Definition

A **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** is a mathematical optimization model used to determine the **best possible outcome** (such as maximum profit or minimum cost) from a given set of choices, where both the **objective function** and all the **constraints** are expressed as **linear functions** of the decision variables.

Mathematically, an LPP is an optimization problem of the form:

$$
\text{Optimize } Z = c_1x_1 + c_2x_2 + \dots + c_nx_n
$$

$$
\text{Subject to: } a_{11}x_1 + a_{11}x_2 + \dots + a_{1n}x_n \;\; (\leq, =, \geq) \;\; b_1
$$

$$
\dots
$$

$$
\dots
$$

$$
\dots
$$

$$
a_{m1}x_1 + a_{m2}x_2 + \dots + a_{mn}x_n \;\; (\leq, =, \geq) \;\; b_m
$$

$$
x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \geq 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LPP belongs to the family of **convex optimization problems**. This is the foundational reason why LPPs possess the most powerful and reliable solution guarantee: any *local optimum* is always a **global optimum**, provided a feasible region exists.

---

## 1.2 Essential Components of an LPP

Every Linear Programming Problem, irrespective of its application domain, is built using **four fundamental building blocks**. These are tested in KTU Part A questions almost every cycle.

| # | Component | Description | KTU Notation |
|---|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Decision Variables** | The controllable quantities whose values we wish to determine | $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ |
| 2 | **Objective Function** | A linear mathematical expression measuring the quality of the decision (profit / cost / time) | $Z = c_1x_1 + c_2x_2 + \dots + c_nx_n$ |
| 3 | **Constraints** | Linear inequalities/equalities representing restrictions on resources | $\sum_j a_{ij}x_j \leq b_i$ |
| 4 | **Non-Negativity Restrictions** | Sign restrictions ensuring variables represent physical quantities | $x_j \geq 0 \;\; \forall j$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical vs Standard Form Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Standard Form:** All constraints are of type $\leq$, RHS $\geq 0$, all $x_j \geq 0$, objective is maximization.
> - **Canonical Form:** All constraints are of type $\geq$, RHS $\geq 0$, all $x_j \geq 0$, objective is minimization (used in dual).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The LPP "Bakery Analogy"

Imagine a small bakery called **"KTU Bakers"** that produces two products: **Cakes ($x_1$)** and **Pastries ($x_2$)**.

- The bakery wants to **maximize daily profit** $Z = 50x_1 + 30x_2$.
- The oven can work only **40 hours/day** → $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 40$.
- The flour stock is limited to **60 kg/day** → $3x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 60$.
- The owner **cannot produce negative cakes** → $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The bakery has a *limited world* of possible production plans (the **feasible region**). Within that limited world, it must pick the *single best point* that yields maximum profit. The LPP is exactly this: find the **best feasible decision** under **linear** rules.

This bakery problem has a finite, structured universe of solutions because every relationship is **linear** (straight lines, flat planes). This linearity is the key reason we can solve LPPs with finite, mechanical methods like the **Simplex Method**.

---

## 1.4 Convex Set — Formal Definition

A set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is called a **Convex Set** if for every pair of points $x, y \in S$ and for every scalar $\lambda \in [0, 1]$, the line segment joining $x$ and $y$ is **entirely contained** in $S$.

Mathematically:

$$
\forall \; x, y \in S, \quad \forall \; \lambda \in [0, 1]: \quad \lambda x + (1 - \lambda) y \in S
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** A set is convex if it has **no "dents" or "holes"** when viewed in 2D or 3D. A circle, square, triangle, half-plane, and polyhedron are all convex. A crescent moon, star, or donut are **non-convex**.

---

## 1.5 Convex Function — Formal Definition

A function $f: S \to \mathbb{R}$ defined on a convex set $S$ is called a **Convex Function** if for every pair of points $x, y \in S$ and for every $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$
f(\lambda x + (1 - \lambda)y) \leq \lambda f(x) + (1 - \lambda)f(y)
$$

If the inequality is strict for all $x \neq y$, the function is **strictly convex**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** A convex function **lies below** the chord (line segment) joining any two of its points. A concave function **lies above** the chord. The function $f(x) = x^2$ is convex; $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ is concave.

---

## 1.6 Convexity — The "Why" Behind LPP Success

The reason LPP is so beautifully solvable lies in the deep connection between **linear functions** and **convexity**.

- Every **linear function** is **both convex and concave** simultaneously.
- The **feasible region of an LPP is the intersection of half-planes (or half-spaces)**, and the intersection of convex sets is convex.
- Therefore, the feasible region of every LPP is a **convex polyhedron** (or polytope).
- Since linear objective functions have a unique global minimum and maximum (in the absence of degeneracy), the optimum of an LPP must lie at an **extreme point (vertex/corner)** of this convex feasible region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This property is the theoretical foundation of the **Simplex Method**, which efficiently enumerates extreme points rather than searching the entire feasible region.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of an LPP

### 2.1.1 Decision Variables ($x_j$)

- Represent the **quantities to be decided** (number of products, hours of operation, units allocated).
- Must be **non-negative** in physical problems.
- Can be **integer, continuous, or binary** in extensions (ILP, BLP).

### 2.1.2 Objective Function ($Z$)

- A single linear expression in decision variables.
- Either **Maximized** (profit, revenue, output) or **Minimized** (cost, time, wastage).
- The vector $C = (c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n)$ is called the **cost/profit vector**.

### 2.1.3 Constraints

- Represent **resource limitations, demands, or technological requirements**.
- Each constraint defines a **half-space** in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- The matrix $A = [a_{ij}]_{m \times n}$ is the **technology/consumption matrix**.
- The vector $b = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_m)$ is the **RHS (resource) vector**.

### 2.1.4 Matrix Form of an LPP

The compact **matrix representation** of an LPP is:

$$
\text{Optimize } Z = C^T X
$$

$$
\text{Subject to: } AX \;\; (\leq, =, \geq) \;\; b
$$

$$
X \geq 0
$$

Where:

- $X$ is the $n \times 1$ **column vector of decision variables**.
- $C$ is the $n \times 1$ **column vector of objective coefficients**.
- $A$ is the $m \times n$ **constraint coefficient matrix**.
- $b$ is the $m \times 1$ **RHS vector**.

---

## 2.2 Standard Form Conversion Rules (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Original Form | Conversion Technique |
|---------------|----------------------|
| Minimization: $\min Z$ | Replace with $\max(-Z)$ or take $-Z$ as new objective |
| Constraint $\geq$ | Subtract **Surplus Variable** $s_i \geq 0$ |
| Constraint $\leq$ | Add **Slack Variable** $s_i \geq 0$ |
| Constraint $=$ | Add **Artificial Variable** $R_i \geq 0$ |
| $x_j$ unrestricted | Substitute $x_j = x_j' - x_j''$ with $x_j', x_j'' \geq 0$ |
| RHS $b_i < 0$ | Multiply the entire constraint by $-1$ and flip the inequality |

---

## 2.3 Convex Sets — Properties & Theorems

### 2.3.1 Fundamental Properties of Convex Sets

1. **Intersection of any collection of convex sets is convex.**
2. **The empty set and any single point are convex sets** (trivially).
3. **A closed line segment** joining two points of a convex set lies entirely within it.
4. **The half-space** $H = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : a^T x \leq b\}$ is convex for any $a, b$.
5. **The hyperplane** $H = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : a^T x = b\}$ is convex.
6. **The feasible region of an LPP is the intersection of finitely many half-spaces**, hence convex.

### 2.3.2 Convex Combination

A point $z$ is called a **convex combination** of $k$ points $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k$ if:

$$
z = \lambda_1 x_1 + \lambda_2 x_2 + \dots + \lambda_k x_k
$$

with $\lambda_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i = 1$.

> [!TIP]
> A set $S$ is convex **if and only if** it contains all convex combinations of any finite number of its points.

### 2.3.3 Extreme Points (Vertices)

An **extreme point** of a convex set $S$ is a point $x \in S$ that **cannot be expressed as a convex combination of two distinct points** of $S$.

- Every bounded convex polyhedron has at least one extreme point.
- For an LPP, if an optimal solution exists, an **extreme-point optimal solution** always exists.

---

## 2.4 Convex Functions — Key Properties

### 2.4.1 First-Order Characterization (Differentiability Required)

A differentiable function $f$ is convex on $S$ if and only if:

$$
f(y) \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y - x) \quad \forall x, y \in S
$$

This means: the **tangent line at any point lies below the function** globally.

### 2.4.2 Second-Order Characterization (Twice-Differentiable)

A twice-differentiable function $f$ is convex on $S$ if and only if the **Hessian matrix** is **Positive Semi-Definite** on $S$:

$$
H(x) = \nabla^2 f(x) \quad \text{is PSD} \quad \forall x \in S
$$

For single-variable $f(x)$: $f''(x) \geq 0 \;\; \forall x \in S$.

### 2.4.3 Why Linear Functions are the "Edge" of Convexity

- $f(x) = a^T x + b$ has $H = 0$ (zero matrix), which is trivially PSD.
- Therefore, linear functions are simultaneously the **simplest convex** and the **simplest concave** functions.
- This is why LPPs are the **easiest convex optimization** problems to solve.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is your KTU Board Exam quick-reference. Memorize and reproduce in rough work before solving.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Remarks |
|---------|----------------------|---------|
| **General LPP** | $\max/\min Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | Linear in $x_j$ |
| **Constraints** | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \leq, =, \geq b_i$ | $i = 1, 2, \dots, m$ |
| **Non-negativity** | $x_j \geq 0 \;\; \forall j$ | $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$ |
| **Convex Set Condition** | $\lambda x + (1-\lambda) y \in S$ for $x, y \in S$, $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | Definition |
| **Convex Function Condition** | $f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) y) \leq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$ | Definition |
| **Convex Function (Gradient)** | $f(y) \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y-x)$ | First-order characterization |
| **Convex Function (Hessian)** | $\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq 0$ (PSD) | Second-order characterization |
| **Slack Variable** | $a^T x + s = b$, $s \geq 0$ | For $\leq$ constraints |
| **Surplus Variable** | $a^T x - s = b$, $s \geq 0$ | For $\geq$ constraints |
| **Artificial Variable** | $a^T x + R = b$, $R \geq 0$ | For $=$ or for Big-M / Two-Phase start |
| **Matrix LPP** | $\max C^T X$ s.t. $AX \leq b$, $X \geq 0$ | Standard form |
| **Convex Combination** | $z = \sum \lambda_i x_i$, $\lambda_i \geq 0$, $\sum \lambda_i = 1$ | $k$ points |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

LPP + Convexity is the silent engine behind:

- **Production Planning** in manufacturing (Kanban, MRP systems).
- **Transportation & Logistics** (minimum cost shipping routes).
- **Network Flow** (telecommunications, electrical grids, water distribution).
- **Portfolio Optimization** in finance (Markowitz model).
- **Diet Problem & Blending** in agriculture and food processing.
- **Structural Design** (limit-state analysis in civil engineering).

> [!NOTE]
> The convexity guarantee makes LPPs **polynomial-time solvable** (via interior-point methods) and **industrial-scale tractable**, unlike general nonlinear programming which can be NP-hard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Convexity of an LPP Feasible Region

**Claim:** The feasible region of any LPP is a **convex set**.

### Step 1 — Set up the formal statement

Let $S$ be the feasible region of an LPP, defined as:

$$
S = \left\{ X \in \mathbb{R}^n : A X \leq b, \; X \geq 0 \right\}
$$

We must show: $\forall \; X_1, X_2 \in S$ and $\forall \; \lambda \in [0, 1]$, the point $X = \lambda X_1 + (1 - \lambda) X_2 \in S$.

### Step 2 — Verify the constraint satisfaction

Assume $X_1, X_2 \in S$. Then by definition:

$$
A X_1 \leq b \quad \text{and} \quad A X_2 \leq b
$$

Now consider $X = \lambda X_1 + (1-\lambda) X_2$. Apply the constraint matrix:

$$
A X = A \left( \lambda X_1 + (1-\lambda) X_2 \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Factor out using linearity of $A$

Since $A$ is a matrix, $A(\alpha U + \beta V) = \alpha A U + \beta A V$ (linearity of matrix multiplication):

$$
A X = \lambda A X_1 + (1 - \lambda) A X_2
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the assumption $A X_1 \leq b$ and $A X_2 \leq b$

Substituting the inequalities:

$$
A X = \lambda A X_1 + (1 - \lambda) A X_2
$$

$$
\leq \lambda b + (1 - \lambda) b
$$

### Step 5 — Combine RHS using $\lambda + (1-\lambda) = 1$

$$
A X \leq \left[ \lambda + (1 - \lambda) \right] b = 1 \cdot b = b
$$

Hence $A X \leq b$. ✓

### Step 6 — Verify non-negativity

Since $X_1 \geq 0$ and $X_2 \geq 0$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$
X = \lambda X_1 + (1 - \lambda) X_2 \geq 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 7 — Conclusion

Both constraints and non-negativity are satisfied, so $X \in S$. Therefore, $S$ is convex. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Why the Optimum of an LPP Lies at an Extreme Point

**Theorem (Fundamental Theorem of LPP):** If an LPP has an optimal solution, then there exists an **extreme point (basic feasible solution)** of the feasible region that is optimal.

### Step 1 — Setup

Let $Z_{\max} = C^T X^*$ be the optimal objective value, and let $K$ be the set of all optimal solutions:

$$
K = \{ X \in S : C^T X = Z_{\max} \}
$$

### Step 2 — Show $K$ is convex

For any $X^{(1)}, X^{(2)} \in K$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$
C^T \left[ \lambda X^{(1)} + (1-\lambda) X^{(2)} \right] = \lambda C^T X^{(1)} + (1-\lambda) C^T X^{(2)} = \lambda Z_{\max} + (1-\lambda) Z_{\max} = Z_{\max}
$$

So $K$ is convex (a face of the feasible polyhedron).

### Step 3 — Pick any $X^{(0)} \in K$ that is **not** an extreme point

By definition of non-extreme point:

$$
X^{(0)} = \lambda X^{(1)} + (1-\lambda) X^{(2)} \quad \text{with} \quad 0 < \lambda < 1
$$

for some $X^{(1)}, X^{(2)} \in K$, $X^{(1)} \neq X^{(2)}$.

### Step 4 — Apply Carathéodory's Theorem

Since $X^{(0)}$ lies in a convex polytope in $\mathbb{R}^n$, it can be expressed as a convex combination of **at most $n+1$ extreme points** of $K$. This is Carathéodory's theorem.

### Step 5 — Show that at least one extreme point of the convex hull is in $K$

By convexity of $K$ and linearity of $C^T X$, the **maximum** of $C^T X$ over $K$ is achieved at an **extreme point** of $K$. But the extreme points of $K$ are a subset of the extreme points of $S$. Therefore, an extreme point of $S$ lies in $K$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Consequence:** The Simplex Method can restrict its search to **basic feasible solutions** (extreme points), which are at most $\binom{m+n}{m}$ in number for a problem with $m$ constraints and $n$ variables. This dramatically reduces the search space.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Formulating and Converting an LPP

**Problem:** A small manufacturing unit produces two products $A$ and $B$. Each unit of $A$ requires 2 hours of machining and 1 hour of finishing, yielding a profit of ₹4. Each unit of $B$ requires 1 hour of machining and 2 hours of finishing, yielding a profit of ₹5. The available machining time is 80 hours, finishing time is 60 hours. Formulate as an LPP and convert to standard form.

### Step 1 — Identify decision variables

Let:
- $x_1$ = number of units of $A$ produced
- $x_2$ = number of units of $B$ produced

### Step 2 — Formulate the objective function

Total profit: $Z = 4x_1 + 5x_2$. We want to **maximize** this:

$$
\max Z = 4x_1 + 5x_2
$$

### Step 3 — Formulate the constraints

- **Machining:** $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 80$
- **Finishing:** $x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 60$
- **Non-negativity:** $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

### Step 4 — Convert to standard form (all $\leq$ and all variables $\geq 0$)

Introduce slack variables $s_1$ (machining) and $s_2$ (finishing):

$$
\max Z = 4x_1 + 5x_2 + 0 \cdot s_1 + 0 \cdot s_2
$$

$$
2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 80
$$

$$
x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 = 60
$$

$$
x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 \geq 0
$$

### Step 5 — Verify this is in standard form

- Objective: ✓ Maximization
- All constraints: ✓ Equality
- RHS: 80, 60 both $\geq 0$ ✓
- All variables: $x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 \geq 0$ ✓

The LPP is now in canonical **Simplex-ready** form.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Checking Convexity of a Function

**Problem:** Check whether $f(x) = 2x^2 - 3x + 5$ is convex on $\mathbb{R}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the first derivative

$$
f'(x) = 4x - 3
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the second derivative

$$
f''(x) = 4
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the second-order test

Since $f''(x) = 4 > 0$ for **all** $x \in \mathbb{R}$, the function is **strictly convex** on $\mathbb{R}$. ✓

> [!TIP]
> Note that this is a **quadratic** function. Any quadratic $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ is convex on $\mathbb{R}$ if and only if $a > 0$, and concave iff $a < 0$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — LPP Special Cases

| Case | Feasible Region | Optimal Solution | Graphical Appearance |
|------|----------------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Unique Optimum** | Bounded convex polygon | Single vertex | Objective line touches polygon at exactly one corner |
| **Multiple Optima (Alt. Opt.)** | Bounded convex polygon | Edge / face | Objective line coincides with one of the edges |
| **Unbounded Optimum** | Unbounded region | Ray / direction | Objective function can improve to infinity |
| **Infeasible** | Empty set ($\emptyset$) | No solution | Constraints form an empty intersection |
| **Redundant Constraint** | Same as without constraint | Same optimum | One constraint never active at optimum |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — LPP Solution Methodology Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Problem] --> B[Identify Decision Variables x1 x2 xn]
    B --> C[Formulate Objective Function Z cx]
    C --> D[Identify Constraints and RHS]
    D --> E[Formulate LPP in Words]
    E --> F[Convert to Standard Form]
    F --> G{Constraints are Linear?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Apply Simplex or Graphical Method]
    G -->|No| I[Use Non-Linear Programming Methods]
    H --> J[Check Feasibility of Solution]
    J -->|Feasible| K[Check Optimality]
    J -->|Infeasible| L[Revise Constraints or Add Slack Surplus]
    K -->|Optimal| M[Obtain Final Decision Variables and Z value]
    K -->|Not Optimal| N[Perform Simplex Pivot]
    N --> K
    L --> F
    I --> O[Use KKT or Penalty Methods]
```
