| **Feasible Region** $\mathcal{F}$ | The convex polygon (or polyhedron in higher dimensions) containing all points satisfying every constraint. |
| **Corner Point / Vertex** | The intersection of two (or more) binding constraints, where the feasible region changes direction. |
| **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** | Algebraic equivalent of a corner point — obtained by setting $(n-m)$ variables to zero and solving the remaining $m$ equations, then checking non-negativity. |
| **Objective Function Value** | The numerical value $Z = c^T x$ computed at a particular feasible point. |
| **Binding (Active) Constraint** | A constraint that holds with equality ($=$) at a given feasible point. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Solutions in a Linear Programming Problem

The classification of solutions is a **high-frequency KTU board question**. Master every category:

### A. Basic Solution (BS)
For an LPP with **$m$ equations** and **$n$ decision variables** (where $n > m$), a basic solution is obtained by:
1. Setting $(n - m)$ variables to zero — these are called **non-basic variables**.
2. Solving the resulting $m$ equations for the remaining $m$ variables — these are **basic variables**.

The number of basic solutions is bounded above by $\binom{n}{m}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A basic solution may or may not satisfy $x \geq 0$. Hence, it may or may not be feasible.

### B. Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)
A basic solution in which **all** basic variables are non-negative ($x_B \geq 0$). BFS corresponds geometrically to a **corner point** of the feasible region.

- **Non-degenerate BFS**: All $m$ basic variables are strictly positive ($x_B > 0$). The corresponding corner point lies at the intersection of exactly $m$ binding constraints.
- **Degenerate BFS**: At least one basic variable equals zero. The corresponding corner point has more than $m$ constraints passing through it (redundancy).

### C. Optimal Solution
A BFS that gives the best (maximum or minimum) value of the objective function.

- **Unique Optimal Solution**: Exactly one BFS attains the optimal value.
- **Alternative (Multiple) Optimal Solutions**: Two or more BFS attain the same optimal $Z$ value, and consequently the entire edge (or face) joining them is also optimal.

### D. Unbounded Solution
The objective function can be improved indefinitely (towards $+\infty$ for maximization, $-\infty$ for minimization) within the feasible region. The feasible region extends infinitely in the direction of improvement.

### E. Infeasible Solution / Infeasible LPP
No point exists that satisfies all constraints simultaneously. The feasible region is empty ($\mathcal{F} = \emptyset$).

## 2.2 The Corner Point Method — Stepwise Logic

The Corner Point Method is the **direct graphical algorithm** expected in KTU Module 1 ESE papers:

1. **Formulate** the LPP mathematically (define variables, objective, constraints).
2. **Plot** each constraint line on the $x_1$–$x_2$ plane, ignoring inequality at first.
3. **Identify the feasible half-plane** for each constraint (test origin $(0,0)$).
4. **Shade the common overlap** — this is the feasible region $\mathcal{F}$.
5. **Enumerate all corner points** of $\mathcal{F}$ (including the origin if applicable).
6. **Evaluate the objective function** $Z$ at every corner point.
7. **Select the optimum**: the corner point giving the largest $Z$ (max problem) or smallest $Z$ (min problem).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Standard LPP Form | Maximize $Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | Subject to $\sum a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i$, $x_j \geq 0$ |
| Number of BFS (upper bound) | $\binom{n}{m} = \dfrac{n!}{m!(n-m)!}$ | For $m$ equations, $n$ variables |
| Degeneracy Condition | At least one $x_B = 0$ in the BFS | More binding constraints than $m$ |
| Optimality (max) | $c^T x^* \geq c^T x \quad \forall x \in \mathcal{F}$ | $x^*$ is optimal point |
| Optimality (min) | $c^T x^* \leq c^T x \quad \forall x \in \mathcal{F}$ | $x^*$ is optimal point |
| Unboundedness | $\mathcal{F}$ is unbounded in direction of $\nabla c$ | No finite optimum exists |
| Infeasibility | $\mathcal{F} = \emptyset$ | No solution exists |
| Alternative Optima | $\nabla Z \parallel$ binding constraint edge | Two BFS share same $Z^*$ value |
| Iso-Profit / Iso-Cost Line | $c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 = k$ | Lines of constant objective value |
| Slope of Objective | $m_Z = -c_1 / c_2$ | Slope of iso-profit line |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of optimal solutions is the operational backbone of:

- **Production Planning** — allocating limited machine-hours to maximize profit.
- **Transportation & Logistics** — minimum cost shipping routes.
- **Diet & Nutrition Problems** — cheapest meal plan meeting nutrient constraints.
- **Network Flow & Telecommunications** — maximum data flow across bandwidth limits.
- **Civil Engineering Mix Design** — minimum cost concrete meeting strength constraints.

Every modern **decision-support system (DSS)** and **ERP optimizer** (SAP, Oracle) ultimately reduces to solving LPPs to find optimal solutions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Corner Point Method (Max Problem)

**LPP Statement:**
A small engineering workshop manufactures two products $P_1$ and $P_2$. Each unit of $P_1$ requires **2 hours** of machining and yields a profit of ₹**4**. Each unit of $P_2$ requires **1 hour** of machining and yields a profit of ₹**3**. The available machining time is **1000 hours per week**. Due to a subcontracting limit, total production (in units) cannot exceed **800 units per week**. Maximum demand for $P_1$ is **400 units** and for $P_2$ is **700 units** per week. Formulate and solve to find the profit-maximizing production plan.

### Step 1 — Mathematical Formulation

Let $x_1$ = units of $P_1$ produced per week, $x_2$ = units of $P_2$ produced per week.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } Z &= 4x_1 + 3x_2 \\
\text{Subject to: } \\
2x_1 + x_2 &\leq 1000 \quad \text{(Machining hours)} \\
x_1 + x_2 &\leq 800 \quad \text{(Total production)} \\
x_1 &\leq 400 \quad \text{(Demand of } P_1 \text{)} \\
x_2 &\leq 700 \quad \text{(Demand of } P_2 \text{)} \\
x_1, x_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Feasible Region

The feasible region is a closed convex polygon bounded by the four constraints and the two axes.

### Step 3 — Enumerate Corner Points

| Corner Point | Origin of Intersection | Feasible? | $Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A = (0, 0)$ | Origin | Yes | $0$ |
| $B = (400, 0)$ | $x_1 = 400$ and $x_2 = 0$ | Yes | $4(400) = 1600$ |
| $C = (400, 200)$ | $x_1 = 400$ and $2x_1 + x_2 = 1000$ | Check: $400 + 200 = 600 \leq 800$ ✓ | $4(400) + 3(200) = 2200$ |
| $D = (200, 600)$ | $2x_1 + x_2 = 1000$ and $x_1 + x_2 = 800$ | Yes | $4(200) + 3(600) = 2600$ |
| $E = (100, 700)$ | $x_1 + x_2 = 800$ and $x_2 = 700$ | Check: $2(100)+700 = 900 \leq 1000$ ✓ | $4(100) + 3(700) = 2500$ |
| $F = (0, 700)$ | $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 = 700$ | Yes | $3(700) = 2100$ |

### Step 4 — Algebraic Derivation of Corner $D$ (200, 600)

This is the most important derivation — KTU examiners reward students who show the system-solving step explicitly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 &= 1000 \quad \text{--- (Constraint 1)} \\
x_1 + x_2 &= 800 \quad \text{--- (Constraint 2)} \\
\hline
\text{Subtracting (2) from (1):} \quad x_1 &= 200 \\
\text{Back-substituting into (2):} \quad 200 + x_2 &= 800 \\
\Rightarrow x_2 &= 600
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence corner $D = (200, 600)$.

### Step 5 — Compare Objective Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_A &= 0 \\
Z_B &= 1600 \\
Z_C &= 2200 \\
Z_D &= 2600 \quad \longleftarrow \text{MAXIMUM} \\
Z_E &= 2500 \\
Z_F &= 2100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Optimal Solution

$$
\boxed{x_1^* = 200 \text{ units of } P_1, \quad x_2^* = 600 \text{ units of } P_2, \quad Z_{\max} = \text{₹ } 2600}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Recognizing the Type of Solution

| Graphical Observation | Algebraic Indicator | Type of Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Closed bounded polygon, unique best corner | One BFS gives the extremum | **Unique Optimal Solution** |
| Closed polygon, entire edge has same $Z$ | $\nabla Z$ is parallel to a binding constraint | **Alternative Optimal Solutions** |
| Open feasible region, $Z$ keeps growing | $\mathcal{F}$ is unbounded in direction of $\nabla c$ | **Unbounded Solution** |
| Empty feasible region | No point satisfies all constraints | **Infeasible LPP** |
| One basic variable = 0 at a corner | More than $m$ binding constraints at a BFS | **Degenerate BFS** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **slope test for alternative optima** is critical. The slope of the objective function is $m_Z = -c_1 / c_2$. If this equals the slope of any binding constraint, the iso-profit line is parallel to that constraint edge, and the entire edge is optimal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of LPP Solutions — Concept Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    LPPSol[LPP Solution Space] --> BS[Basic Solution]
    LPPSol --> BFS[Basic Feasible Solution BFS]
    LPPSol --> INF[Infeasible LPP]
    LPPSol --> UNB[Unbounded Solution]

    BS --> BSneg[Negative Components]
    BS --> BSpos[Non-Negative = BFS]

    BFS --> NDG[Non-Degenerate BFS]
    BFS --> DG[Degenerate BFS]
    BFS --> OPT[Optimal BFS]

    OPT --> UNI[Unique Optimum]
    OPT --> ALT[Alternative Optima]

    INF --> INFdesc[Feasible Region Empty]
    UNB --> UNBdesc[Feasible Region Unbounded in Direction of Improvement]

    style LPPSol fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style BFS fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style OPT fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style UNI fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style ALT fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style INF fill:#4a148c,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style UNB fill:#4a148c,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Corner Point Method — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Formulate LPP Mathematically] --> S2[Step 2: Plot All Constraint Lines]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify Feasible Half Planes]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Shade the Feasible Region F]
    S4 --> S5{Is F non-empty?}
    S5 -- No --> STOP1[STOP - Infeasible LPP]
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 5: Enumerate All Corner Points]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Compute Z at Each Corner]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Select Max or Min Z]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: State Optimal x1 x2 and Z Value]

    style S1 fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style S5 fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style S8 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style S9 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style STOP1 fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Geometric View of Solution Types

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Type 1: Unique Optimum] --> A1[Closed Bounded Polygon]
    A1 --> A2[One Corner Maximizes Z]

    B[Type 2: Alternative Optima] --> B1[Closed Bounded Polygon]
    B1 --> B2[Iso Profit Line Parallel to Edge]

    C[Type 3: Unbounded] --> C1[Feasible Region Open]
    C1 --> C2[Z Grows Without Bound]

    D[Type 4: Infeasible] --> D1[No Common Overlap]
    D1 --> D2[Empty Feasible Region]

    style A fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define an optimal solution of a Linear Programming Problem. State the Fundamental Theorem of LPP (Corner Point Theorem).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** An *optimal solution* of an LPP is a feasible solution that yields the best (maximum or minimum) value of the objective function among **all** feasible solutions.
- **(1 mark)** *Fundamental Theorem of LPP:* If an optimal solution exists for an LPP with a bounded non-empty feasible region, then at least one optimal solution occurs at a **corner point (extreme point / vertex)** of the convex feasible region.
- **(1 mark)** *Corollary:* If the LPP has a unique optimal solution, it must occur at a unique corner point. If multiple corners yield the same optimal value, the entire edge joining them is optimal (alternative optima).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between a Basic Feasible Solution (BFS) and an Optimal Solution. What is a degenerate basic feasible solution?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** is a basic solution of the system of equations that satisfies the non-negativity restrictions. It corresponds geometrically to a corner point of the feasible region.
- **(1 mark)** An **Optimal Solution** is that particular BFS (or set of BFS) which gives the most favourable value of the objective function $Z$.
- **(1 mark)** A **degenerate BFS** is one in which at least one basic variable is equal to zero. Geometrically, this means more than $m$ constraints pass through the same corner point (redundancy exists in the constraints).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Solve the following LPP graphically using the corner point method:
$$\text{Maximize } Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to: } 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 1000, \quad x_1 + x_2 \leq 800, \quad x_1 \leq 400, \quad x_2 \leq 700, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$ **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **(1 mark)** Plot all four constraint lines.
- **(2 marks)** Identify the feasible region as a closed convex hexagon.
- **(2 marks)** Enumerate corner points: $A(0,0)$, $B(400,0)$, $C(400,200)$, $D(200,600)$, $E(100,700)$, $F(0,700)$.
- **(2 marks)** Compute objective values:

| Corner | $Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$ |
|---|---|
| $A(0,0)$ | $0$ |
| $B(400,0)$ | $1600$ |
| $C(400,200)$ | $2200$ |
| $D(200,600)$ | $\mathbf{2600}$ |
| $E(100,700)$ | $2500$ |
| $F(0,700)$ | $2100$ |

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1^* = 200$, $x_2^* = 600$, $Z_{\max} = 2600$.

---

**(b)** Discuss the different types of solutions that can arise in a Linear Programming Problem. Illustrate each with a clear explanation. **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **(1 mark)** **Basic Solution:** Solution obtained by setting $(n-m)$ variables to zero and solving the $m$ equations; may or may not satisfy $x \geq 0$.
- **(1 mark)** **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS):** Basic solution satisfying $x \geq 0$. Corresponds to a corner point.
- **(1 mark)** **Degenerate BFS:** A BFS in which at least one basic variable is zero.
- **(1 mark)** **Non-degenerate BFS:** All $m$ basic variables are strictly positive.
- **(1 mark)** **Optimal Solution:** BFS that optimizes the objective function.
- **(1 mark)** **Alternative Optima:** When two or more BFS yield the same optimal $Z$ value.
- **(1 mark)** **Unbounded Solution / Infeasible LPP:** Unbounded = feasible region extends infinitely in the direction of improvement. Infeasible = no feasible point exists at all.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Solve the following LPP graphically and identify the type of solution:
$$\text{Minimize } Z = 2x_1 + 3x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to: } x_1 + x_2 \geq 8, \quad 2x_1 + x_2 \geq 10, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$ **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **(1 mark)** Identify feasible half-planes (above both lines, first quadrant).
- **(2 marks)** Corner points of the unbounded feasible region: $A(0, 10)$, $B(2, 6)$, $C(8, 0)$.
- **(2 marks)** Compute $Z$:

| Corner | $Z = 2x_1 + 3x_2$ |
|---|---|
| $A(0,10)$ | $30$ |
| $B(2,6)$ | $\mathbf{18}$ |
| $C(8,0)$ | $16$ ← MIN |

- **(1 mark)** Re-checking: at $C(8,0)$: $2(8)+3(0)=16$. The minimum is at $C(8,0)$ with $Z_{\min} = 16$.
- **(1 mark)** Note: Although the feasible region is unbounded, the objective function has a **finite minimum** — the LPP is well-posed.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1^* = 8$, $x_2^* = 0$, $Z_{\min} = 16$.

---

**(b)** State and explain the Corner Point Method for solving an LPP. Why does the optimal solution always occur at a corner point? **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **(2 marks)** **Statement of Corner Point Method:** The feasible region of an LPP is a convex polyhedron. To find the optimum, evaluate the objective function at every corner point and select the one giving the best value.
- **(2 marks)** **Stepwise Procedure:** (i) Formulate, (ii) Plot constraints, (iii) Identify feasible region, (iv) List all corner points, (v) Compute $Z$ at each, (vi) Select optimum.
- **(3 marks)** **Reason why optimum lies at a corner (Geometric Intuition):** Iso-profit lines (lines of constant $Z$) are parallel straight lines. As we translate the iso-profit line in the improving direction (perpendicular to the gradient $\nabla c$), the last point of contact with the convex feasible region is always a vertex. Any interior point can be expressed as a convex combination of two corner points, and by linearity the average of two $Z$ values lies between them, so a corner must beat (or tie) the interior.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to evaluate $Z$ at the origin** — even $(0,0)$ is a corner point; omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Not verifying feasibility** of intersection points — every candidate corner must satisfy ALL constraints, not just the two used to find it. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if feasibility is not checked.
> 3. **Confusing degeneracy with infeasibility** — a degenerate BFS is still feasible; only the basic variable values have a zero.
> 4. **Stating "unbounded = no solution"** — incorrect! An unbounded feasible region CAN still yield a finite optimum (e.g., minimization with the optimum in the correct direction). Only when the LPP is a *maximization* with the region open in the gradient direction is it truly unbounded.
> 5. **Skipping the algebraic solving step** — KTU expects the simultaneous-equation solving for corner coordinates to be shown explicitly. A diagram alone is NOT sufficient for full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Optimal Solution** = best feasible solution (max or min) of the objective function.
- **Corner Point Theorem (Fundamental Theorem of LPP):** The optimum of an LPP, if it exists, lies at a **corner point** of the feasible region.
- **Corner Point Method** is the **graphical** algorithm: enumerate corners → evaluate $Z$ → pick best.
- **Five Types of LPP Solutions:** Basic Solution, BFS, Optimal BFS, Unbounded, Infeasible.
- **Degenerate BFS** ⇔ at least one basic variable = 0 (redundancy in constraints).
- **Alternative Optima** ⇔ iso-profit line is parallel to a binding constraint edge.
- **Unbounded LPP** ⇔ objective improves indefinitely in an unbounded feasible region.
- **Infeasible LPP** ⇔ feasible region is empty — NO solution exists.
- **Slope test for alternative optima:** $m_Z = -c_1 / c_2$ equals the slope of some binding constraint.
- **Number of candidate BFS** for an $m \times n$ LPP is at most $\binom{n}{m}$.
- **Always verify feasibility** of every candidate corner against ALL constraints.
- **Always evaluate $Z$ at the origin** $(0,0)$ — it is a valid corner of the first-quadrant region.
- **Linearity is critical** — both objective and constraints must be linear; otherwise the corner-point theorem fails.
- **Convexity of the feasible region** is guaranteed because LPPs involve linear inequalities — every BFS is a vertex of a convex polyhedron.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Simplex Method – The Simplex Method in Tabular Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Simplex Method – The Simplex Method in Tabular Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Simplex Method in Tabular Form** (also called the **Simplex Tableau Method**) is an organized, matrix-based computational procedure for solving Linear Programming Problems (LPP) developed by **George Bernard Dantzig in 1947**. It systematically arranges all coefficients of the objective function, constraints, and slack/surplus variables into a single rectangular array called the **Simplex Tableau**, allowing for iterative algebraic elimination (Gauss-Jordan row operations) to move from one Basic Feasible Solution (BFS) to an adjacent BFS until optimality is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The tabular form converts the entire simplex problem into a structured matrix where each iteration corresponds to a pivot operation. This eliminates the need to rewrite equations and dramatically reduces computational errors — a frequent valuation point in KTU End Semester Examinations.

Mathematically, for a standard LPP:
$$\text{Maximize } Z = c^T x$$
$$\text{Subject to: } Ax = b, \quad x \geq 0$$

The tabular form consolidates the augmented matrix $[A \mid b]$, the cost coefficients $c^T$, and the reduced costs $c_j - z_j$ into one tableau for repeated pivoting.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are climbing a mountain in dense fog, and you cannot see the summit. However, you have a compass that tells you:
- The current altitude (your objective value, $Z$)
- The slope in every direction (the reduced costs, $c_j - z_j$)
- All possible stepping stones (the basic feasible solutions)

The **simplex tableau** is essentially your "altitude map." At each tableau, you:
1. Check if any direction goes **uphill** (a positive $c_j - z_j$ for maximization).
2. If no uphill direction exists, you are at the **summit** (optimal solution).
3. Otherwise, you take a step in the steepest uphill direction (most positive $c_j - z_j$) — this is your **entering variable**.
4. You decide how far you can step without falling off a cliff (the **minimum ratio test** determines the **leaving variable**).
5. You pivot, and the map updates with a new "you are here" location.

> [!NOTE]
> The tabular form is simply a **compact bookkeeping device** that automates Gauss-Jordan elimination, ensuring that one basic variable is expressed in terms of non-basic variables in each row, and that the basis matrix is always the identity matrix.

## 1.3 Standard Tableau Components

A canonical simplex tableau contains the following elements:

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $C_B$ | Cost coefficients of current basic variables |
| $X_B$ | Current values of basic variables |
| $c_j - z_j$ (or $c_j - \bar{c}_j$) | Reduced costs (net evaluation row) |
| $b$ | Right-hand side constants |
| $a_{ij}$ | Constraint coefficients |
| $\theta$ | Ratio column for minimum ratio test |

## 1.4 Key Terminology in KTU Context

- **Basic Variable (BV):** A variable currently in the solution, coefficient column = unit vector.
- **Non-Basic Variable (NBV):** A variable set to **zero** in the current iteration.
- **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS):** A solution where all $X_B \geq 0$.
- **Optimal Solution:** A BFS where all $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ (maximization).
- **Unbounded Solution:** When at least one $c_j - z_j > 0$ but all corresponding $a_{ij} \leq 0$.
- **Infeasible Solution:** When any $X_B < 0$ and no artificial variable resolution applies.
- **Degenerate Solution:** A BFS where one or more basic variables equal **zero**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind the Tabular Form

The simplex method's tabular representation is built on **three foundational pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Standard Form Conversion
Every LPP must be converted to standard form before tabulation:
- All constraints become **equalities** by adding **slack variables** ($s_i \geq 0$) for "$\leq$" constraints, or **surplus variables** ($-s_i$) plus **artificial variables** ($a_i \geq 0$) for "$\geq$" and "$=$" constraints.
- For minimization, the objective is multiplied by $-1$ to convert to maximization: $\min Z = -\max(-Z)$.
- All variables must satisfy **non-negativity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners award **2 marks** for correctly converting the problem to standard form. Missing slack variables or forgetting to flip minimization signs are the most common deductions.

### Pillar 2: The Starting Tableau
The initial tableau is built directly from the standard form:

- The first column lists the basis variables $X_B$.
- The next columns contain the coefficients of decision variables $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.
- The slack variables form the **initial identity matrix** (basis).
- The rightmost column is the **RHS** (values of $X_B$).
- The bottom row contains the objective function values:
  - For maximization: $Z_j = \sum c_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}$, then $c_j - Z_j$ is computed.
  - The optimality condition is $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$ for all $j$.

### Pillar 3: Iterative Pivoting
A single iteration involves:
1. **Selection of Entering Variable:** The column with the most positive $c_j - Z_j$ (for maximization) — called the **pivot column**.
2. **Selection of Leaving Variable:** Apply the **Minimum Ratio Rule**:
$$\theta = \min_{a_{ij} > 0}\left\{\frac{X_{B,i}}{a_{ij}}\right\}$$
   The row achieving this minimum is the **pivot row**, and its basic variable leaves the basis.
3. **Pivot Element:** The intersection $(a_{pq})$ where row $p$ meets column $q$.
4. **Gauss-Jordan Row Operations:**
   - New pivot row = Old pivot row / pivot element.
   - All other rows: $R_i^{\text{new}} = R_i^{\text{old}} - (a_{iq} \cdot R_p^{\text{new}})$.
5. **Update $C_B$ column** with the new basic variable's cost coefficient.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Net Evaluation | $c_j - Z_j = c_j - \sum_{i} c_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}$ | Tests optimality |
| Optimality (Max) | $c_j - Z_j \leq 0 \; \forall j$ | Stopping condition |
| Optimality (Min) | $c_j - Z_j \geq 0 \; \forall j$ | Stopping condition |
| Minimum Ratio | $\theta = \min\limits_{a_{ij}>0} \dfrac{X_{B,i}}{a_{ij}}$ | Selects leaving variable |
| New Pivot Row | $R_p^{\text{new}} = R_p^{\text{old}} / a_{pq}$ | Normalizes pivot row |
| New Other Rows | $R_i^{\text{new}} = R_i^{\text{old}} - a_{iq} \cdot R_p^{\text{new}}$ | Eliminates pivot column |
| Objective Value | $Z = \sum c_{B,i} \cdot X_{B,i}$ | Computes current $Z$ |
| Slack Variable Sum | $\sum s_i = \sum b_i - \sum (\text{used resources})$ | Surplus identification |
| Big-M Penalty | $M$ (very large positive) | Penalizes artificial variables |
| Two-Phase Phase-I | $\min W = \sum a_i$ | Eliminates artificials |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The simplex tableau is the **computational backbone** of nearly every modern optimization solver:
- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Optimal warehouse allocation and vehicle routing.
- **Production Planning:** Manufacturing mix decisions, batch sizing in chemical plants.
- **Telecommunications:** Bandwidth allocation in networks.
- **Energy Systems:** Optimal power dispatch in electrical grids (Unit Commitment).
- **Civil Engineering:** Project scheduling, resource leveling in construction.
- **Finance:** Portfolio optimization with linear constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> Software like **LINGO, CPLEX, Gurobi, and Excel Solver** all internally implement variations of the revised simplex method (a memory-optimized version of the tabular form).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (Maximize Type)

**Problem:**
$$\text{Maximize } Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to:}$$
$$x_1 \leq 4$$
$$2x_2 \leq 12$$
$$3x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 18$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

### Step 1: Standard Form Conversion
Add slack variables $s_1, s_2, s_3 \geq 0$:
$$x_1 + s_1 = 4$$
$$2x_2 + s_2 = 12$$
$$3x_1 + 2x_2 + s_3 = 18$$
$$Z - 3x_1 - 5x_2 = 0$$

### Step 2: Initial Simplex Tableau Construction

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS ($X_B$) | Ratio $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 4 | 4/1 = **4** |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 12 | 12/2 = 6 |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 18 | 18/2 = **9** |
| $Z_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $Z = 0$ | — |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | **3** | **5** | 0 | 0 | 0 | — | — |

**Logic:** Compute $Z_j = \sum c_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}$. Since all $c_{B,i} = 0$, all $Z_j = 0$. Hence $c_j - Z_j = c_j$.

### Step 3: Identify Entering Variable
The most positive $c_j - Z_j$ is **5** (column $x_2$). So **$x_2$ enters the basis**. This is the **pivot column**.

### Step 4: Apply Minimum Ratio Rule
Compute $\theta_i = X_{B,i} / a_{i,x_2}$ for rows where $a_{i,x_2} > 0$:
- Row 1 ($s_1$): $a = 0 \Rightarrow$ skipped.
- Row 2 ($s_2$): $\theta = 12/2 = 6$.
- Row 3 ($s_3$): $\theta = 18/2 = 9$.

Minimum is **6** in Row 2. So **$s_2$ leaves the basis**. **Pivot element = 2**.

### Step 5: Perform Row Operations

**New Row 2 (Pivot Row) = Old Row 2 / 2:**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1/2 | 0 | 6 |

**New Row 1 = Old Row 1 − (0 × New Row 2):** (no change)

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 4 |

**New Row 3 = Old Row 3 − (2 × New Row 2):**

$$R_3^{\text{new}} = [3, 2, 0, 0, 1, 18] - 2 \times [0, 1, 0, 1/2, 0, 6]$$

$$R_3^{\text{new}} = [3-0, \; 2-2, \; 0-0, \; 0-1, \; 1-0, \; 18-12] = [3, 0, 0, -1, 1, 6]$$

**New $Z_j$ Row:**
$$Z_j = \sum c_{B,i} \cdot a_{ij}$$

With $C_B = [0, 5, 0]$:

$$Z_{x_1} = (0)(1) + (5)(0) + (0)(3) = 0$$
$$Z_{x_2} = (0)(0) + (5)(1) + (0)(0) = 5$$
$$Z_{s_1} = (0)(1) + (5)(0) + (0)(0) = 0$$
$$Z_{s_2} = (0)(0) + (5)(1/2) + (0)(-1) = 5/2$$
$$Z_{s_3} = (0)(0) + (5)(0) + (0)(1) = 0$$

**Net Evaluations $c_j - Z_j$:**

$$c_{x_1} - Z_{x_1} = 3 - 0 = 3 \quad (\text{still positive, not optimal})$$
$$c_{x_2} - Z_{x_2} = 5 - 5 = 0 \quad (\text{alternate optimum possible})$$
$$c_{s_1} - Z_{s_1} = 0 - 0 = 0$$
$$c_{s_2} - Z_{s_2} = 0 - 5/2 = -5/2$$
$$c_{s_3} - Z_{s_3} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

### Step 6: Second Tableau (Iteration 2)

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | Ratio |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | 0 | **1** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 4 | 4/1 = **4** |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1/2 | 0 | 6 | — |
| $s_3$ | 0 | **3** | 0 | 0 | -1 | 1 | 6 | 6/3 = 2 |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | **3** | 0 | 0 | -5/2 | 0 | $Z = 30$ | — |

Entering variable: $x_1$ (most positive $c_j - Z_j = 3$).

Ratios: Row 1 → 4, Row 3 → 2. **Minimum = 2, Row 3, leaving $s_3$. Pivot = 3.**

### Step 7: Third Tableau (Iteration 3)

**New Row 3 = Old Row 3 / 3:**

$$R_3^{\text{new}} = [1, 0, 0, -1/3, 1/3, 2]$$

**New Row 1 = Old Row 1 − (1 × New Row 3):**

$$R_1^{\text{new}} = [1-1, \; 0, \; 1, \; 0+1/3, \; 0-1/3, \; 4-2] = [0, 0, 1, 1/3, -1/3, 2]$$

**New Row 2 = Old Row 2 − (0 × New Row 3):** (no change, since $a_{2,x_1} = 0$)

**New $C_B$:** $x_1$ enters with cost 3. New $C_B = [0, 5, 3]$.

**New $Z_j$:**
$$Z_{x_1} = 0(0) + 5(0) + 3(1) = 3$$
$$Z_{x_2} = 0(0) + 5(1) + 3(0) = 5$$
$$Z_{s_1} = 0(1) + 5(0) + 3(1/3) = 1$$
$$Z_{s_2} = 0(-1/3) + 5(1/2) + 3(-1/3) = 5/2 - 1 = 3/2$$
$$Z_{s_3} = 0(0) + 5(0) + 3(1/3) = 1$$

**New Net Evaluations $c_j - Z_j$:**
$$c_{x_1} - Z_{x_1} = 3 - 3 = 0$$
$$c_{x_2} - Z_{x_2} = 5 - 5 = 0$$
$$c_{s_1} - Z_{s_1} = 0 - 1 = -1 \quad (\text{negative, OK})$$
$$c_{s_2} - Z_{s_2} = 0 - 3/2 = -3/2 \quad (\text{negative, OK})$$
$$c_{s_3} - Z_{s_3} = 0 - 1 = -1 \quad (\text{negative, OK})$$

**All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0 \Rightarrow$ OPTIMAL!**

### Step 8: Read the Optimal Solution

| Basis | $C_B$ | $X_B$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | 0 | 2 |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 6 |
| $x_1$ | 3 | 2 |

$$x_1^* = 2, \quad x_2^* = 6, \quad s_1 = 2, \quad s_2 = 0, \quad s_3 = 0$$
$$Z_{\max} = 3(2) + 5(6) = 6 + 30 = 36$$

Since $s_1 = 2 > 0$, the first constraint is non-binding (slack remains), and the second and third constraints are binding.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Condition Detected | Conclusion | KTU Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$ (max) | Optimal solution reached | Stop and read solution |
| Some $c_j - Z_j > 0$, all $a_{ij} \leq 0$ for that column | Unbounded solution | Declare LPP is unbounded |
| All $c_j - Z_j \geq 0$ (min problem) | Optimal solution | Stop and read solution |
| $X_B < 0$ appears in tableau | Infeasibility | Apply Big-M or Two-Phase |
| Two or more equal minimum ratios | Degeneracy | Choose any; cycling possible |
| Zero in $c_j - Z_j$ for non-basic at optimum | Alternate optima | Find by pivoting on that column |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Simplex Tabular Method Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: LPP in Words"] --> B["Convert to Standard Form with Slacks"]
    B --> C["Build Initial Simplex Tableau"]
    C --> D["Compute cj minus Zj Row"]
    D --> E{"All cj minus Zj less than or equal to 0?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["STOP: Optimal Solution Found"]
    E -->|No| G["Pick Most Positive: Entering Variable"]
    G --> H["Compute Theta Ratios for Positive aij"]
    H --> I{"All aij less than or equal to 0?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["STOP: Unbounded Solution"]
    I -->|No| K["Min Ratio Rule: Leaving Variable"]
    K --> L["Identify Pivot Element at Intersection"]
    L --> M["Divide Pivot Row by Pivot Element"]
    M --> N["Eliminate Pivot Column in Other Rows"]
    N --> O["Update CB Column with New Basic Variable"]
    O --> D
    F --> P["Read Xb and Z values from Final Tableau"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Tableau Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TP["Simplex Tableau Structure"]
        direction TB
        B1["Column 1: Basis Xb Labels"]
        B2["Column 2: CB Cost Vector"]
        B3["Columns 3 to n+2: Decision and Slack Coefficients"]
        B4["Column n+3: RHS Xb Values"]
        B5["Column n+4: Theta Ratios"]
    end

    subgraph BR["Bottom Net Evaluation Row"]
        direction TB
        R1["Zj: Sum of CB times aij"]
        R2["cj minus Zj: Optimality Indicator"]
        R3["Z equals Sum of CB times Xb"]
    end

    TP --> BR
    BR --> DEC{"Optimality Test"}
    DEC -->|Pass| OPT["Optimal Solution"]
    DEC -->|Fail| PIV["Pivot to Next Tableau"]
    PIV --> TP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: Pivot Operation Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T0 as Initial Tableau
    participant PC as Pivot Column Selection
    participant PR as Pivot Row via Ratio Test
    participant PE as Pivot Element Identification
    participant GR as Gauss Jordan Row Updates
    participant T1 as Next Tableau

    T0->>PC: Compute cj minus Zj for all j
    PC->>PR: Choose most positive cj minus Zj
    PR->>PE: Compute theta equals Xb over aij
    PE->>GR: aij is the pivot element
    GR->>GR: New pivot row equals old divided by aij
    GR->>GR: New other row equals old minus aij times new pivot row
    GR->>T1: Form new tableau with updated CB
    T1->>T1: Recheck optimality condition
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Basic Feasible Solution" (BFS) as used in the Simplex Method. How does the Simplex Method in tabular form transition from one BFS to an adjacent BFS?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** is a solution of the system $Ax = b$ (with $A$ being an $m \times n$ matrix, $m < n$) obtained by setting $n - m$ variables (non-basic variables) to zero and solving for the remaining $m$ variables (basic variables), provided all basic variables are non-negative.

In the **tabular form**, the transition from one BFS to an adjacent BFS occurs through a **pivot operation**:
1. An entering variable is selected (column with most positive $c_j - Z_j$).
2. A leaving variable is selected (row with minimum positive ratio $\theta = X_B / a_{ij}$).
3. Gauss-Jordan row operations replace the leaving variable with the entering variable in the basis, producing a new tableau representing the adjacent BFS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse BFS with "basic solution." A basic solution may have negative values, but a BFS must satisfy $X_B \geq 0$. Failure to specify non-negativity costs **1 mark**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the optimality condition for a maximization LPP in the Simplex Tabular Method. What action is taken if the condition is satisfied?**

**Model Answer:**
The **optimality condition** for a maximization problem in the simplex tableau is:

$$c_j - Z_j \leq 0 \quad \text{for all } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

This means the net evaluation (or reduced cost) of every non-basic variable must be non-positive. In economic terms, increasing any non-basic variable from zero will not improve the objective function $Z$.

**Action when satisfied:** The current tableau represents the **optimal solution**. The values of basic variables $X_B$ are read directly, and the optimal objective value is:

$$Z^* = \sum_{i} c_{B,i} \cdot X_{B,i}$$

The algorithm **terminates**, and the solution is reported along with the slack variable values to identify binding/non-binding constraints.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** Solve the following LPP using the Simplex Method in Tabular Form:

$$\text{Maximize } Z = 4x_1 + 10x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to:}$$
$$2x_1 + x_2 \leq 50$$
$$2x_1 + 5x_2 \leq 100$$
$$2x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 90$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Standard Form and Initial Tableau

**Step 1: Standard Form**
Add slack variables $s_1, s_2, s_3 \geq 0$:
$$2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 50$$
$$2x_1 + 5x_2 + s_2 = 100$$
$$2x_1 + 3x_2 + s_3 = 90$$
$$Z - 4x_1 - 10x_2 = 0$$

**Step 2: Initial Tableau**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 50 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 2 | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 100 | **20** |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 2 | 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 90 | 30 |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | 4 | **10** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |

Entering: $x_2$ (most positive $c_j - Z_j = 10$). Leaving: $s_2$ ($\theta_{\min} = 20$). Pivot = 5.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Complete Iterations to Optimality

**Iteration 1 — New Pivot Row ($R_2 \div 5$):**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $x_2$ | 10 | 2/5 | 1 | 0 | 1/5 | 0 | 20 |

**New $R_1 = R_1 - (1 \cdot R_2^{\text{new}})$:**

$$R_1^{\text{new}} = [2-2/5, \; 0, \; 1, \; 0-1/5, \; 0, \; 50-20] = [8/5, 0, 1, -1/5, 0, 30]$$

**New $R_3 = R_3 - (3 \cdot R_2^{\text{new}})$:**

$$R_3^{\text{new}} = [2-6/5, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0-3/5, \; 1, \; 90-60] = [4/5, 0, 0, -3/5, 1, 30]$$

**New $Z_j$ row with $C_B = [0, 10, 0]$:**

$$Z_{x_1} = 0(8/5) + 10(2/5) + 0(4/5) = 4$$
$$Z_{x_2} = 0(0) + 10(1) + 0(0) = 10$$
$$Z_{s_1} = 0(1) + 10(0) + 0(0) = 0$$
$$Z_{s_2} = 0(-1/5) + 10(1/5) + 0(-3/5) = 2$$
$$Z_{s_3} = 0(0) + 10(0) + 0(1) = 0$$

**Net Evaluations:**

$$c_{x_1} - Z_{x_1} = 4 - 4 = 0$$
$$c_{x_2} - Z_{x_2} = 10 - 10 = 0$$
$$c_{s_1} - Z_{s_1} = 0 - 0 = 0$$
$$c_{s_2} - Z_{s_2} = 0 - 2 = -2 \quad (\text{negative, OK})$$
$$c_{s_3} - Z_{s_3} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$ ⇒ OPTIMAL.**

**Optimal Solution:**

$$x_1^* = 0, \quad x_2^* = 20, \quad s_1 = 30, \quad s_2 = 0, \quad s_3 = 30$$
$$Z_{\max} = 4(0) + 10(20) = \mathbf{200}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** A diet planner wants to minimize the cost of a meal. The meal must contain at least 8 units of Vitamin A and 10 units of Vitamin C. Two foods $F_1$ and $F_2$ are available. $F_1$ provides 2 units of Vitamin A and 1 unit of Vitamin C per kg at a cost of ₹5. $F_2$ provides 1 unit of Vitamin A and 2 units of Vitamin C per kg at a cost of ₹10. Formulate as an LPP and solve using the Simplex Method in Tabular Form. Identify any special cases encountered.

### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Formulation and Standard Form

**Formulation:**

Let $x_1, x_2$ = kg of $F_1$ and $F_2$ respectively.

$$\text{Minimize } Z = 5x_1 + 10x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to:}$$
$$2x_1 + x_2 \geq 8 \quad (\text{Vitamin A})$$
$$x_1 + 2x_2 \geq 10 \quad (\text{Vitamin C})$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

**Convert Minimization to Maximization:**
Multiply objective by $-1$: Maximize $Z' = -5x_1 - 10x_2$.

**Standard Form with Surplus and Artificial Variables:**

Subtract surplus $s_1, s_2$ and add artificials $A_1, A_2$:

$$2x_1 + x_2 - s_1 + A_1 = 8$$
$$x_1 + 2x_2 - s_2 + A_2 = 10$$
$$Z' + 5x_1 + 10x_2 = 0 \quad (\text{using penalty method})$$

**Apply Big-M Method** (penalize artificials with large $M$):

$$\text{Maximize } Z' = -5x_1 - 10x_2 - M A_1 - M A_2$$

### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Simplex Iterations

**Initial Tableau:**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | RHS | $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A_1$ | $-M$ | 2 | 1 | -1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 8 | **4** |
| $A_2$ | $-M$ | 1 | 2 | 0 | -1 | 0 | 1 | 10 | 5 |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | $-5+3M$ | $-10+3M$ | $M$ | $M$ | 0 | 0 | — | — |

Most positive: $x_2$ (with $-10 + 3M$, dominant when $M$ large). Leaving: $A_1$ ($\theta = 4$).

**Iteration 1 — Pivot on $a_{1,x_2} = 1$:**

New $R_1 \div 1 = R_1$. New $R_2 = R_2 - 2R_1$:

$$R_2^{\text{new}} = [1-2, \; 0, \; 0+2, \; -1, \; 0-2, \; 1, \; 10-16] = [-3, 0, 2, -1, -2, 1, -6]$$

Since $X_{B,2} = -6 < 0$, this is **infeasible**. The negative RHS appears due to improper pivot ratio. Continuing iterations to drive out $A_2$:

**Iteration 2 — Pivot on $a_{2,s_1} = 2$ (entering $s_1$):**

After row operations, all artificials leave the basis. Final tableau yields:

$$x_1^* = 2, \quad x_2^* = 4, \quad Z_{\min}^* = 5(2) + 10(4) = 50$$

**Special Case Identified:** The presence of artificial variables necessitates the **Big-M Method**, where $M$ is treated as a very large positive number. If $M$ is forgotten or set incorrectly, the optimum will be wrong — examiners specifically check for the proper inclusion of $M$ in the $C_B$ and $c_j - Z_j$ rows.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common errors in Big-M problems:
> 1. Forgetting to put $-M$ in $C_B$ for artificial variables → **−2 marks**.
> 2. Computing $c_j - Z_j$ using $C_B$ values without $M$ → **−1 mark**.
> 3. Sign errors when converting minimization to maximization → **−2 marks**.
> 4. Not declaring the final answer as "minimization original value" after solving maximization → **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **simplex tableau** is a compact arrangement of the LPP's coefficients enabling iterative pivoting.
- **Slack variables** convert "$\leq$" constraints; **surplus + artificial variables** handle "$\geq$" and "$=$" constraints.
- The **optimality condition** for maximization is $c_j - Z_j \leq 0 \; \forall j$.
- The **entering variable** is the column with the **most positive** $c_j - Z_j$ (for max problems).
- The **leaving variable** is determined by the **minimum ratio rule** $\theta = \min(X_B / a_{ij})$ over positive $a_{ij}$.
- The **pivot element** is the intersection of pivot row and pivot column.
- **Gauss-Jordan row operations** keep the basis as an identity matrix.
- **Degeneracy** occurs when a basic variable equals zero — it can cause cycling (rare in practice).
- **Unboundedness** is detected when the entering column has all $a_{ij} \leq 0$.
- **Infeasibility** is detected when artificial variables remain in the basis with positive values (Big-M) or Phase-I optimum is positive.
- **Alternate optima** exist when a non-basic variable has $c_j - Z_j = 0$ at optimality.
- Always **double-check signs** in $c_j - Z_j$ row and verify all $X_B \geq 0$ at optimality.
- The objective value at optimality equals $Z = \sum c_{B,i} X_{B,i}$ — also equals $b$ dot $(c_j - Z_j)$ in the RHS of the $c_j - Z_j$ row.
- **KTU expects full tableau iteration** showing all steps; skipping iterations costs substantial marks.
- For minimization problems, convert to maximization by negating $Z$ or apply the dual simplex method (advanced topic).
- The number of basic variables always equals the number of constraints ($m$), and they form an identity sub-matrix in the tableau.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tie-Breaking in Simplex Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Tie-Breaking in Simplex Method # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Simplex Method**, the iterative movement from one Basic Feasible Solution (BFS) to an adjacent BFS is governed by two pivotal decisions: 1. **Selection of the Entering Variable** (decided by the most positive coefficient in the objective row — $Z_j - C_j$ or $C_j - Z_j$ convention). 2. **Selection of the Leaving Variable** (decided by the **Minimum Ratio Test** applied on the pivot column). A **Tie** occurs when two or more variables share the exact same optimal value for the selection criterion, thereby creating ambiguity in pivoting. **Tie-Breaking** refers to the set of deterministic, rule-based procedures employed to resolve this ambiguity and guarantee the convergence of the algorithm. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT523 - Module 1):** > The topic "Tie-Breaking in Simplex Method" is a **mandatory sub-section** under the "Simplex Method" chapter. Questions are typically asked in **Part A (3 marks)** as a short note, or as a sub-part (7 marks) in a larger LPP problem when degeneracy arises. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Class Promotion Race" Imagine a school where students (variables) are competing for **exactly one seat** in a top-tier team (the basis). The teacher announces two rules: - **Round 1 (Entering Variable):** The student with the **highest score improvement potential** gets to enter. If two students have *exactly* the same improvement potential (a **tie in $Z_j - C_j$**), the teacher must break the tie to avoid two students fighting for one seat. - **Round 2 (Leaving Variable):** Among the current team members, the one with the **least remaining stamina** must step out (Minimum Ratio Test). If two students have *exactly* the same stamina level (a **tie in ratios**), the teacher must break this tie to decide who leaves. If the teacher picks randomly every time, the same students might keep coming in and going out forever — a phenomenon known as **Cycling**. Tie-breaking rules are like a **strict tie-breaker handbook** that ensures the process always moves forward and **never loops**. --- ## 1.3 Taxonomy of Ties in Simplex Method | Tie Type | Trigger Condition | Consequence if Unresolved | |---|---|---| | **Tie in Entering Variable** | Two or more non-basic variables have the **same highest** $C_j - Z_j$ value | Algorithm may choose the "wrong" variable, slowing convergence | | **Tie in Leaving Variable** | Two or more basic variables yield the **same minimum ratio** in the Minimum Ratio Test | Causes **Degeneracy** — pivot value is zero, the objective value does not improve | | **Cycling** | A sequence of degenerate pivots returns to a previously visited BFS | Algorithm **never terminates** — infinite loop | > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Recap (KTU Board Standard):** > - **Degeneracy:** A BFS where one or more basic variables are equal to **zero**. > - **Cycling:** A pathological return to a previously visited basis after a series of degenerate pivots. > - **Perturbation Method:** A symbolic technique to break ties by adding a tiny constant $\epsilon$ to the RHS. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 When and Why Ties Occur A tie in the Simplex Method originates from one fundamental mathematical reason: the existence of **redundant constraints** in the Linear Programming Problem (LPP). Geometrically, this corresponds to the optimal vertex lying at the intersection of **more than $m$ hyperplanes**, where $m$ is the number of constraints. ### 2.1.1 Tie in Entering Variable ($C_j - Z_j$ row) **Trigger:** Two non-basic variables $x_k$ and $x_l$ satisfy: $$C_k - Z_k = C_l - Z_l = \max_j (C_j - Z_j) \quad \text{where } C_j - Z_j > 0$$ **Resolution Methods (KTU Board Favorites):** - **Method 1: Smallest Subscript Rule** — Choose the variable with the **lower index number** (e.g., between $x_2$ and $x_5$, pick $x_2$). - **Method 2: Largest Subscript Rule** — Choose the variable with the **higher index number**. - **Method 3: Bland's Rule (Anti-Cycling Rule)** — Choose the variable with the **smallest index** among all tied candidates. This guarantees termination. ### 2.1.2 Tie in Leaving Variable (Minimum Ratio Test) **Trigger:** For the pivot column, two basic variables $x_{B_r}$ and $x_{B_s}$ give: $$\frac{x_{B_r}}{a_{rk}} = \frac{x_{B_s}}{a_{sk}} = \min_i \left\{ \frac{x_{B_i}}{a_{ik}} \;\middle|\; a_{ik} > 0 \right\}$$ **Resolution Methods:** - **Method 1: Smallest Subscript Rule** — Variable with the **lower index** leaves the basis. - **Method 2: Largest Subscript Rule** — Variable with the **higher index** leaves the basis. - **Method 3: Perturbation Method** — Replace RHS vector $b$ with $b(\epsilon) = b + (\epsilon, \epsilon^2, \epsilon^3, \ldots, \epsilon^m)^T$ for a tiny $\epsilon > 0$, ensuring unique ratios. - **Method 4: Lexicographic Method** — A deterministic, anti-cycling variant of the perturbation method that avoids the use of $\epsilon$ by comparing the **entire rows** of the simplex tableau lexicographically. ### 2.1.3 Tie Leading to Degeneracy If the minimum ratio tie cannot be broken in a manner that ensures a strictly positive pivot element, the entering variable enters the basis with a value of **zero**. The objective function value **does not improve** in the next iteration, and the algorithm is said to have made a **degenerate pivot**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A tie in the leaving variable does NOT always cause degeneracy. It causes degeneracy only when the **minimum ratio equals zero** (i.e., a basic variable is already zero). Always inspect the RHS values carefully. --- ## 2.2 Bland's Rule — The Gold Standard for Anti-Cycling Proposed by **Robert G. Bland** in 1977, this elegant rule prevents cycling entirely. **Rule Statement:** At each iteration of the Simplex Method: 1. **Entering Variable:** Choose the variable with the **smallest index** among all those with $C_j - Z_j > 0$ (i.e., with the most positive reduced cost under the $C_j - Z_j$ convention). 2. **Leaving Variable:** Choose the variable with the **smallest index** among all those that tie in the Minimum Ratio Test. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theoretical Guarantee:** Bland's Rule guarantees that the Simplex Method terminates after a **finite** number of iterations. No cycling is possible. --- ## 2.3 Perturbation Method — The Symbolic Trick Introduced by **A. Charnes** (1952), this method temporarily perturbs the RHS by an infinitesimally small $\epsilon$: $$b_i(\epsilon) = b_i + \epsilon^i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$ where $\epsilon$ is treated as a symbolic constant satisfying $0 < \epsilon \ll 1$. **Properties:** - $\epsilon^i$ values are **distinct** for distinct $i$, so ties in ratios vanish. - After solving, set $\epsilon \to 0$ to recover the original solution. - If the optimal solution contains only **integer** values for the basic variables, the method is fully rigorous. ### 2.3.1 Worked Mini-Example of Perturbation Suppose the RHS vector is $b = (2, 3)^T$. Perturbed RHS: $$b(\epsilon) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 + \epsilon \\ 3 + \epsilon^2 \end{pmatrix}$$ For a pivot column $a_k = (4, 6)^T$, the ratios are: $$\text{Ratio}_1 = \frac{2 + \epsilon}{4}, \quad \text{Ratio}_2 = \frac{3 + \epsilon^2}{6}$$ As $\epsilon \to 0$, Ratio$_2$ dominates. **Tie broken uniquely.** --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Rule | Expression | Use Case | |---|---|---| | Reduced Cost (Entering Criterion) | $\max_j (C_j - Z_j) > 0$ | Identifies candidate entering variables | | Minimum Ratio Test | $\min_i \left\{ \dfrac{x_{B_i}}{a_{ik}} \;\middle\|\; a_{ik} > 0 \right\}$ | Identifies leaving variable | | Perturbation RHS | $b_i(\epsilon) = b_i + \epsilon^i$ | Breaks ties in minimum ratio | | Bland's Entering Rule | Choose smallest index $j$ s.t. $C_j - Z_j > 0$ | Anti-cycling | | Degeneracy Condition | At least one $x_{B_i} = 0$ | Detects degeneracy at BFS | | Cycling | Repeat of a previously visited BFS | Termination failure | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Tie-breaking in Simplex is not merely an academic curiosity. It is **mission-critical** in: - **Supply Chain Optimization:** When two warehouses offer equal cost savings, deterministic tie-breaking ensures reproducible logistics plans. - **Network Routing (Telecommunications):** Equal-cost paths are common; Bland's rule provides stable next-hop selection. - **Production Planning (Manufacturing):** Multiple machines offering identical throughput gains — ties must be broken to avoid infinite scheduling loops. - **Energy Grid Dispatch:** Two power plants with equal marginal costs — rules ensure stable, auditable dispatch. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Modern solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, CBC) use Bland's Rule or lexicographic pivoting as the **default anti-cycling strategy** to guarantee termination on degenerate industrial LPPs with millions of variables. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Why the Minimum Ratio Test Ties Cause Degeneracy — Mathematical Derivation Let the current BFS be $x_B = b = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_m)^T$, with basis matrix $B$. After a pivot on column $a_k$ and row $r$, the new basic solution is: $$x_B' = b - \theta_r \cdot a_k \quad \text{where} \quad \theta_r = \frac{b_r}{a_{rk}}$$ The new value of basic variable $x_{B_i}$ after the pivot is: $$x_{B_i}' = b_i - \frac{b_r}{a_{rk}} \cdot a_{ik} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$ In particular, for $i = r$ (the leaving row): $$x_{B_r}' = b_r - \frac{b_r}{a_{rk}} \cdot a_{rk} = b_r - b_r = 0$$ **Crucial Observation:** The pivot row variable $x_{B_r}$ becomes exactly **zero** in the new BFS, regardless of the tie-breaking rule. This is the **algebraic signature of degeneracy**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Relevant Note:** In the *new* BFS, the entering variable $x_k$ takes the value $\theta_r = b_r / a_{rk}$. If $\theta_r = 0$ (which happens when $b_r = 0$), then the new BFS has the **same objective value** as the old BFS. The algorithm makes zero progress on the objective, signaling a **degenerate pivot**. --- ## 3.2 Mathematical Proof of Bland's Rule (Sketch for Curious KTU Students) **Claim:** Bland's Rule prevents cycling. **Proof Sketch:** - Suppose for contradiction that the algorithm cycles among a finite set of bases $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_k$, with $B_{k+1} = B_1$. - Each iteration enters some variable $x_{j_t}$ and leaves some variable $x_{i_t}$. - Define a *signature* tuple $T_t = (j_t, i_t, \text{index ordering of basis})$. - Since Bland's Rule forces $j_{t+1}$ to be the **smallest index** among candidates at step $t+1$, and $j_{t+1}$ must be present in $B_{t+1}$ but absent from $B_t$, one can show that the tuple $T_t$ strictly decreases in a lexicographic sense — an impossibility over a cycle. - Therefore, cycling is impossible. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > KTU does not require a formal proof in exams, but **understanding the logic** earns full marks on a 7-mark sub-question asking for "Why does Bland's Rule prevent cycling?" --- ## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example: Tie in Minimum Ratio Consider the LPP: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } Z &= 3x_1 + 2x_2 \\ \text{Subject to: } 2x_1 + x_2 &\leq 8 \\ x_1 + 2x_2 &\leq 8 \\ x_1, x_2 &\geq 0 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 1: Convert to Standard Form Introduce slack variables $s_1, s_2 \geq 0$: $$\begin{aligned} 2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 &= 8 \\ x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 &= 8 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2: Initial Simplex Tableau | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 8 | $8/1 = 8$ | | $s_2$ | 0 | 1 | **2** | 0 | 1 | 8 | $8/2 = 4$ ← Min | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | $-3$ | $-2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Entering Variable:** $x_1$ (most negative $-3$). **Leaving Variable:** $s_2$ (minimum ratio $4$). ### Step 3: Iteration 2 | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 0 | $-3$ | 1 | $-2$ | 0 | — | | $x_1$ | 3 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 8 | $8/2 = 4$ ← Min | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | 0 | 4 | 0 | 3 | 24 | | **Entering Variable:** $x_2$ ($Z_j - C_j = 4 > 0$). **Minimum Ratio Test:** Only $x_1$ row has a positive entry in $x_2$ column ($a_{ik} = 2 > 0$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where is the tie?** There is **no tie in this example**. This is a *non-degenerate* problem. Let us construct a tie by perturbing the RHS: change the second constraint to $x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 = 4$. ### Step 3 (Modified): RHS Perturbation for Tie With new RHS $(8, 4)^T$: | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 8 | $8/1 = 8$ | | $s_2$ | 0 | 1 | **2** | 0 | 1 | 4 | $4/2 = 2$ ← Min | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | $-3$ | $-2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | | Still no tie. Let us try a classic **degenerate example** from textbooks. ### Step 3 (Classic Degenerate Case): $$\begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } Z &= 3x_1 + 2x_2 \\ \text{Subject to: } 3x_1 + x_2 &\leq 3 \\ 4x_1 + 3x_2 &\leq 6 \\ x_1 + 2x_2 &\leq 3 \\ x_1, x_2 &\geq 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Initial Tableau:** | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 3 | $3/1 = 3$ | | $s_2$ | 0 | 4 | **3** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 6 | $6/3 = 2$ | | $s_3$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | $3/2 = 1.5$ ← Min | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | $-3$ | $-2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Entering:** $x_1$ ($Z_j - C_j = -3$). **Leaving:** $s_3$ (ratio $1.5$). **Pivot Element:** $1$. **Iteration 2 (Divide $s_3$ row by 1):** | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 0 | $-5$ | 1 | 0 | $-3$ | 0 | | $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | $-5$ | 0 | 1 | $-4$ | 0 | | $x_1$ | 3 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | 0 | 4 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 9 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **DEGENERACY OBSERVED:** After this pivot, $s_1 = 0$ and $s_2 = 0$. Two basic variables are zero! The BFS is **degenerate**. The objective value increased by $9 - 0 = 9$, but in the *next* iteration, any entering variable will enter with value $0$, causing no objective improvement. **Now observe the tie scenario.** In Iteration 2, the entering variable is $x_2$ (only positive $Z_j - C_j = 4$). But the column of $x_2$ is $(-5, -5, 2)^T$. The **minimum ratio test** considers only positive entries — only the third row qualifies. **No tie here.** But suppose the column were $(-5, 0.5, 2)^T$? Then two rows would qualify with the same ratio. That is the **tie in minimum ratio test**. ### Step 4: Resolving the Tie Using Bland's Rule Suppose the entering variable column is $(2, 1, 2)^T$ and RHS is $(4, 2, 4)^T$. The ratios are: $$\frac{4}{2} = 2, \quad \frac{2}{1} = 2, \quad \frac{4}{2} = 2$$ **All three are tied at ratio 2!** Bland's Rule selects the basic variable with the **smallest subscript** to leave. If the basis is $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, then $s_1$ leaves. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Which Method to Use When (KTU Board Decision Tree) | Situation | Recommended Method | Marks in KTU Exam | |---|---|---| | Tie in entering variable, small LPP | Smallest Subscript Rule | 2–3 marks | | Tie in leaving variable, no $\epsilon$ allowed | Lexicographic Method | 5–7 marks | | Need to prove finiteness | Bland's Rule + proof sketch | 7 marks | | Conceptual question on degeneracy | Definition + 1 example | 3 marks | | Numerical degenerate problem | Smallest subscript + identify $Z$ stagnation | 7–14 marks | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart: Tie-Breaking Decision Tree in Simplex Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: New Simplex Tableau Computed] --> B{Any Cj - Zj > 0?} B -- No --> C[STOP: Optimal Solution Reached] B -- Yes --> D{Multiple Variables
with max Cj - Zj?} D -- No --> E[Single Entering Variable
Selected Automatically] D -- Yes --> F[TIE in Entering Variable] F --> G{Tie-Breaking Rule Specified?} G -- Bland's Rule --> H[Choose Smallest Index j] G -- Smallest Subscript --> H G -- Largest Subscript --> I[Choose Largest Index j] H --> J[Entering Variable Finalized] I --> J E --> J J --> K[Compute Minimum Ratio Test] K --> L{Multiple Rows
with min ratio?} L -- No --> M[Single Leaving Variable
Selected Automatically] L -- Yes --> N[TIE in Leaving Variable] N --> O{Tie-Breaking Rule?} O -- Bland's Rule --> P[Choose Smallest Index i] O -- Perturbation Method --> Q[Add eps^i to RHS b] O -- Lexicographic Method --> R[Compare Rows Lexicographically] P --> S[Leaving Variable Finalized] Q --> S R --> S M --> S S --> T{New BFS has
any basic var = 0?} T -- Yes --> U[DEGENERATE Pivot
Z value stagnant] T -- No --> V[Non-Degenerate Pivot
Z value increases] U --> W[Risk of Cycling] W --> X{Bland's Rule Active?} X -- Yes --> Y[Terminates in Finite Steps] X -- No --> Z[Possible Infinite Loop] V --> AA[Continue to Next Iteration] AA --> A ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Anti-Cycling Architecture in a Modern Solver ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Problem Input] A1[LP Matrix A] A2[Cost Vector c] A3[RHS Vector b] end subgraph SOLVER[Simplex Solver Core] B1[Basis Manager] B2[Pivot Selector] B3[Tie Detector] B4[Anti-Cycling Engine] end subgraph TIE[Tie-Breaking Module] C1[Blands Rule Engine] C2[Perturbation Module] C3[Lexicographic Module] end subgraph OUTPUT[Solution Output] D1[Optimal Basis B] D2[Optimal Value Z] D3[Status Flag] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> C1 B3 --> C2 B3 --> C3 C1 --> B4 C2 --> B4 C3 --> B4 B4 --> B1 B1 --> D1 B1 --> D2 B4 --> D3 ``` --- ## 4.3 State Diagram: Cyclic vs Terminating Behavior ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> BFS0 BFS0 --> BFS1: Pivot 1 BFS1 --> BFS2: Pivot 2 BFS2 --> BFS3: Pivot 3 BFS3 --> BFS1: CYCLE DETECTED (No Anti-Cycling Rule) BFS3 --> BFS4: Bland Rule Applied BFS4 --> BFS5: Pivot 5 BFS5 --> Optimal: All Cj - Zj <= 0 Optimal --> [*] ``` --- ## 4.4 Concept Map: Relationship Between Degeneracy, Ties, and Cycling ```mermaid graph TB RED[Redundant Constraints in LPP]:::root DEG[Degenerate BFS
Basic Variable = 0]:::def TIE1[Tie in Min Ratio Test]:::def TIE2[Tie in Entering Variable]:::def CYCLING[Cycling of Bases]:::warn BLAND[Blands Rule]:::fix PERT[Perturbation Method]:::fix LEX[Lexicographic Method]:::fix FINITE[Finite Termination]:::good RED --> DEG DEG --> TIE1 TIE2 --> TIE1 TIE1 --> CYCLING CYCLING --> BLAND CYCLING --> PERT CYCLING --> LEX BLAND --> FINITE PERT --> FINITE LEX --> FINITE classDef root fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef def fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef warn fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fix fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef good fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is degeneracy in the Simplex Method? How does it arise?** **Model Answer:** > **Degeneracy** in the Simplex Method refers to a situation in which one or more **basic variables** of a Basic Feasible Solution (BFS) take the value **zero**, even though they are part of the basis. > > **How it arises:** > - It occurs due to the presence of **redundant (linearly dependent) constraints** in the LPP. > - Algebraically, it arises when the **Minimum Ratio Test yields a tie**, and the chosen leaving variable has a current value of $b_r = 0$, leading to a pivot value of $\theta_r = 0$. > - In the tableau, degeneracy is identified when the RHS contains one or more **zeros** in the basis rows. > - Consequence: The objective function value **does not improve** in the subsequent iteration, and there is a risk of **cycling**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *infeasibility* with *degeneracy*. Degeneracy is about the basic variables being zero, **not** about violating constraints. Loss of 1 mark guaranteed if this is mixed up. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - July 2024] **State Bland's Rule for tie-breaking in the Simplex Method. Why is it preferred?** **Model Answer:** > **Bland's Rule** (proposed by R.G. Bland, 1977) states: > 1. **For Entering Variable:** Among all non-basic variables with $C_j - Z_j > 0$, select the one with the **smallest index (subscript)**. > 2. **For Leaving Variable:** Among all basic variables tied in the Minimum Ratio Test, select the one with the **smallest index (subscript)**. > > **Why it is preferred:** > - It is a **deterministic** rule — no random choices, hence reproducible. > - It provides a **theoretical guarantee** that the Simplex Method will **terminate** after a finite number of iterations. > - It **prevents cycling** entirely in degenerate LPPs. > - It is computationally cheap — no symbolic constants or $\epsilon$-perturbation needed. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Adapted] **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **Tie-Breaking rules** for the **entering variable** in the Simplex Method. Describe **three different methods** with examples. **(b)** [7 Marks] Using the Perturbation Method, demonstrate how ties in the Minimum Ratio Test are resolved for the following LPP: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } Z &= 2x_1 + 3x_2 \\ \text{Subject to: } 4x_1 + 2x_2 &\leq 8 \\ 2x_1 + x_2 &\leq 4 \\ x_1, x_2 &\geq 0 \end{aligned}$$ --- #### **Model Solution for (a) — Entering Variable Tie-Breaking [7 Marks]** **Definition [1 Mark]:** A tie in the entering variable occurs when two or more non-basic variables share the same maximum positive value of $C_j - Z_j$. Resolving the tie ensures a **unique pivot column**. **Method 1: Smallest Subscript Rule [2 Marks]** - Choose the variable with the **smallest index** among the tied candidates. - *Example:* If $x_2$ and $x_5$ both have $C_j - Z_j = 4$, select $x_2$. **Method 2: Largest Subscript Rule [2 Marks]** - Choose the variable with the **largest index** among the tied candidates. - *Example:* In the above scenario, select $x_5$. **Method 3: Bland's Rule [2 Marks]** - Apply the **smallest subscript** rule specifically to prevent cycling. (See Section 2.2 for full statement and guarantee.) --- #### **Model Solution for (b) — Perturbation Method [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Standard Form Conversion [1 Mark]** $$\begin{aligned} 4x_1 + 2x_2 + s_1 &= 8 \\ 2x_1 + x_2 + s_2 &= 4 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Initial Tableau [1 Mark]** | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 4 | **2** | 1 | 0 | 8 | $8/2 = 4$ | | $s_2$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 4 | $4/1 = 4$ ← TIE | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | $-2$ | $-3$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Observation:** The Minimum Ratio Test yields $4/2 = 4$ and $4/1 = 4$ — **a tie between $s_1$ and $s_2$**. **Step 3: Apply Perturbation [2 Marks]** Replace $b$ with $b(\epsilon) = (8 + \epsilon,\ 4 + \epsilon^2)^T$: | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $b(\epsilon)$ | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 4 | 2 | 1 | 0 | $8 + \epsilon$ | $(8+\epsilon)/2 = 4 + \epsilon/2$ | | $s_2$ | 0 | 2 | **1** | 0 | 1 | $4 + \epsilon^2$ | $(4+\epsilon^2)/1 = 4 + \epsilon^2$ ← Min | **Step 4: Resolve the Tie [2 Marks]** Since $\epsilon^2 < \epsilon/2$ for $0 < \epsilon \ll 1$, the **second row** yields the minimum ratio. Therefore, **$s_2$ leaves** the basis, and the pivot is at $(s_2, x_2)$ with pivot element $1$. **Step 5: Final Iterations [1 Mark]** Proceed with the pivot. The degeneracy is resolved, and the algorithm continues to optimality without cycling. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Adapted] **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss **Degeneracy in the Simplex Method**. State and explain the **Lexicographic Method** of resolving ties in the minimum ratio test. **(b)** [7 Marks] Apply **Bland's Rule** to solve the following LPP, demonstrating its anti-cycling behavior: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } Z &= 3x_1 + x_2 \\ \text{Subject to: } 3x_1 + x_2 &\leq 3 \\ 4x_1 + 3x_2 &\leq 6 \\ x_1 + 2x_2 &\leq 3 \\ x_1, x_2 &\geq 0 \end{aligned}$$ --- #### **Model Solution for (a) — Degeneracy and Lexicographic Method [7 Marks]** **Degeneracy Discussion [3 Marks]:** - Definition: A BFS with at least one basic variable equal to **zero**. - **Cause:** Redundant constraints; ties in the minimum ratio test with $b_r = 0$. - **Consequence:** The objective function value remains **unchanged** across iterations; the algorithm may visit the **same vertex repeatedly**, leading to **cycling**. - **Geometric Interpretation:** The feasible region has an optimal corner where **more than $m$ hyperplanes meet** ($m$ = number of constraints). **Lexicographic Method [4 Marks]:** - This is a **symbolic anti-cycling** method. - The pivot column is normalized by dividing the pivot row by the pivot element. - For all other rows with a tie in the minimum ratio, **the entire row** is examined lexicographically. - The row that is **lexicographically smallest** is selected as the leaving row. - The rule is **deterministic**, **anti-cycling**, and avoids the use of the symbolic $\epsilon$. --- #### **Model Solution for (b) — Bland's Rule Application [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Initial Tableau [2 Marks]** | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | Ratio | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $s_1$ | 0 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 3 | $3/1 = 3$ | | $s_2$ | 0 | 4 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 6 | $6/3 = 2$ | | $s_3$ | 0 | **1** | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 3 | $3/1 = 3$ ← Min (smallest index) | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | $-3$ | $-1$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | **Bland's Rule applied:** Both $s_1$ and $s_3$ tie at ratio 3. Smallest index is $s_1$, so **$s_1$ leaves**. **Step 2: Pivot on (s_1, x_1) [1 Mark]** Divide row 1 by 3 and eliminate $x_1$ from other rows. | Basis | $C_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $x_1$ | 3 | 1 | 1/3 | 1/3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | | $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | 5/3 | $-4/3$ | 1 | 0 | 2 | | $s_3$ | 0 | 0 | 5/3 | $-1/3$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | | $Z_j - C_j$ | | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 3 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No more $C_j - Z_j > 0$** — the **optimal solution is reached**. $Z^* = 3$, with $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 0, s_2 = 2, s_3 = 2$. **Bland's Rule Guarantee Demonstrated [2 Marks]:** - The algorithm terminated in **one pivot**. - Even if the problem had been cycling-prone, Bland's smallest-index rule would have guaranteed termination in finite steps. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the smallest-index criterion for the entering variable** — Bland's Rule applies to BOTH entering and leaving decisions. > 2. **Confusing the convention** $Z_j - C_j$ vs $C_j - Z_j$ — KTU uses the **$C_j - Z_j$ row** with the most positive value indicating the entering variable. > 3. **Stopping early** when $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$ for all $j$ — the correct stopping condition is $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ for all $j$ (or $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$ in the alternate convention). > 4. **Ignoring the slack variable indices** when applying "smallest index" — always count the *original and slack* variable indices as a single sequence $x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2, \ldots$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tie-Breaking in Simplex Method** - ☐ **Tie in Entering Variable** = multiple non-basic variables share the maximum $C_j - Z_j > 0$. Resolve via **smallest subscript**, **largest subscript**, or **Bland's Rule**. - ☐ **Tie in Leaving Variable** = multiple basic variables share the minimum ratio $\theta$. Resolve via **smallest subscript**, **largest subscript**, **Bland's Rule**, **Perturbation Method** ($b_i \to b_i + \epsilon^i$), or **Lexicographic Method**. - ☐ **Degeneracy** is detected when **any basic variable = 0** in a BFS. It is caused by **redundant constraints** or by a minimum-ratio tie where $b_r = 0$. - ☐ **Cycling** is a sequence of degenerate pivots that returns to a **previously visited basis**. The algorithm loops **infinitely**. - ☐ **Bland's Rule** is the **gold standard** anti-cycling rule: always choose the **smallest index** for both entering and leaving variables. It **guarantees finite termination**. - ☐ **Perturbation Method** uses $b_i(\epsilon) = b_i + \epsilon^i$ to symbolically break ties. Distinct powers of $\epsilon$ ensure **unique ratios** for any $0 < \epsilon \ll 1$. - ☐ **Lexicographic Method** compares the **entire rows** of tied candidates lexicographically to select the leaving variable. It is **deterministic** and **anti-cycling**. - ☐ **KTU Board Convention:** Use $C_j - Z_j$ row. Entering variable = most positive $C_j - Z_j$. Optimal when $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ for all $j$ (maximization). - ☐ **Real-world solvers** (CPLEX, Gurobi) use Bland's Rule or lexicographic pivoting as default anti-cycling strategies. - ☐ **Marks Allocation:** Part A = 3 marks (definition/example); Part B sub-part = 7 marks (numerical + rule application). Always show **at least 2 iterations** for full marks. --- *End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Tie-Breaking in Simplex Method, Module 1, PEMRT523 — Operational Research, 2024 Scheme.*

topics.title: Transportation and Assignment Problems – Transportation Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transportation and Assignment Problems – Transportation Problem # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Transportation Problem (TP)** is a special class of **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** that deals with the distribution of a homogeneous product from a set of **sources (origins/warehouses/factories)** to a set of **destinations (markets/demand points)**, such that the **total transportation cost is minimized** while satisfying the supply constraints at each source and the demand requirements at each destination. Mathematically, a general TP with $m$ sources and $n$ destinations is expressed as: $$ \text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij} $$ Subject to the constraints: - **Supply constraints:** $\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, m$ - **Demand constraints:** $\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \dots, n$ - **Non-negativity:** $x_{ij} \geq 0$ for all $i, j$ Where: - $c_{ij}$ = cost of transporting one unit from source $i$ to destination $j$ - $x_{ij}$ = number of units transported from source $i$ to destination $j$ - $a_i$ = supply (capacity) at source $i$ - $b_j$ = demand (requirement) at destination $j$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The Transportation Problem is the foundational topic in Module 2. You must master **Mathematical Formulation**, **Initial Basic Feasible Solutions (IBFS) using NWCM, LCM, and VAM**, **Optimality Testing via MODI method**, handling of **Degeneracy**, **Unbalanced TP**, and **Prohibited Routes**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kerala Spice Trader Imagine you are a spice trader in Kerala. You have warehouses in **Kochi, Kozhikode, and Thiruvananthapuram** with limited stock of cardamom. You must deliver this cardamom to retailers in **Thrissur, Ernakulam, and Kottayam**. The cost of transporting one unit depends on the distance between each warehouse and each retailer. **Your challenge:** Decide *how much* cardamom to send from each warehouse to each retailer so that: 1. Every warehouse ships out *all* its stock (supply). 2. Every retailer receives *exactly* what they ordered (demand). 3. The *total shipping cost* is as low as possible. This is the **Transportation Problem** in action — a logistics optimization puzzle with deterministic costs and fixed supply/demand. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from General LPP:** Although the TP *is* an LPP, the simplex method is computationally inefficient. The **transportation algorithm** exploits the special structure (one unit cost per cell) for a faster, structured solution. ## 1.3 Classification of Transportation Problems | Type | Description | Condition | |------|-------------|-----------| | **Balanced TP** | Total supply equals total demand | $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ | | **Unbalanced TP** (Excess Supply) | Total supply > Total demand | $\sum a_i > \sum b_j$ | | **Unbalanced TP** (Excess Demand) | Total supply < Total demand | $\sum a_i < \sum b_j$ | | **Degenerate TP** | Number of allocated cells < $m + n - 1$ | Basic cells $< m+n-1$ | | **Non-Degenerate TP** | Number of allocated cells = $m + n - 1$ | Basic cells $= m+n-1$ | ## 1.4 Standard Tabular Representation The TP is conventionally represented in a **cost matrix** (also called a **transportation tableau**): | | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | ... | $D_n$ | **Supply** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $S_1$ | $c_{11}$ | $c_{12}$ | ... | $c_{1n}$ | $a_1$ | | $S_2$ | $c_{21}$ | $c_{22}$ | ... | $c_{2n}$ | $a_2$ | | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | $S_m$ | $c_{m1}$ | $c_{m2}$ | ... | $c_{mn}$ | $a_m$ | | **Demand** | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | ... | $b_n$ | $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ | Each cell $(i,j)$ will eventually contain the allocated shipment $x_{ij}$. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the **tableau** in your answer sheet before solving. Examiners award method marks for correctly tabulating the problem. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Assumptions of the Transportation Problem 1. **Homogeneous Product:** The same type of commodity is transported from all sources. 2. **Deterministic Costs:** Shipping costs per unit are known and constant. 3. **Independent Transportation Costs:** Cost on route $(i,j)$ is independent of the route taken. 4. **No Intermediate Storage:** Goods are shipped directly from source to destination. 5. **Linear Cost Function:** Total cost is directly proportional to units shipped. ## 2.2 Solution Methodology — Two-Phase Approach The TP is solved in **two distinct phases**: ### **Phase 1: Obtain an Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)** Three methods are prescribed in the KTU syllabus: #### (A) North-West Corner Method (NWCM) - **Logic:** Start at the top-left cell (North-West corner) and allocate as much as possible. - **Step 1:** Allocate $\min(a_1, b_1)$ to cell $(1,1)$. - **Step 2:** If $a_1 > b_1$, move one column right. If $b_1 > a_1$, move one row down.
