## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> State and explain the necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of a feasible solution to a Transportation Problem.

**Model Answer:**

A feasible solution to a Transportation Problem exists if and only if:

1. **All supplies and demands are non-negative:** $a_i \geq 0$ for all $i = 1, 2, \dots, m$ and $b_j \geq 0$ for all $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$.
2. **Total supply equals total demand (after balancing):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j
$$

If the problem is unbalanced, a **dummy row or column** must be added to make it balanced. The dummy absorbs the excess supply or demand with zero transportation cost. Once balanced, the constraint matrix has rank $m + n - 1$, guaranteeing a consistent solution.

**Distribution of Marks:**
- Condition 1 (non-negativity): **1 mark**
- Condition 2 (balance equation): **1 mark**
- Explanation of dummy row/column and rank logic: **1 mark**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> What is a degenerate Basic Feasible Solution in a Transportation Problem? How is it identified?

**Model Answer:**

A BFS of a Transportation Problem is called **degenerate** when the number of **positive (occupied) allocations** is **strictly less than** $m + n - 1$, where $m$ is the number of sources and $n$ is the number of destinations.

**Identification:** After constructing an Initial BFS using NWCM, LCM, or VAM, count the number of cells with positive values. If the count is less than $m + n - 1$, the solution is degenerate. Degeneracy often arises due to **tied allocations** or when supply/demand values coincide in a way that simultaneously exhausts both a row and a column.

**Resolution:** Introduce a small quantity $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) in one of the empty cells to artificially make the count equal to $m + n - 1$, ensuring the rank condition is satisfied.

**Distribution of Marks:**
- Definition with formula: **1 mark**
- Identification procedure: **1 mark**
- Resolution using epsilon: **1 mark**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the mathematical conditions required for the existence of a feasible solution to a Transportation Problem. Explain how the rank of the constraint matrix leads to the result $m + n - 1$ basic variables. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company has three factories F1, F2, F3 with monthly capacities of 200, 150, and 250 units respectively. It supplies to four warehouses W1, W2, W3, W4 with monthly demands of 100, 175, 125, and 200 units respectively. Check whether a feasible solution exists. If not, balance the problem and construct an Initial Basic Feasible Solution using the North-West Corner Method. The cost matrix (in Rs) is given below:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 8 & 6 & 4 \\ 4 & 7 & 9 & 3 \\ 8 & 5 & 4 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mathematical Conditions for Existence (7 marks):**

Step 1 — **Formulation of TP:** A TP minimises $Z = \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ subject to supply and demand constraints. The constraint matrix has $m + n$ rows and $mn$ columns (one for each $x_{ij}$).

Step 2 — **Non-negativity and finiteness:** All $a_i, b_j$ must be finite and $\geq 0$.

Step 3 — **Balance condition:** $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ (after introducing dummy rows/columns). *(2 marks)*

Step 4 — **Derivation of $m + n - 1$:** Sum the $m$ supply equations to obtain $\sum_{i,j} x_{ij} = S$. Sum the $n$ demand equations to obtain $\sum_{i,j} x_{ij} = D$. When $S = D$, both sums are equal, proving one equation is **linearly dependent** on the others. Therefore, rank $= (m+n) - 1 = m + n - 1$. *(3 marks)*

Step 5 — **Conclusion:** The number of basic variables in any BFS equals $m + n - 1$. This is the fundamental rank condition that guarantees the existence of a BFS. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Construction (7 marks):**

Step 1 — **Compute total supply and demand:**

$$
S = 200 + 150 + 250 = 600
$$
$$
D = 100 + 175 + 125 + 200 = 600
$$

Step 2 — **Check existence:** Since $S = D = 600$, the problem is **already balanced**. A feasible solution exists directly. *(1 mark)*

Step 3 — **Compute number of basic variables:**

$$
N = m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6
$$

So the BFS must occupy **6 cells**. *(1 mark)*

Step 4 — **Apply North-West Corner Method:**

| Allocation | Cell | Value | Remaining Supply | Remaining Demand |
|------------|------|-------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $x_{11}$ | $\min(200, 100) = 100$ | F1: 100 | W1: 0 |
| 2 | $x_{12}$ | $\min(100, 175) = 100$ | F1: 0 | W2: 75 |
| 3 | $x_{22}$ | $\min(150, 75) = 75$ | F2: 75 | W2: 0 |
| 4 | $x_{23}$ | $\min(75, 125) = 75$ | F2: 0 | W3: 50 |
| 5 | $x_{33}$ | $\min(250, 50) = 50$ | F3: 200 | W3: 0 |
| 6 | $x_{34}$ | $\min(200, 200) = 200$ | F3: 0 | W4: 0 |

Step 5 — **Count occupied cells:** Six cells (100, 100, 75, 75, 50, 200) — matches $N = 6$. *(1 mark)*

Step 6 — **Compute Initial Transportation Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{NWCR}} &= (5)(100) + (8)(100) + (7)(75) + (9)(75) + (4)(50) + (6)(200) \\
&= 500 + 800 + 525 + 675 + 200 + 1200 \\
&= 3900 \text{ Rs}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Conclusion:** A feasible solution exists, and the NWCR gives $Z = 3900$ Rs.

---

**Question B (14 Marks):**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Define a Transportation Problem. Explain the three cases that arise when comparing total supply and total demand, with suitable illustrations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Transportation Problem has the following cost matrix, supply, and demand values:

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|--|----|----|----|--------|
| **F1** | 3 | 1 | 7 | 50 |
| **F2** | 2 | 5 | 4 | 70 |
| **F3** | 6 | 8 | 3 | 30 |
| **Demand** | 40 | 60 | 50 | |

> Determine whether a feasible solution exists. If the problem is unbalanced, balance it. Using the **Least Cost Method**, construct an Initial BFS and compute the total transportation cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Three Cases (7 marks):**

A **Transportation Problem (TP)** is a special LPP that determines the optimal shipping schedule of a single product from multiple sources (with limited supply) to multiple destinations (with specified demand) to minimise total transportation cost. *(2 marks)*

**Three cases when comparing $S$ and $D$:**

- **Case 1 — Balanced ($S = D$):** A feasible solution exists without any modification. Example: Total supply = 100 units, total demand = 100 units. *(2 marks)*
- **Case 2 — Excess Supply ($S > D$):** Add a dummy destination with demand $(S - D)$ and zero cost row. Example: Supply = 150, demand = 130 → add dummy demand of 20. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Case 3 — Excess Demand ($S < D$):** Add a dummy source with supply $(D - S)$ and zero cost column. Example: Supply = 80, demand = 100 → add dummy supply of 20. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — **Compute total supply and demand:**

$$
S = 50 + 70 + 30 = 150
$$
$$
D = 40 + 60 + 50 = 150
$$

Step 2 — **Existence check:** $S = D = 150$, so the problem is **balanced**. Feasible solution exists directly. *(1 mark)*

Step 3 — **Number of basic variables:** $N = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$ cells. *(0.5 marks)*

Step 4 — **Apply Least Cost Method (LCM):**

Identify the cell with the minimum cost: $c_{12} = 1$ (F1 → W2). Allocate $\min(50, 60) = 50$. Now F1 is exhausted; W2 demand reduces to 10. *(1 mark)*

Next minimum: $c_{21} = 2$ (F2 → W1). Allocate $\min(70, 40) = 40$. W1 fulfilled; F2 supply reduces to 30. *(0.5 marks)*

Next minimum: $c_{33} = 3$ (F3 → W3). Allocate $\min(30, 50) = 30$. F3 exhausted; W3 demand reduces to 20. *(0.5 marks)*

Next minimum: $c_{23} = 4$ (F2 → W3). Allocate $\min(30, 20) = 20$. W3 fulfilled; F2 supply reduces to 10. *(0.5 marks)*

Next minimum: $c_{22} = 5$ (F2 → W2). Allocate $\min(10, 10) = 10$. Both exhausted. *(0.5 marks)*

**Allocation Table:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|--|----|----|----|--------|
| **F1** | 0 | 50 | 0 | 50 |
| **F2** | 40 | 10 | 20 | 70 |
| **F3** | 0 | 0 | 30 | 30 |
| **Demand** | 40 | 60 | 50 | |

Step 5 — **Verify occupied cells:** Five cells (50, 40, 10, 20, 30) — matches $N = 5$. *(0.5 marks)*

Step 6 — **Compute total cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{LCM}} &= (1)(50) + (2)(40) + (5)(10) + (4)(20) + (3)(30) \\
&= 50 + 80 + 50 + 80 + 90 \\
&= 350
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Conclusion:** Feasible solution exists, and LCM yields a cost of **350 units**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $-1$ in $m + n - 1$:** This is the single most common error. Students often write $m + n$, which leads to incorrect BFS count and degeneracy misidentification. **Always remember the rank condition is $m + n - 1$.**
>
> 2. **Not Balancing Before Solving:** If the TP is unbalanced, applying NWCM, LCM, or VAM *directly* gives a solution that violates the demand/supply constraints. **Always balance first.**
>
> 3. **Misidentifying Degeneracy:** Degeneracy is *not* a solution quality issue — it is a **count mismatch**. A BFS with fewer than $m + n - 1$ occupied cells is degenerate, even if the cost looks reasonable.
>
> 4. **Wrong Dummy Cost:** When adding a dummy destination for excess supply, the cost should be **zero** (or a small penalty if specified). Using arbitrary large costs will distort the optimal solution.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Non-Negativity Verification:** Examiners frequently award partial marks just for explicitly stating and checking $a_i \geq 0$ and $b_j \geq 0$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - A Transportation Problem is a special LPP for shipping a single commodity at minimum cost.
> - **Existence Theorem:** Feasible solution exists iff $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ (after balancing) AND all $a_i, b_j \geq 0$.
> - Three cases: **Balanced** ($S = D$), **Excess Supply** ($S > D$, add dummy destination), **Excess Demand** ($S < D$, add dummy source).
> - Dummy row/column cost is **zero** (or penalty $M$ if stated).
> - **Rank of constraint matrix** = $m + n - 1$ (one equation is redundant when balanced).
> - **Number of basic variables** in any BFS = $m + n - 1$.
> - **Degenerate BFS:** Positive allocations **$< m + n - 1$**. Resolution: introduce a small $\varepsilon$ in an empty cell.
> - **Non-Degenerate BFS:** Positive allocations **$= m + n - 1$**.
> - Initial BFS methods: **NWCM** (mechanical), **LCM** (cost-based), **VAM** (penalty-based). All are used to *start* the solution — optimality requires **MODI** or **Stepping Stone**.
> - **KTU Mnemonic:** "**M+N-1 is the magic number**" — every BFS must respect this count.
> - **Always check** the balance condition **before** constructing any allocation.
> - A **negative supply or demand** makes the problem **infeasible**, even if $S = D$.
> - The transportation cost function $Z$ is computed as $\sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ at the optimal allocation.
> - For KTU exams, **state the existence theorem, derive $m + n - 1$, and demonstrate one numerical example** — these three together guarantee full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solutions of Transportation Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solutions of Transportation Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transportation Problem (TP):** A special class of Linear Programming Problems (LPP) that deals with the distribution of a **homogeneous product** from multiple **sources (origins/origins)** to multiple **destinations (sinks/destinations)**, where the objective is to determine the optimal shipping schedule that **minimizes total transportation cost** while satisfying both supply constraints at origins and demand requirements at destinations.

Mathematically, the Transportation Problem is formulated as:

$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

Subject to the constraints:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \quad \text{(Supply constraints)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n \quad \text{(Demand constraints)}$$

$$x_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } i, j$$

Where:
- $m$ = Number of origins (sources)
- $n$ = Number of destinations
- $a_i$ = Supply at origin $i$
- $b_j$ = Demand at destination $j$
- $c_{ij}$ = Unit transportation cost from origin $i$ to destination $j$
- $x_{ij}$ = Quantity shipped from origin $i$ to destination $j$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Amazon Delivery Network:**
> Imagine you are the logistics manager for a major e-commerce company (like Amazon India). You have **3 warehouses** in Kerala (Kochi, Kozhikode, Trivandrum) and need to ship products to **4 cities** (Thrissur, Palakkad, Kottayam, Kannur). Each warehouse has a **fixed stock** (supply), and each city has a **fixed order requirement** (demand). The shipping cost per unit varies between every warehouse-city pair (Kochi–Kannur is expensive, Kozhikode–Kannur is cheap).
>
> Your job: **Decide HOW MUCH to ship from WHICH warehouse to WHICH city** so that all demands are met, no warehouse exceeds its stock, and **total shipping cost is minimized**. This is exactly the Transportation Problem!

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Essential KTU Vocabulary to Memorize:**
> - **Balanced TP:** $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ (Total supply equals total demand)
> - **Unbalanced TP:** $\sum a_i \neq \sum b_j$ (Add a dummy row/column with zero costs to balance)
> - **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS):** A solution satisfying all constraints with $m + n - 1$ allocations
> - **Non-degenerate BFS:** Exactly $m + n - 1$ occupied cells (allocations)
> - **Degenerate BFS:** Fewer than $m + n - 1$ occupied cells (requires special handling with $\epsilon$)
> - **Optimal Solution:** BFS that minimizes the total cost $Z$

### 1.4 Two-Phase Solution Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern — Always Solve TP in Two Phases:**
> 1. **Phase 1:** Find an Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS) using a heuristic method.
> 2. **Phase 2:** Test optimality and iterate to reach the minimum cost solution.

The three IBFS methods mandated by KTU syllabus are:
1. **North-West Corner Method (NWCM)** — Allocation logic based on position, not cost
2. **Least Cost Method (LCM)** — Allocation logic based on minimum cost
3. **Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)** — Allocation logic based on penalty/regret

The two optimality testing methods are:
1. **Stepping Stone Method** — Path-based evaluation
2. **MODI Method (u-v method)** — Algebraic evaluation using dual variables

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phase 1 Methods: Finding Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)

#### A. North-West Corner Method (NWCM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Ignore costs entirely. Start from the **top-left (North-West) cell** and allocate as much as possible, then move right or down systematically.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Begin at cell $(1,1)$. Allocate $x_{11} = \min(a_1, b_1)$.
2. If $a_1 > b_1$: demand of column 1 is satisfied → **move down** to cell $(2,1)$, reduce $a_1$.
3. If $a_1 < b_1$: supply of row 1 is exhausted → **move right** to cell $(1,2)$, reduce $b_1$.
4. If $a_1 = b_1$: both exhausted → **move diagonally** to cell $(2,2)$.
5. Repeat until all supplies and demands are exhausted.

**Merits:** Fastest, simplest. **Demerits:** Ignores cost — usually gives a poor (expensive) solution.

#### B. Least Cost Method (LCM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Allocate first to the cell with the **lowest unit cost** in the entire matrix. Tie-break arbitrarily.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Identify the cell with $\min(c_{ij})$.
2. Allocate $x_{ij} = \min(a_i, b_j)$ to that cell.
3. Strike out the satisfied row or column (or both if equal).
4. From the remaining matrix, find the next minimum cost and repeat.
5. If a tie occurs at zero in an already-struck row/column, skip it.

**Merits:** Better than NWCM because it uses cost data. **Demerits:** Still a greedy approach, not always optimal.

#### C. Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** VAM is the **most accurate IBFS method** taught in KTU. It uses the concept of **"penalty" or "regret"** — the opportunity cost of NOT allocating to the cheapest cell in a row/column.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. For each **row**, compute the penalty = (second smallest cost) − (smallest cost).
2. For each **column**, compute the penalty = (second smallest cost) − (smallest cost).
3. Identify the **row or column with the highest penalty**.
4. In that selected row/column, choose the cell with the **smallest cost** and allocate $x_{ij} = \min(a_i, b_j)$.
5. Strike out the satisfied row/column. Recompute penalties for the reduced matrix.
6. Repeat until all allocations are made.

**Why VAM Works:** A high penalty means there is a large cost difference — failing to use the cheapest cell will be very expensive. Thus VAM "preempts" costly mistakes.

#### 2.2 Phase 2 Methods: Testing Optimality

#### D. Stepping Stone Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** For each **unoccupied (empty) cell**, trace a closed loop (path) using occupied cells as vertices. The path must have corners only at occupied cells and turns at right angles. Compute the **net cost change** of routing one unit through this cell.

**Net Evaluation for empty cell $(i,j)$:**
$$\Delta_{ij} = \sum (\text{costs at + signs}) - \sum (\text{costs at - signs})$$

**Optimality Condition:** The solution is **optimal** if $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ for ALL empty cells.

**If any $\Delta_{ij} < 0$:** Improve the solution by adding units to the most negative $\Delta_{ij}$ cell, rebalancing along the path.

#### E. MODI Method (u-v Method) — Preferred in KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic:** The MODI (Modified Distribution) method uses **dual variables** $u_i$ (for rows) and $v_j$ (for columns). For occupied cells, $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$. For unoccupied cells, the **opportunity cost** is $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Set $u_1 = 0$ (or any row).
2. For each occupied cell: $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ → solve sequentially to find all $u_i, v_j$.
3. For each unoccupied cell: compute $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$.
4. **Optimality Test:** If all $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ → **OPTIMAL solution found**.
5. If any $\Delta_{ij} < 0$, select the cell with the **most negative** $\Delta_{ij}$ and form a closed loop. Allocate $\theta = \min(\text{values at - signs})$.
6. Add $\theta$ to (+) cells, subtract $\theta$ from (−) cells. Repeat from step 1.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Number of allocations in BFS | $m + n - 1$ | Detect degeneracy if fewer |
| NWCM total cost | $Z = \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ | Direct summation |
| LCM tie-break | Choose any lowest cost cell | If multiple minima |
| VAM penalty (row $i$) | $P_i = (\text{2nd min in row}) - (\text{1st min in row})$ | Computed for all rows/cols |
| VAM penalty (col $j$) | $P_j = (\text{2nd min in col}) - (\text{1st min in col})$ | Computed for all rows/cols |
| Stepping Stone $\Delta_{ij}$ | $\Delta = \sum c^{+}_{ij} - \sum c^{-}_{ij}$ | Evaluate empty cells |
| MODI occupied cell | $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ | Solve for dual variables |
| MODI empty cell | $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ | Optimality indicator |
| Optimality Condition | $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0 \; \forall$ empty cells | $\forall$ means "for all" |
| Improvement value | $\theta = \min(x_{ij} \mid \text{at - signs})$ | Reallocate along loop |
| Degeneracy resolution | Place $\epsilon$ in independent cell | Maintains $m+n-1$ cells |
| Unbalanced TP — excess supply | Add dummy column with $c = 0$ | Makes $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ |
| Unbalanced TP — excess demand | Add dummy row with $c = 0$ | Makes $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Where TPs are used in Industry:**
> - **Manufacturing:** Distributing finished goods from factories to warehouses
> - **Agriculture:** Moving grain from farms to mills/markets
> - **Energy:** Optimal power dispatch from generating stations to substations
> - **Supply Chain:** Minimizing logistics cost in e-commerce (Flipkart, Amazon)
> - **Defense:** Troop/equipment relocation planning
> - **Healthcare:** Blood bank inventory distribution to hospitals

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: VAM + MODI Method

> [!NOTE]
> This is a complete, exam-ready KTU-style problem solved end-to-end. **Trace every step carefully.**

**Problem Statement:**
A company has 3 factories (F1, F2, F3) with supplies 200, 300, 500 units respectively. It must ship to 4 warehouses (W1, W2, W3, W4) with demands 180, 150, 270, 400 units. The unit transportation cost matrix is given below. Find the **optimal transportation schedule** using **VAM** for IBFS and **MODI** for optimality.

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 21 | 16 | 25 | 13 | 200 |
| **F2** | 17 | 18 | 14 | 23 | 300 |
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 41 | 500 |
| **Demand** | 180 | 150 | 270 | 400 | **1000** |

**Step 0: Check Balance**

$$\sum a_i = 200 + 300 + 500 = 1200$$
$$\sum b_j = 180 + 150 + 270 + 400 = 1000$$

$$\sum a_i \neq \sum b_j \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Unbalanced: Supply > Demand by 200}$$

**Add a dummy warehouse (W5) with demand 200 and zero costs** to make it balanced. The new cost matrix becomes:

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | W5 (dummy) | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 21 | 16 | 25 | 13 | 0 | 200 |
| **F2** | 17 | 18 | 14 | 23 | 0 | 300 |
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 41 | 0 | 500 |
| **Demand** | 180 | 150 | 270 | 400 | 200 | **1200** |

---

#### PHASE 1: Initial Basic Feasible Solution using VAM

**Iteration 1:** Compute penalties for all rows and columns.

For each row, penalty = (2nd smallest cost) − (smallest cost):
- Row 1: Sorted costs (0, 13, 16, 21, 25) → Penalty = $13 - 0 = 13$
- Row 2: Sorted costs (0, 14, 17, 18, 23) → Penalty = $14 - 0 = 14$
- Row 3: Sorted costs (0, 18, 27, 32, 41) → Penalty = $18 - 0 = 18$

For each column, penalty = (2nd smallest cost) − (smallest cost):
- Col 1: Sorted (17, 21, 32) → Penalty = $21 - 17 = 4$
- Col 2: Sorted (16, 18, 27) → Penalty = $18 - 16 = 2$
- Col 3: Sorted (14, 18, 25) → Penalty = $18 - 14 = 4$
- Col 4: Sorted (13, 23, 41) → Penalty = $23 - 13 = 10$
- Col 5: All zeros → Penalty = $0 - 0 = 0$

| Penalty | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | W5 | |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 21 | 16 | 25 | 13 | 0 | 13 |
| **F2** | 17 | 18 | 14 | 23 | 0 | 14 |
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 41 | 0 | **18** ← Max |
| **Penalty** | 4 | 2 | 4 | 10 | 0 | |

**Highest penalty = 18 → Row F3.** In Row F3, minimum cost = $0$ at cell (F3, W5).

$$\text{Allocate } x_{35} = \min(500, 200) = 200$$

F3 becomes $500 - 200 = 300$. W5 is satisfied → strike out column W5.

**Updated Matrix (Column W5 removed):**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 21 | 16 | 25 | 13 | 200 |
| **F2** | 17 | 18 | 14 | 23 | 300 |
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 41 | 300 |
| **Demand** | 180 | 150 | 270 | 400 | |

**Iteration 2:** Recompute penalties.

- Row 1: Sorted (13, 16, 21, 25) → Penalty = $16 - 13 = 3$
- Row 2: Sorted (14, 17, 18, 23) → Penalty = $17 - 14 = 3$
- Row 3: Sorted (18, 27, 32, 41) → Penalty = $27 - 18 = 9$

- Col 1: Sorted (17, 21, 32) → Penalty = $21 - 17 = 4$
- Col 2: Sorted (16, 18, 27) → Penalty = $18 - 16 = 2$
- Col 3: Sorted (14, 18, 25) → Penalty = $18 - 14 = 4$
- Col 4: Sorted (13, 23, 41) → Penalty = $23 - 13 = 10$ ← Max

| Penalty | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 21 | 16 | 25 | 13 | 3 |
| **F2** | 17 | 18 | 14 | 23 | 3 |
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 41 | 9 |
| **Penalty** | 4 | 2 | 4 | **10** | |

**Highest penalty = 10 → Column W4.** In W4, minimum cost = $13$ at cell (F1, W4).

$$\text{Allocate } x_{14} = \min(200, 400) = 200$$

F1 becomes $200 - 200 = 0$. F1 is satisfied → strike out row F1.

**Updated Matrix (Row F1 removed):**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F2** | 17 | 18 | 14 | 23 | 300 |
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 41 | 300 |
| **Demand** | 180 | 150 | 270 | 200 | |

**Iteration 3:** Recompute penalties.

- Row 2: Sorted (14, 17, 18, 23) → Penalty = $17 - 14 = 3$
- Row 3: Sorted (18, 27, 32, 41) → Penalty = $27 - 18 = 9$

- Col 1: Sorted (17, 32) → Penalty = $32 - 17 = 15$ ← Max
- Col 2: Sorted (18, 27) → Penalty = $27 - 18 = 9$
- Col 3: Sorted (14, 18) → Penalty = $18 - 14 = 4$
- Col 4: Sorted (23, 41) → Penalty = $41 - 23 = 18$ ← Max (Tie)

**Tie between Col 1 (15) and Col 4 (18).** Col 4 has the higher penalty → select Col 4. In Col 4, min cost = $23$ at cell (F2, W4).

$$\text{Allocate } x_{24} = \min(300, 200) = 200$$

F2 becomes $300 - 200 = 100$. W4 is satisfied → strike out column W4.

**Updated Matrix:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F2** | 17 | 18 | 14 | 100 |
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 300 |
| **Demand** | 180 | 150 | 270 | |

**Iteration 4:** Recompute penalties.

- Row 2: Sorted (14, 17, 18) → Penalty = $17 - 14 = 3$
- Row 3: Sorted (18, 27, 32) → Penalty = $27 - 18 = 9$ ← Max

- Col 1: Sorted (17, 32) → Penalty = $32 - 17 = 15$ ← Max
- Col 2: Sorted (18, 27) → Penalty = $27 - 18 = 9$
- Col 3: Sorted (14, 18) → Penalty = $18 - 14 = 4$

**Highest penalty = 15 (Col 1) and 9 (Row 3) — Col 1 wins.** In Col 1, min cost = $17$ at cell (F2, W1).

$$\text{Allocate } x_{21} = \min(100, 180) = 100$$

F2 becomes $0$ → strike out row F2.

**Updated Matrix:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F3** | 32 | 27 | 18 | 300 |
| **Demand** | 80 | 150 | 270 | |

**Iteration 5:** Recompute penalties.

- Row 3: Sorted (18, 27, 32) → Penalty = $27 - 18 = 9$
- Col 1: Only 32 → Penalty = 0 (insufficient data; convention: 0)
- Col 2: Only 27 → Penalty = 0
- Col 3: Only 18 → Penalty = 0

**Row 3 has highest penalty = 9.** Min cost in Row 3 = $18$ at cell (F3, W3).

$$\text{Allocate } x_{33} = \min(300, 270) = 270$$

F3 becomes $300 - 270 = 30$. W3 is satisfied → strike out column W3.

**Iteration 6:** Only F3 remains with 30 units and W1 needs 80, W2 needs 150.

- Allocate $x_{31} = \min(30, 80) = 30$
- F3 becomes 0. W1 now needs $80 - 30 = 50$.
- Allocate $x_{32} = \min(0, 50) = 0$ (degenerate — F3 is already exhausted)

**Final IBFS Allocations:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | W5 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | | | | 200 | | 200 |
| **F2** | 100 | | | 200 | | 300 |
| **F3** | 30 | 0 | 270 | | 200 | 500 |
| **Demand** | 180 | 150 | 270 | 400 | 200 | |

**Count allocations:** $x_{14}, x_{21}, x_{24}, x_{31}, x_{32}, x_{33}, x_{35}$ = 7 cells. Required = $m + n - 1 = 3 + 5 - 1 = 7$. ✓ **Non-degenerate.**

**Initial Transportation Cost:**

$$Z_{IBFS} = (13 \times 200) + (17 \times 100) + (23 \times 200) + (32 \times 30) + (27 \times 0) + (18 \times 270) + (0 \times 200)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{IBFS} &= 2600 + 1700 + 4600 + 960 + 0 + 4860 + 0 \\
&= 14720
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### PHASE 2: Optimality Test using MODI Method

**Step 1: Set up equations for $u_i$ and $v_j$.**

For each occupied cell: $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$. Set $u_1 = 0$.

| Occupied Cell | Equation | Value |
|---|---|---|
| (F1, W4) | $u_1 + v_4 = 13$ | $0 + v_4 = 13 \Rightarrow v_4 = 13$ |
| (F2, W1) | $u_2 + v_1 = 17$ | $u_2 + v_1 = 17$ |
| (F2, W4) | $u_2 + v_4 = 23$ | $u_2 + 13 = 23 \Rightarrow u_2 = 10$ |
| (F2, W1) → back-substitute | $10 + v_1 = 17 \Rightarrow v_1 = 7$ |
| (F3, W1) | $u_3 + v_1 = 32$ | $u_3 + 7 = 32 \Rightarrow u_3 = 25$ |
| (F3, W2) | $u_3 + v_2 = 27$ | $25 + v_2 = 27 \Rightarrow v_2 = 2$ |
| (F3, W3) | $u_3 + v_3 = 18$ | $25 + v_3 = 18 \Rightarrow v_3 = -7$ |
| (F3, W5) | $u_3 + v_5 = 0$ | $25 + v_5 = 0 \Rightarrow v_5 = -25$ |

**Dual variables:** $u = (0, 10, 25)$, $v = (7, 2, -7, 13, -25)$

**Step 2: Compute opportunity cost $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ for empty cells.**

| Empty Cell | $c_{ij}$ | $u_i$ | $v_j$ | $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (F1, W1) | 21 | 0 | 7 | $21 - 0 - 7 = 14$ |
| (F1, W2) | 16 | 0 | 2 | $16 - 0 - 2 = 14$ |
| (F1, W3) | 25 | 0 | -7 | $25 - 0 - (-7) = 32$ |
| (F1, W5) | 0 | 0 | -25 | $0 - 0 - (-25) = 25$ |
| (F2, W2) | 18 | 10 | 2 | $18 - 10 - 2 = 6$ |
| (F2, W3) | 14 | 10 | -7 | $14 - 10 - (-7) = 11$ |
| (F2, W5) | 0 | 10 | -25 | $0 - 10 - (-25) = 15$ |
| (F3, W4) | 41 | 25 | 13 | $41 - 25 - 13 = 3$ |

**All $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$.** ✓ **The IBFS is OPTIMAL!**

**Optimal Transportation Schedule:**

| Route | Units | Cost/unit | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 → W4 | 200 | 13 | 2600 |
| F2 → W1 | 100 | 17 | 1700 |
| F2 → W4 | 200 | 23 | 4600 |
| F3 → W1 | 30 | 32 | 960 |
| F3 → W3 | 270 | 18 | 4860 |
| F3 → W5 (no shipment) | 200 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | | | **14720** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** $x_{35} = 200$ units remain unshipped (allocated to dummy warehouse with zero cost). In reality, this means F3 has 200 units of excess inventory that are not delivered. The **minimum total transportation cost is Rs. 14,720.**

### 3.2 Degeneracy — Mathematical Treatment

If the IBFS has fewer than $m + n - 1$ allocations, the problem is **degenerate**. This happens when:
- A row and column are both satisfied simultaneously in any allocation step.
- The VAM tie-breaking creates a degenerate situation.

**Resolution Technique:** Introduce a very small quantity $\epsilon$ (epsilon, treated as $0$ for cost calculations) into an independent empty cell to make the count $= m + n - 1$. The cell chosen must NOT form a closed loop with existing occupied cells.

### 3.3 Prohibited Table Trick: Avoid Using `|`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Grading Note:** In KTU answer sheets, students often use a tabular allocation format. Always clearly distinguish **occupied cells** (with allocation and cost) from **empty cells** (showing only cost). Strike out satisfied rows/columns with a single line. Mark degeneracies with $\epsilon$ in brackets.

### 4.1 Master Flowchart: Complete TP Solution Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Balanced Transportation Problem"] --> B["Check Balance: Sum a_i = Sum b_j?"]
    B -- "No" --> C["Add Dummy Row or Column with Zero Cost"]
    C --> D["Balanced TP"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D
    D --> E["PHASE 1: Find IBFS"]
    E --> F1["Method 1: NWCM"]
    E --> F2["Method 2: Least Cost Method"]
    E --> F3["Method 3: VAM Penalty Method"]
    F1 --> G["Verify: m + n - 1 Allocations"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G -- "Degenerate" --> H["Add Epsilon to Independent Cell"]
    H --> I["PHASE 2: Optimality Test"]
    G -- "Non-Degenerate" --> I
    I --> J1["Method A: Stepping Stone"]
    I --> J2["Method B: MODI u-v Method"]
    J1 --> K["All Delta ij >= 0?"]
    J2 --> K
    K -- "Yes" --> L["OPTIMAL SOLUTION FOUND"]
    K -- "No" --> M["Select Most Negative Delta ij"]
    M --> N["Form Closed Loop"]
    N --> O["Allocate Theta = min values at negative corners"]
    O --> I
    L --> P["END: Report Min Cost Z"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    M --> R1 --> R2
    M --> C1 --> C2
    R2 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> U1 --> U2
    U2 --> R1
    U2 --> C1
```

### 4.3 MODI Method Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Current BFS with m+n-1 Occupied Cells"] --> B["Set u_1 = 0"]
    B --> C["Solve u_i + v_j = c_ij for all occupied cells"]
    C --> D["Compute Delta_ij = c_ij - u_i - v_j for all empty cells"]
    D --> E{"All Delta_ij >= 0 ?"}
    E -- "YES" --> F["STOP Optimal Solution Reached"]
    E -- "NO" --> G["Select cell with most negative Delta_ij"]
    G --> H["Draw closed loop through occupied cells"]
    H --> I["Assign plus and minus signs alternately"]
    I --> J["Theta = min allocation at minus corners"]
    J --> K["Add Theta to plus corners subtract from minus corners"]
    K --> C
```
