## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the Transportation Problem. State the necessary and sufficient condition for a balanced Transportation Problem.*

**Model Answer:**
The Transportation Problem is a special class of Linear Programming Problem that aims to determine the optimal shipping schedule of a homogeneous product from multiple origins (sources) to multiple destinations (sinks) such that the total transportation cost is minimized, subject to supply and demand constraints.

**Necessary and sufficient condition for balance:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j$$

That is, **total supply must equal total demand**. If unbalanced, a dummy row/column with zero cost is added to restore balance.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks breakdown: Definition (1.5) + Condition with equation (1) + Dummy concept (0.5).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is degeneracy in a Transportation Problem? How is it resolved?*

**Model Answer:**
**Degeneracy** in a Transportation Problem occurs when the number of occupied (allocated) cells in the basic feasible solution is **less than** $m + n - 1$, where $m$ is the number of origins and $n$ is the number of destinations.

**Resolution:** A very small quantity $\epsilon$ (epsilon) is introduced into an **independent empty cell** (one that does not form a closed loop with existing occupied cells) to make the total occupied cells equal to $m + n - 1$. The value of $\epsilon$ is treated as zero in cost calculations but maintains the mathematical structure of the BFS.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks breakdown: Definition (1) + Formula for required allocations (1) + Resolution method (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below are TWO alternative full questions (OR pattern).

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**A company has 4 factories shipping to 4 warehouses. Solve the following Transportation Problem to find the minimum cost:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 19 | 30 | 50 | 10 | 7 |
| **F2** | 70 | 30 | 40 | 60 | 9 |
| **F3** | 40 | 8 | 70 | 20 | 18 |
| **F4** | 5 | 20 | 40 | 30 | 4 |
| **Demand** | 11 | 6 | 13 | 8 | **38** |

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the IBFS using Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM). Compute the initial transportation cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Test the IBFS for optimality using the MODI method. If not optimal, find the optimal solution.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Check Balance:** $\sum a_i = 7 + 9 + 18 + 4 = 38$. $\sum b_j = 11 + 6 + 13 + 8 = 38$. **Balanced.** ✓

**VAM Iteration 1 — Compute Penalties:**

Row penalties:
- F1: Sort (10, 19, 30, 50) → $19 - 10 = 9$
- F2: Sort (30, 40, 60, 70) → $40 - 30 = 10$
- F3: Sort (8, 20, 40, 70) → $20 - 8 = 12$
- F4: Sort (5, 20, 30, 40) → $20 - 5 = 15$

Column penalties:
- W1: Sort (5, 19, 40, 70) → $19 - 5 = 14$ ← Max
- W2: Sort (8, 20, 30, 30) → $20 - 8 = 12$
- W3: Sort (40, 40, 50, 70) → $50 - 40 = 10$
- W4: Sort (10, 20, 30, 60) → $20 - 10 = 10$

**Highest penalty = 15 (Row F4).** Min cost in F4 = 5 at (F4, W1). Allocate $x_{41} = \min(4, 11) = 4$. F4 exhausted → strike out row F4. W1 needs $11 - 4 = 7$.

**VAM Iteration 2:**

Row penalties (F4 removed):
- F1: (10, 19, 30, 50) → 9
- F2: (30, 40, 60, 70) → 10
- F3: (8, 20, 40, 70) → 12

Column penalties (W1 now has values 19, 70, 40):
- W1: Sort (19, 40, 70) → $40 - 19 = 21$ ← Max
- W2: Sort (8, 20, 30) → $20 - 8 = 12$
- W3: Sort (40, 50, 70) → $50 - 40 = 10$
- W4: Sort (10, 20, 60) → $20 - 10 = 10$

**Highest = 21 (Col W1).** Min cost in W1 = 19 at (F1, W1). Allocate $x_{11} = \min(7, 7) = 7$. F1 exhausted AND W1 satisfied → strike out row F1 and column W1.

**VAM Iteration 3:**

Row penalties (F1, F4 removed):
- F2: (30, 40, 60) → 10
- F3: (8, 20, 40) → 12

Column penalties (F1, F4 removed from columns W1; only W2, W3, W4 remain with values from F2, F3):
- W2: (8, 30) → $30 - 8 = 22$ ← Max
- W3: (40, 70) → $70 - 40 = 30$ ← Max (Tie — Col W3 wins or either)
- W4: (10, 60) → $60 - 10 = 50$ ← Max (Highest)

**Highest = 50 (Col W4).** Min cost in W4 = 10 at (F1, W4). But F1 is already struck! Next min in W4 = 20 at (F3, W4). Allocate $x_{34} = \min(18, 8) = 8$. W4 satisfied → strike out column W4. F3 becomes $18 - 8 = 10$.

**VAM Iteration 4:**

Row penalties:
- F2: (30, 40) → 10
- F3: (8, 20) → 12

Column penalties:
- W2: (8, 30) → 22
- W3: (40, 70) → 30 ← Max

**Highest = 30 (Col W3).** Min in W3 = 40 at (F2, W3). Allocate $x_{23} = \min(9, 13) = 9$. F2 exhausted → strike out row F2. W3 needs $13 - 9 = 4$.

**VAM Iteration 5:**

Only F3 remains (supply 10) and columns W2 (demand 6) and W3 (demand 4) remain.

- Min cost in F3 is 8 at (F3, W2). Allocate $x_{32} = \min(10, 6) = 6$. W2 satisfied.
- F3 supply now $10 - 6 = 4$. W3 needs 4. Allocate $x_{33} = 4$.

**Final IBFS Allocations:**

| | W1 | W2 | W3 | W4 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | 7 | | | | 7 |
| **F2** | | | 9 | | 9 |
| **F3** | | 6 | 4 | 8 | 18 |
| **F4** | 4 | | | | 4 |
| **Demand** | 11 | 6 | 13 | 8 | |

**Number of allocations = 6 = $m + n - 1 = 4 + 4 - 1 = 7$?** Wait — count: $x_{11}, x_{23}, x_{32}, x_{33}, x_{34}, x_{41}$ = 6 cells. Required = 7. **DEGENERATE!** Add $\epsilon$ at an independent cell, e.g., $x_{14}$.

**Initial Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{IBFS} &= (19 \times 7) + (40 \times 9) + (8 \times 6) + (18 \times 4) + (20 \times 8) + (5 \times 4) \\
&= 133 + 360 + 48 + 72 + 160 + 20 \\
&= 793
\end{aligned}$$

> For brevity in this exam-bank solution, the MODI optimality test follows the same procedure as detailed in Section 3.1: solve $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for occupied cells, compute $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ for empty cells, and iterate until all $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the North-West Corner Method and Least Cost Method for finding the Initial Basic Feasible Solution of a Transportation Problem. Compare their merits.

**Model Answer Outline:**

**North-West Corner Method (NWCM):**
- Allocation begins at the top-left cell $(1,1)$ regardless of cost.
- $x_{ij} = \min(a_i, b_j)$.
- If $a_i > b_j$: move down (row++), reduce $a_i$.
- If $a_i < b_j$: move right (col++), reduce $b_j$.
- If $a_i = b_j$: move diagonally (row++, col++).
- Does not consider cost → poor initial solution.

**Least Cost Method (LCM):**
- Identify the cell with minimum $c_{ij}$ in the entire matrix.
- Allocate $x_{ij} = \min(a_i, b_j)$ there.
- Strike out the satisfied row/column.
- Repeat from the reduced matrix.
- Uses cost data → better initial solution than NWCM.

**Comparison Table (verbal):**

| Aspect | NWCM | LCM |
|---|---|---|
| Uses cost data? | No | Yes |
| Speed | Fastest | Moderate |
| IBFS Quality | Usually poor | Better than NWCM |
| Complexity | Simplest | Slightly complex |
| Recommended use | Quick estimate | Practical applications |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the optimality conditions in the MODI method. What is the role of dual variables $u_i$ and $v_j$?

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Optimality Condition in MODI Method:**
The current BFS is optimal if and only if:
$$\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j \geq 0 \quad \forall \text{ empty cells } (i, j)$$

**Role of Dual Variables $u_i$ and $v_j$:**
- $u_i$ = dual variable associated with the $i$-th supply constraint (row).
- $v_j$ = dual variable associated with the $j$-th demand constraint (column).
- For **occupied cells**: $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ → this system of $m + n - 1$ equations in $m + n$ unknowns is solved by setting $u_1 = 0$ (arbitrary baseline).
- The opportunity cost $\Delta_{ij}$ represents the **marginal cost increase** if one unit is forced through empty cell $(i,j)$.
- If $\Delta_{ij} < 0$, the current solution is **not optimal**, and shipping through cell $(i,j)$ would **decrease** total cost.
- The cell with the most negative $\Delta_{ij}$ enters the basis in the next iteration, and a closed loop is constructed to reallocate shipments.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to balance** an unbalanced TP — always add a dummy row/column with $c = 0$.
> 2. **Tie-breaking errors in VAM** — when two rows/columns have the same maximum penalty, choose the one with the **lower minimum cost** to break the tie.
> 3. **Closed loop construction errors** in Stepping Stone / MODI — the loop must be a "rectangular" path with right-angle turns, using only occupied cells as corners.
> 4. **Misidentifying degeneracy** — always count the allocations: if less than $m + n - 1$, you MUST introduce $\epsilon$.
> 5. **Setting $u_1 = 0$ but failing to back-substitute** consistently — trace each $u_i, v_j$ from connected occupied cells.
> 6. **Allocating $\theta$ in the wrong sign position** — $\theta$ is added to $(+)$ corners and subtracted from $(-)$ corners of the closed loop.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solutions of Transportation Problem:**
>
> - [x] **TP Objective:** Minimize $\sum \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ subject to supply and demand equality constraints.
> - [x] **Balance check FIRST:** If $\sum a_i \neq \sum b_j$, add a dummy row/column with $c = 0$.
> - [x] **Three IBFS methods:** NWCM (position-based), LCM (cost-based), VAM (penalty-based).
> - [x] **VAM is the most accurate IBFS method** — KTU examiners expect VAM for full marks.
> - [x] **VAM Penalty formula:** $P = (\text{2nd minimum cost}) - (\text{1st minimum cost})$ for each row/column.
> - [x] **Number of allocations in BFS:** $m + n - 1$.
> - [x] **Degeneracy** = fewer than $m + n - 1$ cells. Resolved with **$\epsilon$** at an independent cell.
> - [x] **MODI Optimality:** Compute $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ for all empty cells. Optimal if $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$.
> - [x] **MODI Equations:** $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for occupied cells; set $u_1 = 0$ as baseline.
> - [x] **Stepping Stone Optimality:** $\Delta_{ij} = \sum c^{+} - \sum c^{-} \geq 0$ for all empty cells.
> - [x] **Iteration Rule:** If not optimal, enter the most negative $\Delta_{ij}$ cell, draw closed loop, reallocate $\theta = \min(\text{values at - signs})$.
> - [x] **Dummy cell allocations** represent unmet supply or unfulfilled demand (carry zero cost).
> - [x] **KTU exam tip:** Always show penalty table, allocation table, and dual variable computation in **separate, clearly labeled steps**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finding an Initial Basic Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finding an Initial Basic Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Transportation Problem** in Linear Programming, an **Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)** is the very first feasible shipment plan — a set of non-negative allocations $x_{ij}$ from $m$ origins (sources/factories) to $n$ destinations (warehouses/markets) — obtained **before any optimization** is applied. It serves as the starting point for the iterative **MODI Method (Modified Distribution Method)** or the **Stepping Stone Method** to reach the optimal solution.

The mathematical formulation of a balanced transportation problem is:

$$
\text{Minimise } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij}\,x_{ij}
$$

Subject to the constraints:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

$$
x_{ij} \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } i, j
$$

Where:
- $a_i$ = supply at origin $i$ (factory capacity)
- $b_j$ = demand at destination $j$ (market requirement)
- $c_{ij}$ = unit transportation cost from origin $i$ to destination $j$
- $x_{ij}$ = units shipped from origin $i$ to destination $j$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Any feasible solution to a transportation problem must contain exactly **$m + n - 1$** occupied cells (basic variables). If the number of occupied cells is less than $m + n - 1$, the solution is **degenerate**. If greater, it is **infeasible**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are the **logistics manager of a Kerala-based spice export company**. You have **3 warehouses** in Kochi, Calicut, and Thiruvananthapuram stocked with cardamom. You must deliver to **3 international buyers** in Dubai, London, and Singapore. Every shipping route has a different cost per tonne (fuel, customs, distance).

> "Given my warehouse stock and buyer demand, what is the **cheapest** first-draft shipping plan I can create?"

The **Initial Basic Solution** is precisely that first-draft plan. It doesn't have to be the cheapest possible plan — it just has to be a **valid** plan (no warehouse ships more than its stock; no buyer receives less than ordered). Later, optimization techniques (MODI / Stepping Stone) will *improve* this draft.

## 1.3 The Three KTU-Prescribed Methods

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus mandates the study of **three** algorithms for finding the IBFS:

| # | Method | Acronym | Core Logic |
|---|--------|---------|------------|
| 1 | North-West Corner Rule | NWCR | Allocate from the top-left cell, ignoring costs |
| 2 | Least Cost Method | LCM | Allocate first to the **cheapest available cell** |
| 3 | Vogel's Approximation Method | VAM | Allocate based on **maximum penalty** (opportunity cost) |

> [!NOTE]
> Among the three, **VAM generally yields the lowest initial cost**, closest to the optimal. **NWCR is the fastest to compute but the costliest draft**. **LCM is a balance** between speed and quality.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Algorithms — Logic Decomposition

### 2.1.1 North-West Corner Rule (NWCR)

**Logic**: Begin at the cell $(1,1)$ — the *north-west* corner of the cost matrix. Allocate the maximum possible quantity to that cell by taking the minimum of the corresponding row supply and column demand. Move one cell **right** if the row is satisfied, or one cell **down** if the column is satisfied. Repeat until all supply and demand are exhausted.

**Strength**: Mechanically simple — does **not** require the cost matrix.  
**Weakness**: Completely ignores costs; may produce a very expensive starting plan.

### 2.1.2 Least Cost Method (LCM) — also called Matrix Minimum Method

**Logic**: Identify the cell with the **lowest unit cost** $c_{ij}$ in the entire matrix. Allocate as much as possible to that cell. Cross out the row or column whose supply/demand is satisfied. Repeat with the remaining sub-matrix.

**Strength**: Cost-aware; produces a better initial plan than NWCR.  
**Weakness**: Myopic — looks at one cell at a time, not the strategic cost structure.

### 2.1.3 Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) — Most Preferred in KTU Exams

**Logic**: For each **row** and each **column** of the unreduced cost matrix, compute the **penalty** as the difference between the **two smallest** unit costs in that row/column. The penalty represents the *extra cost incurred by NOT shipping through the cheapest cell*. Select the row/column with the **largest penalty**; in that row/column, allocate the maximum possible quantity to the cell with the **smallest cost**. Strike off the satisfied row/column and repeat.

**Strength**: Considers opportunity cost; generally gives the best (lowest-cost) IBFS.  
**Weakness**: Computationally intensive — requires repeated penalty calculations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete set of formulas** required for solving IBFS problems in KTU 2024 examinations.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables Explained |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Total Cost | $Z = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij}\,x_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ = unit cost, $x_{ij}$ = units shipped |
| Balance Condition | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j$ | Required for a *balanced* problem |
| Number of Allocations (Non-Degenerate) | $N = m + n - 1$ | $m$ = origins, $n$ = destinations |
| VAM Row Penalty | $P_i = \vert\, c_{i(2)} - c_{i(1)} \,\vert$ | Two smallest costs in row $i$ |
| VAM Column Penalty | $Q_j = \vert\, c_{(2)j} - c_{(1)j} \,\vert$ | Two smallest costs in column $j$ |
| Degeneracy Condition | Number of occupied cells $< m + n - 1$ | Requires $\varepsilon$ adjustment |
| Allocation Quantity at $(i,j)$ | $x_{ij} = \min(a_i^{\text{rem}},\, b_j^{\text{rem}})$ | Remaining supply vs remaining demand |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Valuation:** In VAM penalty calculations, if a row/column has only **one** feasible cost left, the penalty is taken as the **difference between that cost and zero** (or is set to a very large value, depending on the textbook convention). State your assumption clearly in the exam.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of IBFS |
|--------|---------------------|
| Supply Chain Management | First-cut dispatch plans between factories and distribution centers |
| Military Logistics | Initial deployment of troops/ammo to frontline bases within budget |
| Power Grid Engineering | Preliminary allocation of generated power from plants to substations |
| Public Transport (KSRTC) | Initial bus scheduling from depots to routes minimizing fuel cost |
| Production Planning | Initial allocation of machines to job orders in FMS environments |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Worked Example (Common to All Three Methods)

To compare the three techniques rigorously, consider the following **balanced transportation problem** (3 origins, 3 destinations):

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\text{Origin} \backslash \text{Dest} & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & \text{Supply } (a_i) \\
\hline
O_1 & 8 & 6 & 10 & 20 \\
\hline
O_2 & 9 & 12 & 13 & 30 \\
\hline
O_3 & 14 & 9 & 16 & 50 \\
\hline
\text{Demand } (b_j) & 30 & 40 & 30 & \mathbf{100} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Verification of balance:**

$$
\sum a_i = 20 + 30 + 50 = 100 \quad ; \quad \sum b_j = 30 + 40 + 30 = 100 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Required allocations:** $m + n - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$ occupied cells.

---

## 3.2 Method 1 — North-West Corner Rule (NWCR)

**Iteration 1:** Start at cell $(O_1, D_1)$.  
Allocation: $x_{11} = \min(20, 30) = 20$.  
Row $O_1$ is satisfied → strike off Row 1. New $D_1$ demand = $30 - 20 = 10$.

**Iteration 2:** Move down to cell $(O_2, D_1)$.  
Allocation: $x_{21} = \min(30, 10) = 10$.  
Column $D_1$ is satisfied → strike off Column 1. New $O_2$ supply = $30 - 10 = 20$.

**Iteration 3:** Move right to cell $(O_2, D_2)$.  
Allocation: $x_{22} = \min(20, 40) = 20$.  
Row $O_2$ is satisfied → strike off Row 2. New $D_2$ demand = $40 - 20 = 20$.

**Iteration 4:** Move down to cell $(O_3, D_2)$.  
Allocation: $x_{32} = \min(50, 20) = 20$.  
Column $D_2$ is satisfied → strike off Column 2. New $O_3$ supply = $50 - 20 = 30$.

**Iteration 5:** Move right to cell $(O_3, D_3)$.  
Allocation: $x_{33} = \min(30, 30) = 30$.  
Both row and column satisfied. Process complete.

**Final allocation matrix (NWCR):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & a_i \\
\hline
O_1 & \mathbf{20} & - & - & 20 \\
\hline
O_2 & \mathbf{10} & \mathbf{20} & - & 30 \\
\hline
O_3 & - & \mathbf{20} & \mathbf{30} & 50 \\
\hline
b_j & 30 & 40 & 30 & 100 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Number of occupied cells:** $5 = m + n - 1$ → **Non-degenerate**. ✓

**Total transportation cost (NWCR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{NWCR}} &= (8)(20) + (9)(10) + (12)(20) + (9)(20) + (16)(30) \\
&= 160 + 90 + 240 + 180 + 480 \\
&= \mathbf{1150}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Method 2 — Least Cost Method (LCM)

**Iteration 1:** Find the minimum cost in the matrix. The smallest is $c_{12} = 6$ at cell $(O_1, D_2)$.  
Allocation: $x_{12} = \min(20, 40) = 20$. Row $O_1$ is satisfied → strike off Row 1. New $D_2$ demand = $40 - 20 = 20$.

**Iteration 2:** Find the next minimum in the remaining sub-matrix. The smallest is $c_{21} = 9$ at cell $(O_2, D_1)$.  
Allocation: $x_{21} = \min(30, 30) = 30$. Both Row $O_2$ and Column $D_1$ are satisfied **simultaneously**. We strike off both and introduce an $\varepsilon = 0$ allocation at any empty cell in Row 2 (or Column 1) to maintain non-degeneracy. New $D_2$ demand = $20$, $O_3$ supply = $50$.

**Iteration 3:** Find the next minimum in the remaining sub-matrix. The smallest is $c_{32} = 9$ at cell $(O_3, D_2)$.  
Allocation: $x_{32} = \min(50, 20) = 20$. Column $D_2$ is satisfied → strike off Column 2. New $O_3$ supply = $30$.

**Iteration 4:** The only remaining cell is $(O_3, D_3)$ with cost $16$.  
Allocation: $x_{33} = \min(30, 30) = 30$. Process complete.

**Final allocation matrix (LCM):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & a_i \\
\hline
O_1 & - & \mathbf{20} & - & 20 \\
\hline
O_2 & \mathbf{30} & \varepsilon & - & 30 \\
\hline
O_3 & - & \mathbf{20} & \mathbf{30} & 50 \\
\hline
b_j & 30 & 40 & 30 & 100 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Number of occupied cells:** $5$ (including $\varepsilon$) → **Non-degenerate**. ✓

**Total transportation cost (LCM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{LCM}} &= (6)(20) + (9)(30) + (9)(20) + (16)(30) + (13)(0) \\
&= 120 + 270 + 180 + 480 + 0 \\
&= \mathbf{1050}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Method 3 — Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)

**Iteration 1 — Compute penalties:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row penalties:}\quad P_1 &= \vert 10 - 8 \vert = 2, \quad P_2 = \vert 12 - 9 \vert = 3, \quad P_3 = \vert 14 - 9 \vert = 5 \\
\text{Column penalties:}\quad Q_1 &= \vert 9 - 8 \vert = 1, \quad Q_2 = \vert 9 - 6 \vert = 3, \quad Q_3 = \vert 13 - 10 \vert = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum penalty is $5$ at **Row $O_3$**. The smallest cost in $O_3$ is $c_{32} = 9$.  
Allocation: $x_{32} = \min(50, 40) = 40$. Column $D_2$ is satisfied → strike off Column 2. New $O_3$ supply = $10$.

**Reduced matrix after Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_3 & a_i \\
\hline
O_1 & 8 & 10 & 20 \\
\hline
O_2 & 9 & 13 & 30 \\
\hline
O_3 & 14 & 16 & 10 \\
\hline
b_j & 30 & 30 & 70 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Iteration 2 — Recompute penalties:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \vert 10 - 8 \vert = 2, \quad P_2 = \vert 13 - 9 \vert = 4, \quad P_3 = \vert 16 - 14 \vert = 2 \\
Q_1 &= \vert 9 - 8 \vert = 1, \quad Q_3 = \vert 13 - 10 \vert = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum penalty is $4$ at **Row $O_2$**. The smallest cost in $O_2$ is $c_{21} = 9$.  
Allocation: $x_{21} = \min(30, 30) = 30$. Both Row $O_2$ and Column $D_1$ are satisfied **simultaneously**. We strike off both. Introduce $\varepsilon = 0$ at any empty cell in the crossed row/column.

**Reduced matrix after Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_3 & a_i \\
\hline
O_1 & 10 & 20 \\
\hline
O_3 & 16 & 10 \\
\hline
b_j & 30 & 30 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Iteration 3:** Allocate $x_{13} = 20$ and $x_{33} = 10$. Process complete.

**Final allocation matrix (VAM):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & a_i \\
\hline
O_1 & - & - & \mathbf{20} & 20 \\
\hline
O_2 & \mathbf{30} & \varepsilon & - & 30 \\
\hline
O_3 & - & \mathbf{40} & \mathbf{10} & 50 \\
\hline
b_j & 30 & 40 & 30 & 100 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Number of occupied cells:** $5$ (including $\varepsilon$) → **Non-degenerate**. ✓

**Total transportation cost (VAM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{VAM}} &= (10)(20) + (9)(30) + (9)(40) + (16)(10) + (12)(0) \\
&= 200 + 270 + 360 + 160 + 0 \\
&= \mathbf{990}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis of All Three Methods

| Method | Total Cost (Z) | Number of Allocations | Quality |
|--------|----------------|------------------------|---------|
| NWCR | $1150$ | 5 | Worst (costliest) |
| LCM | $1050$ | 5 (with $\varepsilon$) | Moderate |
| VAM | $990$ | 5 (with $\varepsilon$) | **Best (cheapest)** |

**Key analytical observation:**

$$
Z_{\text{VAM}} = 990 < Z_{\text{LCM}} = 1050 < Z_{\text{NWCR}} = 1150
$$

VAM's lower initial cost is due to its **strategic penalty mechanism** — it considers the *opportunity cost* of not choosing the second-best alternative, allowing it to make globally informed decisions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right IBFS Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Given Balanced Transportation Problem] --> nodeB[Read Supply Vector ai and Demand Vector bj]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Verify Total Supply equals Total Demand]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Balanced?}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[Add Dummy Origin or Destination with Zero Cost]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Proceed to Method Selection]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF{Choose IBFS Method}
    nodeF -- Need Speed, Ignore Costs --> nodeG[NORTH-WEST CORNER RULE]
    nodeF -- Need Cost-Aware Simple Method --> nodeH[LEAST COST METHOD]
    nodeF -- Need Best Quality IBFS --> nodeI[VOGEL APPROXIMATION METHOD]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Compute Initial Total Cost Z]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Count Occupied Cells equals m + n minus 1?}
    nodeK -- No Degenerate --> nodeL[Add Epsilon to Unoccupied Cell]
    nodeK -- Yes Non-Degenerate --> nodeM[Output Valid IBFS]
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[END: Proceed to MODI or Stepping Stone Optimization]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transportation Problem IBFS Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Origin Supply Vector A] --> coreX[Transportation Cost Matrix C]
    inputB[Destination Demand Vector B] --> coreX
    coreX --> coreY{IBFS Engine}
    coreY --> coreZ1[North-West Corner Module]
    coreY --> coreZ2[Least Cost Allocator]
    coreY --> coreZ3[Vogel Penalty Calculator]
    coreZ1 --> outA[Initial Allocations x_ij]
    coreZ2 --> outA
    coreZ3 --> outA
    outA --> validator{Validator: m + n minus 1 Allocations?}
    validator -- Pass --> finalX[Feasible IBFS Output]
    validator -- Fail --> corrector[Epsilon Injection Module]
    corrector --> finalX
    finalX --> nextStage[Next Stage: Optimality Test via MODI Method]
```

## 4.3 Structural Representation of the IBFS Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1[PHASE 1: Initialize Allocation Count = 0] --> phase2[PHASE 2: Identify Target Cell via Method Rule]
    phase2 --> phase3[PHASE 3: Compute Allocation = min Remaining Supply, Remaining Demand]
    phase3 --> phase4[PHASE 4: Update Remaining Supply and Demand]
    phase4 --> phase5[PHASE 5: Strike off Satisfied Row or Column]
    phase5 --> phase6[PHASE 6: Increment Allocation Count]
    phase6 --> phase7{Allocation Count equals m + n minus 1 OR All Exhausted?}
    phase7 -- No --> phase2
    phase7 -- Yes --> phase8[PHASE 7: Verify Balance and Non-Degeneracy]
    phase8 --> phase9[PHASE 8: Compute Total Transportation Cost Z]
    phase9 --> phase10[END: Output Initial Basic Feasible Solution]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] 
**Define the term "Initial Basic Feasible Solution" in the context of the transportation problem. State the condition for non-degeneracy.**

**Model Answer:**

An *Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)* is a feasible solution to the transportation problem that satisfies all supply and demand constraints with non-negative allocations, obtained prior to any optimization step. It provides the starting point for techniques like the MODI method or Stepping Stone method to reach the optimal solution.

The **non-degeneracy condition** requires the number of occupied cells (basic variables) in the allocation matrix to be exactly equal to $m + n - 1$, where $m$ is the number of origins and $n$ is the number of destinations. If this number is less, the solution is degenerate and requires the introduction of an arbitrarily small quantity $\varepsilon$ at an unoccupied cell.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**Distinguish between the North-West Corner Rule and Vogel's Approximation Method for finding an IBFS.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | North-West Corner Rule (NWCR) | Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) |
|--------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Cost consideration | Completely ignores unit costs | Considers unit costs via penalty mechanism |
| Selection logic | Always starts at top-left cell and moves mechanically | Selects row/column with **maximum penalty** |
| Quality of IBFS | Usually the costliest draft | Usually the cheapest draft, closest to optimal |
| Computational effort | Minimal — purely arithmetic | High — requires repeated penalty calculations |
| Preferred in practice | Not preferred for real problems | Most preferred in KTU board exams |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Find the Initial Basic Feasible Solution using the **North-West Corner Rule** for the following transportation problem:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & D_4 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
O_1 & 19 & 30 & 50 & 10 & 7 \\
\hline
O_2 & 70 & 30 & 40 & 60 & 9 \\
\hline
O_3 & 40 & 8 & 70 & 20 & 18 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 5 & 8 & 7 & 14 & 34 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] Find the IBFS for the problem in part (a) using the **Least Cost Method**. Comment on which method gives a better initial cost.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — NWCR

**Step 1:** Allocate at $(O_1, D_1)$: $x_{11} = \min(7, 5) = 5$. Row 1 demand satisfied. Remaining $O_1$ supply = $7 - 5 = 2$, $D_1$ demand = $0$.

**Step 2:** Move to $(O_1, D_2)$: $x_{12} = \min(2, 8) = 2$. Row 1 exhausted. Remaining $D_2$ demand = $8 - 2 = 6$.

**Step 3:** Move to $(O_2, D_2)$: $x_{22} = \min(9, 6) = 6$. Column 2 demand satisfied. Remaining $O_2$ supply = $9 - 6 = 3$.

**Step 4:** Move to $(O_2, D_3)$: $x_{23} = \min(3, 7) = 3$. Row 2 exhausted. Remaining $D_3$ demand = $7 - 3 = 4$.

**Step 5:** Move to $(O_3, D_3)$: $x_{33} = \min(18, 4) = 4$. Column 3 satisfied. Remaining $O_3$ supply = $18 - 4 = 14$.

**Step 6:** Move to $(O_3, D_4)$: $x_{34} = \min(14, 14) = 14$. Both exhausted. Process complete.

**Number of allocations:** $6 = m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6$ → **Non-degenerate**. ✓

**Total Cost (NWCR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{NWCR}} &= (19)(5) + (30)(2) + (30)(6) + (40)(3) + (70)(4) + (20)(14) \\
&= 95 + 60 + 180 + 120 + 280 + 280 \\
&= \mathbf{1015}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — LCM

**Step 1:** Smallest cost in matrix is $c_{31} = 8$ at $(O_3, D_2)$.  
$x_{32} = \min(18, 8) = 8$. Column $D_2$ satisfied. Remaining $O_3 = 10$.

**Step 2:** Smallest in remaining matrix: $c_{14} = 10$ at $(O_1, D_4)$.  
$x_{14} = \min(7, 14) = 7$. Row $O_1$ exhausted. Remaining $D_4 = 7$.

**Step 3:** Smallest in remaining matrix: $c_{21} = 30$ at $(O_2, D_1)$.  
$x_{21} = \min(9, 5) = 5$. Column $D_1$ satisfied. Remaining $O_2 = 4$.

**Step 4:** Smallest in remaining matrix: $c_{23} = 40$ at $(O_2, D_3)$.  
$x_{23} = \min(4, 7) = 4$. Row $O_2$ exhausted. Remaining $D_3 = 3$.

**Step 5:** Only cell left: $(O_3, D_3)$ with cost $70$.  
$x_{33} = \min(10, 3) = 3$. Column satisfied. Remaining $O_3 = 7$.

**Step 6:** Last cell: $(O_3, D_4)$ with cost $20$.  
$x_{34} = \min(7, 7) = 7$. Process complete.

**Total Cost (LCM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{LCM}} &= (8)(8) + (10)(7) + (30)(5) + (40)(4) + (70)(3) + (20)(7) \\
&= 64 + 70 + 150 + 160 + 210 + 140 \\
&= \mathbf{794}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Since $Z_{\text{LCM}} = 794 < Z_{\text{NWCR}} = 1015$, the **Least Cost Method yields a significantly cheaper initial plan** — approximately **21.8% lower** in cost. This demonstrates the cost-awareness advantage of LCM over the mechanical NWCR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to verify the **non-degeneracy condition** ($m + n - 1$ allocations) and lose 1 mark. Always count the occupied cells in your final matrix and state explicitly whether the IBFS is degenerate or non-degenerate.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Solve the following transportation problem using **Vogel's Approximation Method** to find the IBFS:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & D_3 & \text{Supply} \\
\hline
O_1 & 2 & 7 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
O_2 & 3 & 3 & 1 & 8 \\
\hline
O_3 & 5 & 4 & 7 & 7 \\
\hline
\text{Demand} & 7 & 9 & 4 & 20 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the same problem, find the IBFS using the **North-West Corner Rule**. Compare the initial cost with VAM and state which method is preferred.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — VAM

**Iteration 1 — Penalty calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row penalties:}\quad P_1 &= \vert 4 - 2 \vert = 2, \quad P_2 = \vert 3 - 1 \vert = 2, \quad P_3 = \vert 5 - 4 \vert = 1 \\
\text{Column penalties:}\quad Q_1 &= \vert 3 - 2 \vert = 1, \quad Q_2 &= \vert 4 - 3 \vert = 1, \quad Q_3 &= \vert 4 - 1 \vert = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum penalty = $3$ at **Column $D_3$**. Smallest cost in $D_3$ is $c_{23} = 1$.  
$x_{23} = \min(8, 4) = 4$. Column $D_3$ satisfied. Remaining $O_2 = 4$.

**Iteration 2 — Recompute penalties for reduced matrix:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & a_i \\
\hline
O_1 & 2 & 7 & 5 \\
\hline
O_2 & 3 & 3 & 4 \\
\hline
O_3 & 5 & 4 & 7 \\
\hline
b_j & 7 & 9 & 16 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$$
P_1 = 5, \quad P_2 = 0, \quad P_3 = 1, \quad Q_1 = 1, \quad Q_2 = 1
$$

Maximum penalty = $5$ at **Row $O_1$**. Smallest cost in $O_1$ is $c_{11} = 2$.  
$x_{11} = \min(5, 7) = 5$. Row $O_1$ exhausted. Remaining $D_1 = 2$.

**Iteration 3 — Recompute penalties for reduced matrix:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & D_1 & D_2 & a_i \\
\hline
O_2 & 3 & 3 & 4 \\
\hline
O_3 & 5 & 4 & 7 \\
\hline
b_j & 2 & 9 & 11 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$$
P_2 = 0, \quad P_3 = 1, \quad Q_1 = 2, \quad Q_2 = 1
$$

Maximum penalty = $2$ at **Column $D_1$**. Smallest cost in $D_1$ is $c_{21} = 3$.  
$x_{21} = \min(4, 2) = 2$. Column $D_1$ satisfied. Remaining $O_2 = 2$.

**Iteration 4:** Allocate remaining: $x_{22} = \min(2, 9) = 2$ at cost 3, and $x_{32} = \min(7, 7) = 7$ at cost 4.

**Total allocations:** $5 = m + n - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$ → **Non-degenerate**. ✓

**Total Cost (VAM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{VAM}} &= (1)(4) + (2)(5) + (3)(2) + (3)(2) + (4)(7) \\
&= 4 + 10 + 6 + 6 + 28 \\
&= \mathbf{54}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — NWCR Comparison

**Step-by-step NWCR allocation:**

**Step 1:** $x_{11} = \min(5, 7) = 5$. Row 1 exhausted. $D_1$ remaining = $2$.

**Step 2:** $x_{21} = \min(8, 2) = 2$. Column 1 satisfied. $O_2$ remaining = $6$.

**Step 3:** $x_{22} = \min(6, 9) = 6$. Row 2 exhausted. $D_2$ remaining = $3$.

**Step 4:** $x_{32} = \min(7, 3) = 3$. Column 2 satisfied. $O_3$ remaining = $4$.

**Step 5:** $x_{33} = \min(4, 4) = 4$. Both exhausted.

**Total Cost (NWCR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{NWCR}} &= (2)(5) + (3)(2) + (3)(6) + (4)(3) + (7)(4) \\
&= 10 + 6 + 18 + 12 + 28 \\
&= \mathbf{74}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison & Conclusion:**

$$
Z_{\text{VAM}} = 54 \quad \text{vs} \quad Z_{\text{NWCR}} = 74
$$

The **VAM gives a 27% cheaper** initial cost. Therefore, **VAM is preferred** as the IBFS-generating method because its penalty-based logic mimics an *opportunity cost analysis*, producing a starting plan much closer to the true optimum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Penalty miscalculation**: Always sort costs in ascending order before taking the difference of the two smallest. Using the wrong pair costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Tie-breaking**: When two rows/columns have the **same maximum penalty**, KTU evaluators expect students to mention the tie-breaking rule used (typically: choose the one with the lowest cost in its most-allocated cell).
> 3. **Forgetting balance check**: If supply $\neq$ demand, students must add a dummy row/column with **zero cost** — failing to do so loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping degeneracy check**: Always end with the verification "$N = m + n - 1$ allocations → non-degenerate ✓" or explicitly handle the $\varepsilon$ case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read this 30 minutes before your KTU exam.**

- **Transportation Problem IBFS** is the *first* feasible shipment plan before any optimization (MODI / Stepping Stone).
- **Number of basic variables** required = $m + n - 1$ (the **fundamental count**).
- **NWCR** = top-left start, ignore costs, fastest but worst cost.
- **LCM** = allocate to the cheapest available cell, myopic but cost-aware.
- **VAM** = allocate to the cell with the **maximum row/column penalty** (difference of two smallest costs), best quality IBFS.
- **Degeneracy** occurs when occupied cells $< m + n - 1$ — fix by inserting $\varepsilon = 0$ at an unoccupied cell of a satisfied row/column.
- **Balanced check**: $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$. If not balanced, add a **dummy row/column with zero cost**.
- **Cost formula**: $Z = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij}\,x_{ij}$ — multiply each $c_{ij}$ by its allocation $x_{ij}$ and sum.
- **Penalty formula** (VAM): $P_i = \vert c_{i(2)} - c_{i(1)} \vert$ for row $i$; analogous for columns.
- **Tie-breaking in VAM**: When penalties tie, choose the row/column with the lower minimum cost cell.
- **Practical order of preference**: **VAM $>$ LCM $>$ NWCR** for KTU board exam solutions.
- **Always show**: Iteration table → penalty table → selected row/column → allocation → strike-off → reduced matrix (chain of reasoning for full marks).
- **Always conclude**: State total number of occupied cells, confirm non-degeneracy, and write the final $Z$ value with units.
- **Common KTU trap**: Forgetting that the IBFS is **not the final answer** — the question may continue into MODI optimality test, so clearly demarcate "IBFS" from "Optimal Solution."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vogel's Approximation Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vogel's Approximation Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)** is an iterative heuristic algorithm used in Operations Research to determine an **Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)** for a balanced (or post-balancing) Transportation Problem. It works on the principle of **opportunity cost / penalty cost**, where for every row and every column of the unit cost matrix, the arithmetic difference between the **two smallest transportation costs** is computed. The row or column with the **largest penalty** is then selected, and the maximum feasible quantity is allocated to the cell bearing the **least unit cost** within that row/column. This process is repeated iteratively until all supply and demand constraints are satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** *"VAM is a method of finding an initial basic feasible solution to a transportation problem by taking into account the penalty (difference between the smallest and the next smallest cost) for each row and column, selecting the largest penalty, and allocating the maximum possible units to the cell with the minimum cost in that row or column."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Logistics Manager"

Imagine you are a **dispatch head at a courier company** with three warehouses (sources) and four delivery hubs (destinations). Each route has a different per-unit shipping cost listed on a rate card. A *novice dispatcher* would either grab the cheapest route blindly (this is the **Least Cost Method**) or fill the top-left cell first (**North-West Corner Method**).

A *VAM-style expert dispatcher* does something clever: before committing, they scan the **entire** rate card row-wise and column-wise and ask — *"If I deliberately avoid the cheapest route in this row, what extra cost (regret) am I incurring by picking the second cheapest instead?"* The row/column with the **largest regret** is screaming for attention, because any mis-allocation there will hurt the budget the most. The expert then **locks in the cheapest route** in that high-regret row/column first. Repeating this principle yields an allocation that is *almost always* much closer to the true minimum transportation cost than naive methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** VAM does not greedily chase the absolute lowest cost in the *whole* table. It chases the lowest cost **within the most sensitive** row/column, where a wrong choice would be the most expensive. This is the essence of *opportunity-cost thinking* in OR.

## 1.3 Important Metrics and Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Typical Notation |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Penalty Cost** | Difference between the two smallest costs in a row/column | $P_i$ or $P_j$ |
| **Unit Cost** | Per-unit transportation cost $c_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ |
| **Supply** | Available units at origin $i$ | $s_i$ |
| **Demand** | Required units at destination $j$ | $d_j$ |
| **Allocation** | Units assigned to cell $(i,j)$ | $x_{ij}$ |
| **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** | An allocation satisfying all supply/demand | — |
| **m + n − 1 Rule** | Number of allocations needed for non-degeneracy | $\mathbf{6}$ for $3 \times 4$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The procedure of VAM can be deconstructed into **seven rigorous logical steps**. The pedagogical rationale for each is given alongside.

### Step 1 — Balance the Transportation Tableau
- **How:** Compute $\sum s_i$ and $\sum d_j$. If unequal, add a dummy row/column with zero cost to absorb the surplus.
- **Why:** VAM assumes a closed system; without balance, no feasible allocation can exist.

### Step 2 — Compute Row Penalties
- **How:** For each row $i$, find the two smallest costs, $c_{i}^{\min_1}$ and $c_{i}^{\min_2}$. Set $P_i^{\text{row}} = c_{i}^{\min_2} - c_{i}^{\min_1}$.
- **Why:** $P_i^{\text{row}}$ measures the price of *not* choosing the cheapest cell in that row. A large value warns of severe consequences.

### Step 3 — Compute Column Penalties
- **How:** Similarly, for each column $j$, compute $P_j^{\text{col}} = c_{j}^{\min_2} - c_{j}^{\min_1}$.
- **Why:** Same economic logic, applied column-wise.

### Step 4 — Identify the Maximum Penalty
- **How:** Let $P^{*} = \max\left(\{P_i^{\text{row}}\} \cup \{P_j^{\text{col}}\}\right)$.
- **Why:** The row/column with $P^{*}$ is the *most* vulnerable to a poor decision.

### Step 5 — Resolve Ties
- **How:** If $P^{*}$ occurs once, that row/column is chosen. If tied:
  1. Pick the one containing the **lowest unit cost** in its selected cell.
  2. If still tied, choose arbitrarily (write the assumption down for the examiner).
- **Why:** Prevents ambiguity; KTU examiners reward clearly stated tie-breaking.

### Step 6 — Allocate and Strike Out
- **How:** In the chosen row/column, identify the cell with the **minimum cost** $c_{ij}^{*}$. Set
$$x_{ij} = \min(s_i, d_j)$$
Then update $s_i \leftarrow s_i - x_{ij}$ and $d_j \leftarrow d_j - x_{ij}$. If $s_i = 0$, strike out row $i$; if $d_j = 0$, strike out column $j$.
- **Why:** Honours the "make-the-cheapest-move-now" intuition.

### Step 7 — Iterate or Terminate
- **How:** Recompute penalties for the reduced matrix. If a row or column is left with a single cell, its penalty is treated as **0** (or undefined, and the cell is auto-allocated). Stop when $m + n - 1$ allocations are made.
- **Why:** Each iteration shrinks the problem by at least one cell, guaranteeing termination in at most $mn$ steps.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Mathematical Form | Purpose |
|---|----------------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Row Penalty | $P_i^{\text{row}} = c_{i}^{\min_2} - c_{i}^{\min_1}$ | Measures cost of avoiding the cheapest cell in row $i$ |
| 2 | Column Penalty | $P_j^{\text{col}} = c_{j}^{\min_2} - c_{j}^{\min_1}$ | Measures cost of avoiding the cheapest cell in column $j$ |
| 3 | Selection Rule | $P^{*} = \max\left(\{P_i^{\text{row}}\} \cup \{P_j^{\text{col}}\}\right)$ | Identifies the most critical row/column |
| 4 | Allocation Rule | $x_{ij} = \min(s_i, d_j)$ | Maximum feasible quantity in the chosen cell |
| 5 | Total Transportation Cost | $Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij}\,x_{ij}$ | Objective function of the problem |
| 6 | Balance Condition | $\sum_{i=1}^{m} s_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} d_j$ | Necessary for a closed feasible solution |
| 7 | Non-Degeneracy Check | $\text{No. of allocations} = m + n - 1$ | Ensures a non-degenerate BFS |
| 8 | Degenerate Penalty | $P = 0$ when only one cell remains in a row/column | Default when second-minimum is undefined |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Pitfall:** When a row or column has **only one** unallocated cell, the penalty is conventionally set to **0**, *not* infinity. The cell is then auto-filled with the remaining supply/demand. KTU examiners frequently test this edge case.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering and Managerial Utility

- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** VAM is the first-cut heuristic in distribution-network design for FMCG, e-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart routing decisions), and cold-chain planning.
- **Manufacturing & Production Planning:** Assigning jobs to machines with different processing costs (a generalized assignment variant).
- **Defence & Disaster Logistics:** Optimal allocation of troops/relief materials from depots to demand zones with varied terrain costs.
- **Project Management:** Resource-to-task allocation where the cost matrix represents effort or money per unit.
- **Civil Engineering:** Routing of construction material (cement, steel) from multiple batching plants to multiple project sites with minimal haulage cost.

In practice, VAM gives an **IBFS that is frequently within 1–3% of the true optimal**, making it the *industry-preferred* starting point before running the MODI/Stepping-Stone optimality check.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Master Problem Statement (Used Throughout Section 3)

A pharmaceutical company has **3 warehouses** ($O_1, O_2, O_3$) supplying **4 hospitals** ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$). The per-unit shipping cost in rupees, supply in units, and demand in units are tabulated below.

| From \ To | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | Supply $s_i$ |
|-----------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:-------------:|
| $O_1$     | 6      | 4      | 1      | 5      | **14**       |
| $O_2$     | 8      | 9      | 3      | 7      | **16**       |
| $O_3$     | 4      | 6      | 2      | 8      | **16**       |
| Demand $d_j$ | **12** | **12** | **15** | **7**  | **46 / 46**  |

> **Balance Check:** $\sum s_i = 14 + 16 + 16 = 46$ and $\sum d_j = 12 + 12 + 15 + 7 = 46$. The problem is already balanced, so **no dummy row/column is required**.

## 3.2 Iteration 1 — First Penalty Computation

**Row Penalties** (smallest cost and second-smallest in each row):
- Row $O_1$: smallest $= 1$ (at $D_3$), second smallest $= 4$ (at $D_2$) → $P_{O_1}^{\text{row}} = 4 - 1 = 3$
- Row $O_2$: smallest $= 3$ (at $D_3$), second smallest $= 7$ (at $D_4$) → $P_{O_2}^{\text{row}} = 7 - 3 = 4$
- Row $O_3$: smallest $= 2$ (at $D_3$), second smallest $= 4$ (at $D_1$) → $P_{O_3}^{\text{row}} = 4 - 2 = 2$

**Column Penalties** (smallest cost and second-smallest in each column):
- Col $D_1$: smallest $= 4$ (at $O_3$), second smallest $= 6$ (at $O_1$) → $P_{D_1}^{\text{col}} = 6 - 4 = 2$
- Col $D_2$: smallest $= 4$ (at $O_1$), second smallest $= 6$ (at $O_3$) → $P_{D_2}^{\text{col}} = 6 - 4 = 2$
- Col $D_3$: smallest $= 1$ (at $O_1$), second smallest $= 2$ (at $O_3$) → $P_{D_3}^{\text{col}} = 2 - 1 = 1$
- Col $D_4$: smallest $= 5$ (at $O_1$), second smallest $= 7$ (at $O_2$) → $P_{D_4}^{\text{col}} = 7 - 5 = 2$

**Decision:** Maximum penalty is $P^{*} = 4$ attained uniquely in row $O_2$. The minimum cost in row $O_2$ is **$c_{23} = 3$**. Allocate $x_{23} = \min(16, 15) = 15$. Demand of $D_3$ is fully satisfied → **strike out column $D_3$**. New supply: $s_2 = 16 - 15 = 1$.

## 3.3 Iteration 2 — Reduced Matrix (Column $D_3$ Removed)

| From \ To | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_4$ | Supply $s_i$ |
|-----------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:-------------:|
| $O_1$     | 6      | 4      | 5      | **14**       |
| $O_2$     | 8      | 9      | 7      | **1**        |
| $O_3$     | 4      | 6      | 8      | **16**       |
| Demand $d_j$ | **12** | **12** | **7**  |               |

**Row Penalties:**
- $O_1$: $5 - 4 = 1$
- $O_2$: $8 - 7 = 1$
- $O_3$: $6 - 4 = 2$

**Column Penalties:**
- $D_1$: $6 - 4 = 2$
- $D_2$: $6 - 4 = 2$
- $D_4$: $7 - 5 = 2$

**Decision:** Maximum penalty is $2$, occurring in three rows/columns. By tie-breaking rule, select column $D_1$ (where the lowest cost within the cell is $c_{31} = 4$, the lowest among tied candidates). Allocate $x_{31} = \min(16, 12) = 12$. Column $D_1$ is satisfied → **strike out column $D_1$**. New supply: $s_3 = 16 - 12 = 4$.

## 3.4 Iteration 3 — Reduced Matrix (Column $D_1$ Removed)

| From \ To | $D_2$ | $D_4$ | Supply $s_i$ |
|-----------|:------:|:------:|:-------------:|
| $O_1$     | 4      | 5      | **14**       |
| $O_2$     | 9      | 7      | **1**        |
| $O_3$     | 6      | 8      | **4**        |
| Demand $d_j$ | **12** | **7**  |               |

**Row Penalties:**
- $O_1$: $5 - 4 = 1$
- $O_2$: $9 - 7 = 2$
- $O_3$: $8 - 6 = 2$

**Column Penalties:**
- $D_2$: $6 - 4 = 2$
- $D_4$: $7 - 5 = 2$

**Decision:** Maximum penalty is $2$ (multiple ties). Choose row $O_2$ (lowest minimum cost among tied choices is $c_{24} = 7$). Allocate $x_{24} = \min(1, 7) = 1$. Row $O_2$ is exhausted → **strike out row $O_2$**. New demand: $d_4 = 7 - 1 = 6$.

## 3.5 Iteration 4 — Reduced Matrix (Row $O_2$ Removed)

| From \ To | $D_2$ | $D_4$ | Supply $s_i$ |
|-----------|:------:|:------:|:-------------:|
| $O_1$     | 4      | 5      | **14**       |
| $O_3$     | 6      | 8      | **4**        |
| Demand $d_j$ | **12** | **6**  |               |

**Row Penalties:**
- $O_1$: $5 - 4 = 1$
- $O_3$: $8 - 6 = 2$

**Column Penalties:**
- $D_2$: $6 - 4 = 2$
- $D_4$: $8 - 5 = 3$

**Decision:** Maximum penalty is $P^{*} = 3$ at column $D_4$. Minimum cost in $D_4$ is $c_{14} = 5$. Allocate $x_{14} = \min(14, 6) = 6$. Column $D_4$ is satisfied → **strike out column $D_4$**. New supply: $s_1 = 14 - 6 = 8$.

## 3.6 Iteration 5 — Final Forced Allocation (Deterministic Matrix)

After the last strike-out, only the column $D_2$ remains. With supply $s_1 = 8, s_3 = 4$ and demand $d_2 = 12$:

$$x_{12} = \min(8, 12) = 8, \quad x_{32} = \min(4, 12-8) = 4$$

## 3.7 Final Allocation Matrix and Total Cost

| From \ To | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | Supply |
|-----------|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:------:|
| $O_1$     | —     | **8** (₹4)  | —     | **6** (₹5)  | 14     |
| $O_2$     | —     | —     | **15** (₹3) | **1** (₹7)  | 16     |
| $O_3$     | **12** (₹4) | **4** (₹6)  | —     | —     | 16     |
| Demand    | 12    | 12    | 15    | 7     |        |

**Total Transportation Cost Computation:**

$$Z = \sum_{i,j} c_{ij}\,x_{ij}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= (8 \times 4) + (6 \times 5) + (15 \times 3) + (1 \times 7) + (12 \times 4) + (4 \times 6) \\
&= 32 + 30 + 45 + 7 + 48 + 24 \\
&= \mathbf{186 \; \text{rupees}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Non-Degeneracy Check:**

$$\text{No. of allocations} = 6, \quad m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6 \;\; \checkmark$$

The IBFS is **non-degenerate** and is now ready for the MODI/Stepping-Stone optimality test (covered in Module 3).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of Vogel's Approximation Method

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the full execution pipeline of VAM, including penalty computation, tie-breaking, allocation, and termination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START VAM ALGORITHM] --> B[Read Cost Matrix, Supply Vector, Demand Vector]
    B --> C{Balanced? Sum Supply equals Sum Demand}
    C -->|No| D[Add Dummy Row or Column with Zero Cost]
    C -->|Yes| E[Proceed to Penalty Computation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Row Penalties: Two Smallest Costs Difference]
    E --> G[Compute Column Penalties: Two Smallest Costs Difference]
    F --> H[Find Maximum Penalty P Star]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Multiple Ties for P Star}
    I -->|Yes| J[Apply Tie Break Rule: Lowest Minimum Cost in Cell]
    I -->|No| K[Select Unique Row or Column with P Star]
    J --> L[Pick Cell with Minimum Unit Cost in Selected Line]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Allocate xij = min Supply, Demand]
    M --> N[Update Supply: si minus xij]
    M --> O[Update Demand: dj minus xij]
    N --> P{Supply or Demand becomes Zero}
    O --> P
    P -->|Yes| Q[Strike Out the Exhausted Row or Column]
    P -->|No| R[Recompute Penalties for Reduced Matrix]
    Q --> R
    R --> S{All Supply and Demand Satisfied}
    S -->|No| E
    S -->|Yes| T[Check Non Degeneracy: Allocations equals m plus n minus 1]
    T --> U[Compute Total Cost Z = sum cij xij]
    U --> V[END: IBFS READY FOR OPTIMALITY TEST]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of VAM

In cases where the flowchart above is rendered as a static image, the following block-level architecture captures the data flow between the four key sub-modules of VAM: **Penalty Engine**, **Tie-Break Resolver**, **Allocation Engine**, and **Matrix Reducer**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Cost Matrix C and Vectors S and D] --> PENG[Penalty Engine Module]
    PENG -->|Row Penalties PR| TIE[Tie Break Resolver Module]
    PENG -->|Column Penalties PC| TIE
    TIE -->|Selected Line and Cell| AENG[Allocation Engine Module]
    AENG -->|Allocation xij| MRED[Matrix Reducer Module]
    MRED -->|Updated Sub Problem| PENG
    AENG -->|Final Allocations| COST[Cost Aggregator Module]
    COST -->|Total Z and IBFS| OUTPUT[IBFS Output for Optimality Test]
```

**Reading the Architecture:**
- The **Penalty Engine** is the analytical brain; it constantly cycles.
- The **Tie-Break Resolver** is the policy layer ensuring deterministic choice.
- The **Allocation Engine** is the executive layer performing the actual supply-demand matching.
- The **Matrix Reducer** dynamically shrinks the problem domain after each strike-out.
- The loop closes until a termination signal is emitted, after which the IBFS is dispatched to the optimality test module.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the balance check** before penalty computation. Adding a dummy row/column is mandatory for unbalanced problems; missing it leads to a 1–2 mark penalty.
> 2. **Using $\min$ instead of second-min for penalty** — many students wrongly take the smallest cost as the penalty. The correct formula is $c_{\min_2} - c_{\min_1}$, not $c_{\min_1}$.
> 3. **Ignoring tie-breaking rules** — when two or more penalties are equal, the examiner expects a stated rule. Arbitrary selection without justification may attract a half-mark deduction.
> 4. **Degeneracy** — failing to verify $m + n - 1$ allocations. A degenerate BFS requires an $\varepsilon$-perturbation that is **not** part of VAM but is often tested as a follow-up.
> 5. **Final cost miscalculation** — arithmetic mistakes in the last summation are the single most common reason students lose 1 mark. Always re-add the products in a separate column.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Part A]`
**Define the term "Penalty Cost" in Vogel's Approximation Method. Why is it called an "opportunity cost"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Penalty cost in VAM is defined as the **difference between the two smallest transportation costs** in a given row or column of the cost matrix. It is mathematically expressed as
$$P = c_{\min_2} - c_{\min_1}$$
It is called an *opportunity cost* because it represents the **extra cost that would be incurred** if we fail to allocate to the cheapest cell in that row/column and are forced to use the second-cheapest cell instead. A high penalty indicates a large opportunity cost, signalling that the row/column deserves immediate and careful allocation attention.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Part A]`
**State any three advantages of Vogel's Approximation Method over the North-West Corner Method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Better Quality of IBFS:** VAM produces an initial solution that is much closer to the optimal solution than the one produced by the North-West Corner Method, often reducing the number of MODI iterations required.
2. **Cost-Sensitive Decisions:** Unlike NWCM (which is completely blind to costs), VAM explicitly considers the unit cost matrix and the relative price penalties, leading to economically superior allocations.
3. **Lower Final Transportation Cost:** Empirically, the IBFS obtained via VAM is typically within 1–3% of the true optimum, whereas NWCM can deviate by 10–30% on average, leading to significant cost savings in real-world logistics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2, Part B, Q1]`

A firm has three factories $F_1, F_2, F_3$ supplying four warehouses $W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4$. The per-unit cost matrix (in ₹), supply, and demand are given below.

| From \ To | $W_1$ | $W_2$ | $W_3$ | $W_4$ | Supply |
|-----------|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:------:|
| $F_1$     | 6     | 4     | 1     | 5     | **14** |
| $F_2$     | 8     | 9     | 3     | 7     | **16** |
| $F_3$     | 4     | 6     | 2     | 8     | **16** |
| Demand    | **12** | **12** | **15** | **7** | **46** |

#### (a) Find the Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS) using Vogel's Approximation Method. State the total transportation cost. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Iteration 1:**
Row Penalties: $P_{F_1} = 4 - 1 = 3$, $P_{F_2} = 7 - 3 = 4$, $P_{F_3} = 4 - 2 = 2$.
Column Penalties: $P_{W_1} = 6 - 4 = 2$, $P_{W_2} = 6 - 4 = 2$, $P_{W_3} = 2 - 1 = 1$, $P_{W_4} = 7 - 5 = 2$.
Maximum penalty = $4$ at row $F_2$. Minimum cost in $F_2$ is $c_{23} = 3$. Allocate $x_{23} = \min(16, 15) = 15$. Strike out $W_3$. New $s_2 = 1$.

**Iteration 2:**
Row Penalties: $P_{F_1} = 1$, $P_{F_2} = 1$, $P_{F_3} = 2$. Column Penalties: $P_{W_1} = 2$, $P_{W_2} = 2$, $P_{W_4} = 2$.
Maximum penalty = $2$ (tie). Select $W_1$ (lowest min cost = $c_{31} = 4$). Allocate $x_{31} = \min(16, 12) = 12$. Strike out $W_1$. New $s_3 = 4$.

**Iteration 3:**
Row Penalties: $P_{F_1} = 1$, $P_{F_2} = 2$, $P_{F_3} = 2$. Column Penalties: $P_{W_2} = 2$, $P_{W_4} = 2$.
Maximum penalty = $2$ (tie). Select $F_2$ (lowest min cost = $c_{24} = 7$). Allocate $x_{24} = \min(1, 7) = 1$. Strike out $F_2$. New $d_4 = 6$.

**Iteration 4:**
Row Penalties: $P_{F_1} = 1$, $P_{F_3} = 2$. Column Penalties: $P_{W_2} = 2$, $P_{W_4} = 3$.
Maximum penalty = $3$ at $W_4$. Minimum cost in $W_4$ is $c_{14} = 5$. Allocate $x_{14} = \min(14, 6) = 6$. Strike out $W_4$. New $s_1 = 8$.

**Iteration 5 (Forced):**
Only $W_2$ remains. Allocate $x_{12} = 8$ and $x_{32} = 4$.

**Final Allocations & Total Cost:**

| Cell | $x_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}\,x_{ij}$ |
|------|:--------:|:--------:|:----------------:|
| $(F_1, W_2)$ | 8  | 4 | 32 |
| $(F_1, W_4)$ | 6  | 5 | 30 |
| $(F_2, W_3)$ | 15 | 3 | 45 |
| $(F_2, W_4)$ | 1  | 7 | 7  |
| $(F_3, W_1)$ | 12 | 4 | 48 |
| $(F_3, W_2)$ | 4  | 6 | 24 |
| **Total**    |    |    | **₹186** |

Allocations $= 6 = m + n - 1$ ✓ (Non-degenerate IBFS).

#### (b) Solve the same problem using the **Least Cost Method (LCM)** and compare the total cost with the VAM solution. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**LCM Algorithm Recap:** Repeatedly allocate to the cell with the **globally lowest** unit cost in the entire table.

| Step | Cell chosen | $c_{ij}$ | $x_{ij}$ | Reason for closure |
|------|-------------|:--------:|:--------:|--------------------|
| 1 | $(F_1, W_3)$ | **1** | 14 | $W_3$ partially met (1 left, but $F_1$ exhausted) → $F_1$ struck |
| 2 | $(F_3, W_3)$ | 2 | 1  | $W_3$ met → $W_3$ struck |
| 3 | $(F_3, W_1)$ | 4 | 11 | $F_3$ exhausted → $F_3$ struck |
| 4 | $(F_2, W_4)$ | 7 | 1  | $F_2$ exhausted → $F_2$ struck |
| 5 | Forced: $(F_1, W_4)$ struck; only $W_2$ left | — | — | — |
| 6 | $W_1 = 1, W_2 = 12, W_4 = 6$ left for $F_2$ — already struck; row $F_1$ done. | | | |

After step 1 ($F_1$ exhausted with $x_{13} = 14$), the demand $W_3 = 1$ is left. The cheapest cell now is $(F_3, W_3)$ with cost $2$. Allocate $x_{33} = 1$, strike $W_3$.

The cheapest remaining is $(F_3, W_1)$ with cost $4$. Allocate $x_{31} = 11$ (since $F_3$ has 16 supply, 1 used, 15 left but $W_1$ has 12 demand and $F_2$ still has $c_{21} = 8$ which is higher). Actually $x_{31} = \min(15, 12) = 12$, strike $W_1$. New $s_3 = 3$.

Cheapest remaining: $(F_2, W_4) = 7$. Allocate $x_{24} = \min(16, 7) = 7$, strike $W_4$. New $s_2 = 9$.

Forced: $x_{22} = 9, x_{32} = 3$.

**LCM Total Cost:**

$$Z_{\text{LCM}} = (14 \times 1) + (1 \times 2) + (12 \times 4) + (7 \times 7) + (9 \times 9) + (3 \times 6)$$

$$Z_{\text{LCM}} = 14 + 2 + 48 + 49 + 81 + 18 = 212 \text{ rupees}$$

**Comparison:**

$$Z_{\text{LCM}} - Z_{\text{VAM}} = 212 - 186 = 26 \text{ rupees}$$

VAM yields an IBFS that is **₹26 cheaper** than LCM on the same problem, demonstrating the practical superiority of VAM as a heuristic.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2, Part B, Q2]`

A textile company has two plants $P_1$ and $P_2$ producing a fabric, and three retail outlets $R_1, R_2, R_3$. The per-unit profit (in ₹) on selling from plant to outlet is given in the **profit matrix** below. (Profits: $P_1 = [50, 40, 60]$; $P_2 = [40, 30, 35]$. Supply: $P_1 = 100$ units, $P_2 = 200$ units. Demand: $R_1 = 80$, $R_2 = 120$, $R_3 = 100$ units.)

#### (a) Convert this maximization problem to a minimization one, balance the supply and demand, and hence find the IBFS using VAM. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Maximization to Minimization:**
Find the largest profit: $L = 60$. Construct the **opportunity cost matrix** $c_{ij}' = L - p_{ij}$:

$$c' = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 0 \\ 20 & 30 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Balance Check:**
$\sum s_i = 100 + 200 = 300$. $\sum d_j = 80 + 120 + 100 = 300$. **Balanced** — no dummy needed.

**Step 3 — Apply VAM on the Cost Matrix:**

**Iteration 1:**
Row Penalties: $P_{P_1} = 10 - 0 = 10$, $P_{P_2} = 25 - 20 = 5$.
Column Penalties: $P_{R_1} = 10$, $P_{R_2} = 10$, $P_{R_3} = 25$.
Max penalty = $25$ at $R_3$. Min cost in $R_3$ is $c_{13}' = 0$. Allocate $x_{13} = \min(100, 100) = 100$. Both row $P_1$ and column $R_3$ are exhausted.

**Iteration 2:**
Only $P_2$ vs $R_1, R_2$ left. For $P_2$, only one cell column $R_1$ has cost $20$ and $R_2$ has cost $30$. Penalty for $P_2$ = $0$ (single row). Columns: $R_1$ has 20, $R_2$ has 30. Penalty = $10$ for $R_1$ and undefined for $R_2$.
Choose $R_1$. Allocate $x_{21} = \min(200, 80) = 80$. Strike $R_1$. New $s_2 = 120$.

**Iteration 3 (Forced):**
$x_{22} = 120$.

**Total Opportunity Cost:**

$$Z' = (100 \times 0) + (80 \times 20) + (120 \times 30) = 0 + 1600 + 3600 = 5200$$

**Maximum Profit:**

$$Z = L \cdot \sum x_{ij} - Z' = 60 \times 300 - 5200 = 18000 - 5200 = \mathbf{₹12{,}800}$$

Allocations: $(P_1, R_3) = 100$, $(P_2, R_1) = 80$, $(P_2, R_2) = 120$. Number of allocations = $3 = m + n - 1 = 2 + 3 - 1$ ✓.

#### (b) If a penalty of ₹5 per unit is levied on every unit shipped from $P_2$ (regardless of destination), rework the VAM and find the new optimal profit. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Adjusted Cost Matrix:**
Add ₹5 to all costs in row $P_2$:

$$c'_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 0 \\ 25 & 35 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Re-apply VAM:**

**Iteration 1:**
Row Penalties: $P_{P_1} = 10$, $P_{P_2} = 30 - 25 = 5$.
Column Penalties: $P_{R_1} = 15$, $P_{R_2} = 15$, $P_{R_3} = 30$.
Max penalty = $30$ at $R_3$. Min cost in $R_3$ = $c_{13}' = 0$. Allocate $x_{13} = 100$. Both struck.

**Iteration 2:**
Row $P_2$ vs $R_1, R_2$. Penalties: $P_{P_2} = 35 - 25 = 10$, $P_{R_1} = $ undefined (only one col cell), $P_{R_2} = $ undefined. Max = $10$ at $P_2$. Min cost in $P_2$ = $c_{21}' = 25$. Allocate $x_{21} = 80$. Strike $R_1$. New $s_2 = 120$.

**Iteration 3:** $x_{22} = 120$.

**New Opportunity Cost:**

$$Z'_{\text{new}} = (100 \times 0) + (80 \times 25) + (120 \times 35) = 0 + 2000 + 4200 = 6200$$

**New Maximum Profit:**

$$Z_{\text{new}} = 60 \times 300 - 6200 = 18000 - 6200 = \mathbf{₹11{,}800}$$

**Comparison:** The penalty of ₹5 per unit from $P_2$ reduces profit by exactly $₹(12800 - 11800) = ₹1000$, which equals $5 \times 200 = ₹1000$ (since $P_2$ ships all 200 units). This is a self-consistency sanity check.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** VAM = an IBFS-finding heuristic based on **opportunity cost (penalty)** of rows and columns.
- **Penalty Formula:** $P = c_{\min_2} - c_{\min_1}$ (never just $c_{\min_1}$).
- **Tie-Breaking:** Choose the row/column where the selected cell has the **lowest minimum cost**; if still tied, choose arbitrarily and **state the assumption**.
- **Allocation Rule:** $x_{ij} = \min(s_i, d_j)$.
- **Strike-Out Rule:** If $s_i = 0$, strike the row; if $d_j = 0$, strike the column. **Do not strike both simultaneously** unless both happen.
- **Forced Allocations:** When only one row/column remains, allocate deterministically; penalty is taken as $0$.
- **Balance First:** Always check $\sum s_i = \sum d_j$ before starting. Add a dummy with **zero cost** if unbalanced.
- **Maximization Problems:** Convert to minimization via $c_{ij}' = L - p_{ij}$ (where $L$ = largest profit), then apply VAM normally.
- **Penalty / Taxation Surcharges:** Add a flat per-unit cost to the appropriate row/column **before** penalty computation.
- **Non-Degeneracy:** Verify allocations $= m + n - 1$ at the end. If $< m + n - 1$, the BFS is **degenerate** and needs an $\varepsilon$-perturbation (typically a follow-up question).
- **Total Cost:** $Z = \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ — compute last, double-check the arithmetic.
- **Why VAM is Preferred:** IBFS is typically within 1–3% of optimum, vastly reducing the number of MODI iterations required in Module 3.
- **Examiner's Pet Topics:** (i) Tie-breaking justification, (ii) maximization-to-minimization conversion, (iii) dummy row/column introduction, (iv) degeneracy detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always present your VAM iterations in a **clean tabular format** with row and column penalties shown explicitly in the same table. Examiners award **partial marks for method clarity** even if the arithmetic contains a minor slip. State tie-breaking assumptions in **bold** or as a separate note — it is worth ½ to 1 mark consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignment Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assignment Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assignment Problem (AP):** A special class of Linear Programming Problem (LPP) and a degenerate case of the Transportation Problem where the objective is to assign *n* jobs/tasks to *n* machines/workers on a **one-to-one basis** such that the total cost (or time/profit) is **optimized** (minimized or maximized), subject to the constraint that exactly **one job is assigned to exactly one machine**, and each machine receives **exactly one job**.

**Key Characteristics of an Assignment Problem:**
- It is a **square matrix** problem where the number of rows equals the number of columns.
- It is a **bijective mapping** — a permutation of *n* elements onto *n* positions.
- The cost matrix $C = [c_{ij}]$ is given, where $c_{ij}$ denotes the cost of assigning $i^{th}$ job to $j^{th}$ worker.
- It is a **0-1 assignment** — either assign ($x_{ij} = 1$) or do not assign ($x_{ij} = 0$).

**Mathematical Formulation (LPP Form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } Z &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij} \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} &= 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n \quad \text{(Each job is assigned exactly once)} \\
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} &= 1, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, n \quad \text{(Each worker gets exactly one job)} \\
x_{ij} &\in \{0, 1\}, \quad \forall i, j
\end{aligned}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Manager's Dilemma"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a Project Manager with **4 engineers** (Anu, Bala, Chitra, Deepak) and **4 distinct coding tasks** (Task A, B, C, D). The manager has a performance score for each engineer on each task. The manager must assign **each engineer to exactly one task** and **each task to exactly one engineer** so that the **total project efficiency is maximized** (or total time is minimized). This perfect pairing is the *Assignment Problem*.
>
> The challenge: trying all $4! = 24$ pairings manually is fine, but for $n=10$, there are $10! = 3,628,800$ possibilities — clearly infeasible by brute force. Hence, we need an elegant, polynomial-time algorithm: the **Hungarian Method** (developed by Harold W. Kuhn in 1955, named after König and Egerváry, two Hungarian mathematicians).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cost Matrix** | An $n \times n$ matrix where entry $c_{ij}$ is the cost of assigning job $i$ to worker $j$. |
| **Balanced AP** | An AP where the number of rows equals the number of columns. |
| **Unbalanced AP** | An AP where rows $\neq$ columns; solved by adding **dummy rows/columns** with **zero cost**. |
| **Prohibited Assignment** | An assignment that is not feasible; represented by **$\infty$** (or a very large number M). |
| **Optimal Assignment** | A set of $n$ independent (no two sharing a row or column) zeros in the reduced cost matrix. |
| **Hungarian Method** | The classical O($n^3$) algorithm for solving AP, based on the principle of *opportunity cost*. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Hungarian Method Works

The Hungarian Method is rooted in a powerful principle: **adding or subtracting a constant from any row or column does not change the optimal solution**. It only changes the *numerical value* of the objective function, not the *assignment itself*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem of Assignment Problem:**
> If a constant $k$ is added to (or subtracted from) every element of a particular row (or column) of the cost matrix $C$, then an optimal assignment for the new cost matrix is also an optimal assignment for the original cost matrix.

This theorem allows us to create **zeros** in the matrix — because a "zero" in the cost matrix represents the *most preferred (cheapest)* cell in that row/column. Once we have enough zeros arranged such that we can pick *n* independent zeros (one in each row and each column), we have found the **optimal assignment**.

### 2.2 The Hungarian Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Balance the Matrix**
- If the matrix is not square ($m \neq n$), add dummy rows or columns with cost = 0 (for minimization) to make it square.
- If a particular assignment is prohibited, replace that cell with $\infty$ (a very large number, typically denoted as M).

**Step 2: Row Reduction**
- For each row, find the minimum element in that row.
- Subtract this minimum from **every element of that row**.
- Result: Each row will contain at least one zero.

**Step 3: Column Reduction**
- For each column, find the minimum element in that column.
- Subtract this minimum from **every element of that column**.
- Result: Each column will also contain at least one zero.

**Step 4: Cover All Zeros with Minimum Number of Lines**
- Draw the **minimum number of horizontal and vertical lines** required to cover **all zeros** in the matrix.
- If the number of lines drawn equals $n$, then an **optimal assignment exists** among the zeros. Go to Step 6.
- If the number of lines is **less than $n$**, proceed to Step 5.

**Step 5: Modify the Matrix (König's Method)**
- Find the **smallest uncovered element** (say, $k$) in the matrix.
- Subtract $k$ from **all uncovered elements**.
- Add $k$ to **all elements at the intersection** of two covered lines.
- Leave the other covered elements **unchanged**.
- Return to Step 4.

**Step 6: Make the Assignment**
- Starting from rows (or columns) with a **single zero**, assign the job to that worker.
- **Strike out** the assigned row and column.
- Continue until all jobs are assigned.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes all key formulas, rules, and conditions for the Assignment Problem.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Objective Function (Min)** | $Z_{min} = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ | Minimize total assignment cost. |
| 2 | **Objective Function (Max)** | Convert to min: $c'_{ij} = M - c_{ij}$, where $M = \max(c_{ij})$ | Handle maximization problems. |
| 3 | **Row Reduction** | $c'_{ij} = c_{ij} - \min_{j}(c_{ij})$ for each row $i$ | Create at least one zero per row. |
| 4 | **Column Reduction** | $c''_{ij} = c'_{ij} - \min_{i}(c'_{ij})$ for each column $j$ | Create at least one zero per column. |
| 5 | **Modification Constant** | $k = \min(\text{uncovered elements})$ | Used when lines $\lt n$. |
| 6 | **Prohibited Cell** | $c_{ij} = \infty$ or M | Represents infeasible assignment. |
| 7 | **Dummy Cell Cost** | $c_{ij} = 0$ | Used to balance an unbalanced matrix. |
| 8 | **Optimality Condition** | Minimum lines to cover all zeros $= n$ | Test for optimality. |
| 9 | **Number of Possible Assignments** | $n!$ | For $n=10$, this is $3.6$ million. |
| 10 | **Time Complexity** | $O(n^3)$ | Hungarian Method efficiency. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Planning:** Assigning jobs to machines on a factory shop floor.
- **Crew Scheduling:** Airlines assigning pilots/flight attendants to flights.
- **Vehicle Routing:** Allocating delivery vehicles to delivery routes.
- **Project Management:** Allocating teams to project modules.
- **Supply Chain:** Allocating warehouses to distribution centers.
- **Task Scheduling in OS:** Assigning CPU-bound vs. I/O-bound processes to cores.
- **VLSI Design:** Assigning logic gates to physical chip locations.
