topics.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm – The Minimum Spanning Tree Problem – The Maximum Flow Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dijkstra's Algorithm – The Minimum Spanning Tree Problem – The Maximum Flow Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3: GRAPH-BASED OPTIMIZATION ALGORITHMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

This module introduces three foundational graph-theoretic algorithms that solve real-world network optimization problems — the **Shortest Path Problem (Dijkstra's)**, the **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** problem, and the **Maximum Flow** problem. These are classified as *Network Optimization Models* in Operations Research.

---

### 1.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm (Single-Source Shortest Path)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Dijkstra's Algorithm is a greedy algorithm that finds the shortest path from a designated source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. The algorithm maintains a set of visited (permanent) vertices and iteratively selects the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance, updating the distances of its adjacent vertices.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are standing at a metro station (the **source node**) and you want to find the **cheapest ticket** (shortest distance) to every other station on the map. You are only allowed to travel via roads whose lengths are non-negative (no negative-length roads exist in real life). At each step, you mark the *nearest* unvisited station as "explored," and then check whether going *through* this newly explored station can offer a cheaper route to its neighbours. This greedy "nearest-first" exploration guarantees the global optimum because once a station is marked, no future path can be shorter (since all edges are non-negative).

**Standard Metric Used:** Path length is typically measured in **units of distance, time, or cost** — collectively called the **weight** $w(u,v)$ of edge $(u,v)$.

---

### 1.2 Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *A Spanning Tree of a connected, undirected, weighted graph is a subgraph that contains all the vertices of the original graph and is itself a tree (i.e., it is connected and has no cycles). Among all such spanning trees, the Minimum Spanning Tree is the one whose total edge weight is the minimum possible.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a township electrical company that wants to lay cables to connect **10 houses** to the main grid. It does **not** need to connect every house to *every* other house — that would create redundant, wasteful loops. It only needs exactly the **minimum number of cables** that keeps the whole network connected, with no loops, choosing the **cheapest possible set of cables**. The result is a tree structure where the total cable cost is minimized.

**Key Structural Property:** A spanning tree on $n$ vertices has **exactly $n-1$ edges**.

---

### 1.3 Maximum Flow Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *The Maximum Flow Problem seeks the maximum possible flow of a commodity (e.g., water, electricity, data, vehicles) from a designated source node $s$ to a designated sink node $t$ through a directed network, subject to the capacity constraints of each arc and the flow conservation constraints at every intermediate (transshipment) node.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a city water pipeline network with a **reservoir (source)** at one end and a **town (sink)** at the other. The pipes have different diameters, which means each pipe can carry only a limited amount of water per minute (its **capacity**). The question is: *What is the maximum amount of water that can be delivered from the reservoir to the town per minute, given the pipe capacities?* The answer is the **Maximum Flow**, and the bottleneck combination of pipes that limits this flow is the **Min-Cut**.

**Standard Metrics Used:**
- **Capacity** $c_{ij}$ — measured in **units per time** (e.g., liters/min, Mbps, vehicles/hr).
- **Flow** $f_{ij}$ — same units as capacity.
- **Cut capacity** — sum of capacities crossing a cut.

---

### 1.4 Why These Three Problems Belong Together

All three problems operate on a **graph** $G = (V, E)$ but optimize different objectives:

| Problem | Objective | Graph Type | Edge Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra | Minimize path length (cost) | Directed/Undirected | Non-negative weights |
| MST | Minimize total spanning cost | Undirected, connected | Positive weights |
| Max Flow | Maximize throughput | Directed | Capacity limits |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** These three problems are tested as independent 7-mark or 14-mark questions. The Maximum Flow Problem frequently appears with the *Min-Cut Max-Flow Theorem* as a paired sub-question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Theoretical Breakdown

**Input:** A weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights and a source vertex $s$.
**Output:** The shortest distance $d[v]$ from $s$ to every vertex $v \in V$, plus the predecessor tree $\pi[v]$.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Initialize** the distance to the source as $0$, and the distance to every other vertex as $\infty$ (infinity).
2. Mark all vertices as **unvisited**. Create a set $Q$ containing all unvisited vertices.
3. While $Q$ is not empty:
   - (a) Select the unvisited vertex $u$ with the **smallest tentative distance** $d[u]$. This is the "greedy choice."
   - (b) Mark $u$ as visited (remove from $Q$).
   - (c) For each **neighbour** $v$ of $u$ that is still in $Q$, perform **relaxation**:

$$d[v] = \min\bigl(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u,v)\bigr)$$

4. Terminate when all vertices are visited (or destination reached in early-exit variants).

**Why Greedy Works Here:** Once a vertex is finalized, no alternative path can be shorter because all remaining edge weights are non-negative — the cheapest path to $u$ will only grow (or stay equal) as we extend it.

**Complexity:**
- With a simple array: $O(|V|^2)$
- With a min-heap (priority queue): $O((|V| + |E|) \log |V|)$

**Limitations:**
- Fails on graphs with **negative edge weights** (use Bellman-Ford instead).
- Cannot handle graphs whose weights change dynamically (re-runs are required).

---

### 2.2 Minimum Spanning Tree — Theoretical Breakdown

Two classical algorithms exist: **Prim's Algorithm** and **Kruskal's Algorithm**. Both are greedy.

#### 2.2.1 Prim's Algorithm

1. Start from an arbitrary root vertex $r$. Initialize a tree $T = \{r\}$.
2. At every step, look at all edges $(u, v)$ such that $u \in T$ and $v \notin T$. Pick the **minimum-weight** such edge and add it (along with $v$) to $T$.
3. Repeat until $|T| = |V|$.

**Complexity:** $O(|E| \log |V|)$ with a binary heap.

#### 2.2.2 Kruskal's Algorithm

1. Sort all edges in non-decreasing order of weight.
2. Initialize each vertex as its own disjoint-set (using Union-Find).
3. Iterate through the sorted edges. For each edge $(u, v)$:
   - If $u$ and $v$ are in **different components**, add the edge to the MST and **union** their sets.
   - Otherwise, **skip** the edge (it would form a cycle).
4. Stop when exactly $|V| - 1$ edges have been selected.

**Complexity:** $O(|E| \log |E|)$ dominated by the sort.

**Why Greedy Works Here:** The **Cut Property** states that for any cut of the graph, the minimum-weight edge crossing that cut belongs to *some* MST. Greedy algorithms exploit this property iteratively.

---

### 2.3 Maximum Flow — Theoretical Breakdown

**Notation:**
- $c_{ij}$ — capacity of arc $(i, j)$.
- $f_{ij}$ — flow assigned to arc $(i, j)$.
- $s$ — source, $t$ — sink.

**Two Fundamental Constraints:**

**Capacity Constraint (Arc Capacity):**
$$0 \le f_{ij} \le c_{ij} \quad \forall (i,j) \in E$$

**Flow Conservation (at transshipment nodes):**
$$\sum_{j} f_{ji} \;=\; \sum_{j} f_{ij} \quad \forall\, i \ne s, t$$

**Objective — Maximize the total outflow from the source:**
$$\max\, |F| \;=\; \sum_{j} f_{sj} \;-\; \sum_{j} f_{js}$$

#### 2.3.1 Ford–Fulkerson Method (Residual Network Approach)

1. Start with $f_{ij} = 0$ for all arcs.
2. While there exists an **augmenting path** $P$ from $s$ to $t$ in the **residual network** $G_f$:
   - Compute the **bottleneck** $b = \min\{c_{ij} - f_{ij} : (i,j) \in P\}$.
   - **Augment** $f_{ij} \leftarrow f_{ij} + b$ for forward arcs in $P$.
   - **Reduce** $f_{ji} \leftarrow f_{ji} - b$ for backward arcs in $P$.
3. When no augmenting path exists, the flow is **maximum**.

**Min-Cut Max-Flow Theorem:**
$$|F_{\max}| \;=\; \min_{(S,T)}\, C(S,T) \;=\; \min_{(S,T)} \sum_{i \in S,\ j \in T} c_{ij}$$

This is the *dual* relationship that gives the algorithm its stopping criterion.

**Complexity:** Depends on augmentation method.
- Standard Ford–Fulkerson: $O(|F_{\max}| \cdot |E|)$.
- **Edmonds–Karp** (BFS for shortest augmenting path): $O(|V| \cdot |E|^2)$.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Constraint / Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| Dijkstra Relaxation | $d[v] = \min(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u,v))$ | $w(u,v) \ge 0$ |
| Source Initialisation | $d[s] = 0,\ \forall v \ne s: d[v] = \infty$ | Algorithm start |
| MST Edge Count | $|E_{\text{MST}}| = \vert V \vert - 1$ | Connected, undirected |
| MST Total Cost | $W_{\text{MST}} = \sum_{(u,v)\in T} w(u,v)$ | Minimize over all spanning trees |
| Capacity Constraint | $0 \le f_{ij} \le c_{ij}$ | Every arc |
| Flow Conservation | $\sum_j f_{ji} = \sum_j f_{ij}$ | Intermediate nodes only |
| Net Flow Value | $\vert F \vert = \sum_j f_{sj} - \sum_j f_{js}$ | Source node balance |
| Bottleneck on Path | $b = \min\{c_{ij} - f_{ij} : (i,j) \in P\}$ | Augmenting path $P$ |
| Min-Cut Max-Flow | $\vert F_{\max} \vert = \min_{(S,T)} C(S,T)$ | Dual relationship |
| Cut Capacity | $C(S,T) = \sum_{i \in S,\, j \in T} c_{ij}$ | $s \in S,\ t \in T$ |

**Real-World Engineering Applications:**

- **Dijkstra's:** GPS navigation, network routing (OSPF in computer networks), robotics path planning.
- **MST:** LAN cable layout, VLSI circuit design, cluster analysis, image segmentation.
- **Maximum Flow:** Pipeline transport, internet bandwidth, traffic engineering, project scheduling (PERT/CPM reduction).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains three fully worked numerical examples, one for each sub-topic, in the precise KTU step-by-step tabular format.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dijkstra's Algorithm

**Problem:** Find the shortest path from vertex $A$ to all other vertices for the graph below.

**Edge List (directed, weights shown):**
- $A \to B = 4,\ A \to C = 2$
- $B \to C = 1,\ B \to D = 5$
- $C \to B = 3,\ C \to D = 8$
- $D \to E = 2$
- $E \to D = 6$ (irrelevant — out of our target)

**Source:** $A$.  **Target reachability:** $B, C, D, E$.

**Initialisation Table:**

| Vertex | $d[\cdot]$ | Visited? | Predecessor $\pi[\cdot]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $0$ | No | — |
| $B$ | $\infty$ | No | — |
| $C$ | $\infty$ | No | — |
| $D$ | $\infty$ | No | — |
| $E$ | $\infty$ | No | — |

**Iteration 1:** Pick the unvisited vertex with minimum $d$ → **$A$** ($d=0$). Mark visited. Relax its outgoing edges:

$$d[B] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 4) = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \pi[B] = A$$
$$d[C] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 2) = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \pi[C] = A$$

| Vertex | $d[\cdot]$ | Visited? | $\pi$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $0$ | **Yes** | — |
| $B$ | $4$ | No | $A$ |
| $C$ | $2$ | No | $A$ |
| $D$ | $\infty$ | No | — |
| $E$ | $\infty$ | No | — |

**Iteration 2:** Minimum unvisited → **$C$** ($d=2$). Mark visited. Relax its outgoing edges ($C \to B = 3$, $C \to D = 8$):

$$d[B] = \min(4,\ 2 + 3) = 4 \quad (\text{no change})$$
$$d[D] = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 8) = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \pi[D] = C$$

| Vertex | $d[\cdot]$ | Visited? | $\pi$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $0$ | Yes | — |
| $B$ | $4$ | No | $A$ |
| $C$ | $2$ | **Yes** | $A$ |
| $D$ | $10$ | No | $C$ |
| $E$ | $\infty$ | No | — |

**Iteration 3:** Minimum unvisited → **$B$** ($d=4$). Mark visited. Relax $B \to C = 1$ (already visited, but relabelling doesn't help) and $B \to D = 5$:

$$d[D] = \min(10,\ 4 + 5) = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \pi[D] = B$$

| Vertex | $d[\cdot]$ | Visited? | $\pi$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $0$ | Yes | — |
| $B$ | $4$ | **Yes** | $A$ |
| $C$ | $2$ | Yes | $A$ |
| $D$ | $9$ | No | $B$ |
| $E$ | $\infty$ | No | — |

**Iteration 4:** Minimum unvisited → **$D$** ($d=9$). Mark visited. Relax $D \to E = 2$:

$$d[E] = \min(\infty,\ 9 + 2) = 11 \quad \Rightarrow \pi[E] = D$$

| Vertex | $d[\cdot]$ | Visited? | $\pi$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $0$ | Yes | — |
| $B$ | $4$ | Yes | $A$ |
| $C$ | $2$ | Yes | $A$ |
| $D$ | $9$ | **Yes** | $B$ |
| $E$ | $11$ | No | $D$ |

**Iteration 5:** Only $E$ remains. Mark visited. Final distances:

$$\boxed{d[A]=0,\ d[B]=4,\ d[C]=2,\ d[D]=9,\ d[E]=11}$$

**Shortest path from $A$ to $E$ (tracing $\pi$ backwards):**

$$E \leftarrow D \leftarrow B \leftarrow A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \to B \to D \to E$$

**Verification of total cost:** $4 + 5 + 2 = 11$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Minimum Spanning Tree (Kruskal's Algorithm)

**Problem:** Find the MST of the following undirected graph with 5 vertices.

**Edge List (sorted by weight):**

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| $(A,B)$ | $2$ |
| $(A,C)$ | $3$ |
| $(C,D)$ | $3$ |
| $(B,C)$ | $4$ |
| $(B,D)$ | $5$ |
| $(A,D)$ | $6$ |
| $(C,E)$ | $7$ |
| $(D,E)$ | $8$ |
| $(B,E)$ | $10$ |

We need $n - 1 = 4$ edges.

**Step-by-Step Union-Find Process:**

| Step | Edge Considered | Weight | Action | Components After |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(A,B)$ | $2$ | **Add** | $\{A,B\},\{C\},\{D\},\{E\}$ |
| 2 | $(A,C)$ | $3$ | **Add** | $\{A,B,C\},\{D\},\{E\}$ |
| 3 | $(C,D)$ | $3$ | **Add** | $\{A,B,C,D\},\{E\}$ |
| 4 | $(B,C)$ | $4$ | **Skip** (cycle: $A,B,C,A$) | $\{A,B,C,D\},\{E\}$ |
| 5 | $(B,D)$ | $5$ | **Skip** (cycle) | $\{A,B,C,D\},\{E\}$ |
| 6 | $(A,D)$ | $6$ | **Skip** (cycle) | $\{A,B,C,D\},\{E\}$ |
| 7 | $(C,E)$ | $7$ | **Add** | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ ✓ |

**Final MST Edge Set:** $\{(A,B), (A,C), (C,D), (C,E)\}$

**Total MST Weight:**

$$W_{\text{MST}} = 2 + 3 + 3 + 7 = 15$$

**Mermaid sketch of the MST:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A((A)) ---|2| B((B))
    A ---|3| C((C))
    C ---|3| D((D))
    C ---|7| E((E))
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note that we did *not* include the low-weight edge $(B,E)=10$ even though it was relatively cheap — including it would have created a cycle with the four edges already selected. This illustrates the core discipline of Kruskal's Algorithm.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Maximum Flow (Ford–Fulkerson Method)

**Problem:** Find the maximum flow from $s$ to $t$ in the network below. Capacities are written on each directed arc.

**Arc List (with capacities):**
- $s \to A = 10,\ s \to B = 8$
- $A \to B = 5,\ A \to C = 7$
- $B \to t = 12$
- $C \to B = 3,\ C \to t = 6$

#### Step 1 — Initial Flow: $f = 0$ everywhere.

#### Step 2 — Find Augmenting Path 1 (greedy: $s \to A \to B \to t$)

Bottleneck:

$$b = \min(c_{sA},\ c_{AB},\ c_{Bt}) = \min(10, 5, 12) = 5$$

Update flows:

$$f_{sA} = 5,\quad f_{AB} = 5,\quad f_{Bt} = 5$$

**Total flow so far:** $|F| = 5$.

#### Step 3 — Find Augmenting Path 2 ($s \to A \to C \to t$)

Residual capacities along this path:
- $s \to A$: $10 - 5 = 5$
- $A \to C$: $7 - 0 = 7$
- $C \to t$: $6 - 0 = 6$

Bottleneck:

$$b = \min(5,\ 7,\ 6) = 5$$

Update flows:

$$f_{sA} = 5 + 5 = 10,\quad f_{AC} = 5,\quad f_{Ct} = 5$$

**Total flow so far:** $|F| = 10$.

#### Step 4 — Find Augmenting Path 3 ($s \to B \to t$)

Residual capacities:
- $s \to B$: $8 - 0 = 8$
- $B \to t$: $12 - 5 = 7$

Bottleneck:

$$b = \min(8,\ 7) = 7$$

Update flows:

$$f_{sB} = 7,\quad f_{Bt} = 5 + 7 = 12$$

**Total flow so far:** $|F| = 17$.

#### Step 5 — Check for More Augmenting Paths

In the residual network:
- $s \to A$ is saturated (residual = 0).
- $s \to B$ has residual = $8 - 7 = 1$.
- From $B$, the only forward edge to $t$ is also saturated.

But there is a **backward residual edge** $B \to A$ with capacity $f_{AB} = 5$. We could attempt $s \to B \to A \to C \to t$:

Residual capacities:
- $s \to B$: $1$
- $B \to A$ (backward): $5$
- $A \to C$: $7 - 5 = 2$
- $C \to t$: $6 - 5 = 1$

Bottleneck:

$$b = \min(1,\ 5,\ 2,\ 1) = 1$$

Update flows:
- $f_{sB} = 7 + 1 = 8$
- $f_{BA} = 0 - 1 = -1$ ⟹ flows adjusted: $f_{AB}$ becomes $5 - 1 = 4$
- $f_{AC} = 5 + 1 = 6$
- $f_{Ct} = 5 + 1 = 6$

**Total flow so far:** $|F| = 18$.

#### Step 6 — Final Check

Residual network from $s$:
- $s \to A$: residual = 0 (saturated).
- $s \to B$: residual = 0 (saturated).

No further augmenting path exists. Algorithm terminates.

**Final Maximum Flow:**

$$\boxed{|F_{\max}| = 18}$$

**Verification by Cut:**
Consider the cut $S = \{s, B\}$, $T = \{A, C, t\}$:

$$C(S,T) = c_{sA} + c_{Bt} = 10 + 12 = 22 \quad (\text{upper bound})$$

Now consider the cut $S = \{s, A, B, C\}$, $T = \{t\}$:

$$C(S,T) = c_{At} + c_{Bt} + c_{Ct} = 0 + 12 + 6 = 18 \quad \checkmark$$

The minimum cut equals 18, confirming $|F_{\max}| = 18$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix (Comparison)

| Feature | Dijkstra | Prim / Kruskal (MST) | Ford–Fulkerson (Max Flow) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Objective | Min path cost | Min total spanning cost | Max throughput |
| Graph Type | Directed/Undirected | Undirected, connected | Directed, capacitated |
| Edge Weights | Non-negative | Positive | Capacities (non-negative) |
| Output | Distance + predecessor tree | Spanning tree | Flow values + value $|F|$ |
| Key Equation | Relaxation $d[v] = \min(\cdot)$ | Cut Property | Conservation + Min-Cut Max-Flow |
| Stopping Condition | All nodes visited | $n-1$ edges picked | No augmenting path |
| Greedy Choice | Smallest tentative $d[u]$ | Min-weight safe edge | Any path in residual network |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram — Dijkstra's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose source s] --> B[Initialize d[s]=0, d[v]=infinity for all v]
    B --> C[Insert all vertices into priority queue Q]
    C --> D{Q empty?}
    D -- No --> E[Extract vertex u with minimum d[u]]
    E --> F[Mark u as visited]
    F --> G[For each neighbor v of u still in Q]
    G --> H{d[u] + w(u,v) less than d[v]?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Update d[v] = d[u] + w(u,v), set pi[v] = u]
    H -- No --> J[Keep current d[v]]
    I --> K[Return to queue check]
    J --> K
    K --> D
    D -- Yes --> L[Output shortest distances d[ ] and predecessor tree pi]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Algorithmic Flow Diagram — Kruskal's MST Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read graph G with n vertices] --> B[Sort all edges in non-decreasing order of weight]
    B --> C[Initialize Union-Find: each vertex as its own set]
    C --> D[Initialize MST edge set T as empty]
    D --> E{Edges left to process?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Pick next edge e = u,v in sorted order]
    F --> G{Find u and Find v: in same set?}
    G -- No --> H[Add edge e to T, Union u and v]
    G -- Yes --> I[Skip edge e - would form cycle]
    H --> J{T has n-1 edges?}
    I --> J
    J -- No --> E
    J -- Yes --> K[Output MST T and total weight]
    E -- No --> L[Output MST T and total weight]
    K --> M[End]
    L --> M
```

### 4.3 Algorithmic Flow Diagram — Ford–Fulkerson Max Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input directed network G, source s, sink t] --> B[Initialize all flows f_ij = 0]
    B --> C[Build residual network G_f from current f]
    C --> D{Augmenting path P from s to t exists in G_f?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Find bottleneck b = min residual capacity along P]
    E --> F[For each forward arc in P: increase flow by b]
    F --> G[For each backward arc in P: decrease reverse flow by b]
    G --> H[Update total flow F = F + b]
    H --> C
    D -- No --> I[Apply Min-Cut Max-Flow theorem: identify min cut S,T]
    I --> J[Output maximum flow F_max and min cut]
    J --> K[End]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How the Three Algorithms Relate to a Generic Network Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Real World Network: Roads, Pipes, Cables]
        I2[Graph G = Vertices V, Edges E]
        I3[Weights or Capacities on Edges]
    end

    subgraph OBJECTIVE[Objective Layer]
        O1[Minimize path cost]
        O2[Minimize total connection cost]
        O3[Maximize throughput]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER[Algorithmic Layer]
        S1[Dijkstra: Greedy nearest vertex]
        S2[Prim or Kruskal: Greedy min safe edge]
        S3[Ford-Fulkerson: Augmenting paths in residual net]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        P1[Shortest Path Tree and distances]
        P2[Minimum Spanning Tree and total cost]
        P3[Maximum flow value F and min cut]
    end

    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> O1
    I3 --> O2
    I3 --> O3
    O1 --> S1
    O2 --> S2
    O3 --> S3
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
    S3 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:** A single physical network (e.g., a city) can be modelled once as a graph, but the *objective function* determines which solver is invoked. The same set of vertices and edges yields three completely different, mathematically optimal outputs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Dijkstra's Algorithm. Why does it fail for graphs with negative edge weights?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Dijkstra's Algorithm is a **greedy single-source shortest path algorithm** that works as follows: (1 mark)
1. Initialize $d[s] = 0$ and $d[v] = \infty$ for all other vertices.
2. Repeatedly select the unvisited vertex $u$ with the smallest tentative distance $d[u]$ and mark it visited.
3. Update the distances of $u$'s neighbours using the relaxation rule: $d[v] = \min(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u,v))$.

**Why it fails with negative edges (2 marks):**
The algorithm assumes that once a vertex is finalised, no shorter path can exist. If a negative-weight edge exists, a *later-discovered* path through a yet-unvisited vertex may turn out to be cheaper, violating this assumption. For example, after finalising vertex $B$ with $d[B]=4$ via path $A \to B$, a later relaxation $A \to C \to B$ with $w(A,C)=3$ and $w(C,B)=-5$ gives $d[B] = -2$, contradicting the already-finalised value.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Min-Cut Max-Flow theorem. What does the minimum cut represent in a real-world network?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Theorem (1 mark):** The maximum flow value from source $s$ to sink $t$ in a capacitated network equals the capacity of the minimum $(s,t)$-cut. Formally:

$$|F_{\max}| \;=\; \min_{(S,T):\, s \in S,\ t \in T} C(S,T) \;=\; \min_{(S,T)} \sum_{i \in S,\, j \in T} c_{ij}$$

**Real-world interpretation (2 marks):** The minimum cut identifies the **bottleneck set of arcs** in the network whose combined capacity most tightly constrains the throughput from source to sink. In practical terms, it pinpoints the pipes, cables, or roads that, if upgraded, would yield the greatest increase in network capacity. The Ford–Fulkerson algorithm terminates precisely when it finds this bottleneck.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Dijkstra's Algorithm problems, students frequently forget to update the **predecessor $\pi[v]$** when they update $d[v]$. Without $\pi$, you cannot reconstruct the actual shortest path tree — expect to lose 2 marks.
> - In MST problems, do not show all edges considered. Tabulate your work so the examiner can clearly see **which edges were added and which were skipped (with reason)**.
> - In Max Flow problems, students often forget to **check the residual network for backward-augmenting paths**. The examiner will test you with a network where the first greedy path is *not* the maximum — you must keep augmenting.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Dijkstra's Algorithm + Shortest Path Tree

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

Consider the directed graph below. Apply Dijkstra's Algorithm from source vertex $S$ to determine the shortest path to all other vertices. Also, construct the shortest path tree.

**Edge List:**
- $S \to A = 6$, $S \to B = 2$
- $A \to B = 3$, $A \to D = 1$
- $B \to A = 4$, $B \to C = 5$
- $C \to D = 2$, $C \to E = 4$
- $D \to C = 1$, $D \to E = 3$
- $E \to C = 0$ (ignore — irrelevant zero-cost cycle)

##### (a) [7 Marks] Apply Dijkstra's Algorithm step by step. Show the iteration table with $d[\cdot]$ and $\pi[\cdot]$ values at each stage.

**Model Solution:**

**Initialisation:**

| Vertex | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | $0$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | — | — | — | — | — | — |

**Iteration 1 — Pick $S$ ($d=0$).** Relax $S \to A = 6$, $S \to B = 2$:

$$d[A] = 0 + 6 = 6,\ \pi[A]=S;\quad d[B] = 0 + 2 = 2,\ \pi[B]=S$$

| Vertex | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | **0** | $6$ | $2$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | — | $S$ | $S$ | — | — | — |

**Iteration 2 — Pick $B$ ($d=2$).** Relax $B \to A = 5$ (wait: $B \to A$ via reverse direction is $4$ from list above). The relaxation of $B \to A = 4$:

$$d[A] = \min(6,\ 2 + 4) = 6 \quad (\text{no change})$$

Relax $B \to C = 5$:

$$d[C] = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 5) = 7,\ \pi[C]=B$$

| Vertex | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | $6$ | **2** | $7$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | — | $S$ | $S$ | $B$ | — | — |

**Iteration 3 — Pick $A$ ($d=6$).** Relax $A \to B = 3$ (already visited), $A \to D = 1$:

$$d[D] = \min(\infty,\ 6 + 1) = 7,\ \pi[D]=A$$

| Vertex | $S$ | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d$ | 0 | **6** | 2 | $7$ | $7$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi$ | — | $S$ | $S$ | $B$ | $A$ | — |

**Iteration 4 — Tie between $C$ and $D$ at $d=7$.** Choose $C$ first. Relax $C \to D = 2$ and $C \to E = 4$:

$$d[D] = \min(7,\ 7 + 2) = 7 \quad (\text{no change})$$
$$d[E] = \min(\infty,\ 7 + 4) = 11,\ \pi[E]=C$$

**Iteration 5 — Pick $D$ ($d=7$).** Relax $D \to C = 1$ (visited), $D \to E = 3$:

$$d[E] = \min(11,\ 7 + 3) = 10,\ \pi[E]=D$$

**Iteration 6 — Pick $E$ ($d=10$).** Algorithm terminates.

**Final Shortest Distances:**

$$d[S]=0,\ d[A]=6,\ d[B]=2,\ d[C]=7,\ d[D]=7,\ d[E]=10$$

**Shortest Path Tree** (tracing $\pi$ backwards from each node):
- $E \leftarrow D \leftarrow A \leftarrow S$ ⟹ $S \to A \to D \to E$ (cost $6+1+3 = 10$ ✓)
- $C \leftarrow B \leftarrow S$ ⟹ $S \to B \to C$ (cost $2+5=7$ ✓)

**Mermaid Shortest Path Tree:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    S((S)) --6--> A((A))
    S --2--> B((B))
    A --1--> D((D))
    B --5--> C((C))
    D --3--> E((E))
```

##### (b) [7 Marks] Verify your answer by listing all possible paths from $S$ to $E$ and computing their costs.

**Model Solution:**

All distinct directed paths from $S$ to $E$:

| Path | Cost Computation | Total |
|---|---|---|
| $S \to A \to D \to E$ | $6 + 1 + 3$ | $\mathbf{10}$ |
| $S \to A \to D \to C \to E$ | $6 + 1 + 1 + 4$ | $12$ |
| $S \to B \to C \to E$ | $2 + 5 + 4$ | $11$ |
| $S \to B \to C \to D \to E$ | $2 + 5 + 2 + 3$ | $12$ |
| $S \to A \to B \to C \to E$ | $6 + 3 + 5 + 4$ | $18$ |
| $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to E$ | $6 + 3 + 5 + 2 + 3$ | $19$ |
| $S \to B \to A \to D \to E$ | $2 + 4 + 1 + 3$ | $10$ |
| $S \to B \to A \to D \to C \to E$ | $2 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 4$ | $12$ |

**Minimum cost = 10**, achieved by $S \to A \to D \to E$ (and tied with $S \to B \to A \to D \to E$). This matches the algorithm's output of $d[E] = 10$. ✓

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Minimum Spanning Tree + Maximum Flow

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of the following undirected graph using **Kruskal's Algorithm**. List the edges in the order they are selected/skipped and compute the total MST cost.

**Edge List (vertices: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6):**

| Edge | Weight | | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1,2) | 3 | | (3,4) | 2 |
| (1,3) | 1 | | (3,6) | 8 |
| (2,3) | 4 | | (4,5) | 6 |
| (2,4) | 5 | | (4,6) | 7 |
| (2,5) | 9 | | (5,6) | 10 |

**Model Solution (Kruskal's):**

Need $n - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5$ edges.

Sorted edge list: (1,3)=1, (3,4)=2, (1,2)=3, (2,3)=4, (2,4)=5, (4,5)=6, (4,6)=7, (3,6)=8, (2,5)=9, (5,6)=10.

| Step | Edge | Weight | Action | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (1,3) | 1 | **Add** | Different components |
| 2 | (3,4) | 2 | **Add** | Merges 1-3 with 4 |
| 3 | (1,2) | 3 | **Add** | Merges 2 into main set |
| 4 | (2,3) | 4 | **Skip** | 2 and 3 already in same set (cycle) |
| 5 | (2,4) | 5 | **Skip** | Cycle |
| 6 | (4,5) | 6 | **Add** | Brings vertex 5 in |
| 7 | (4,6) | 7 | **Add** | Brings vertex 6 in — 5 edges done ✓ |

**Final MST Edge Set:** $\{(1,3),\ (3,4),\ (1,2),\ (4,5),\ (4,6)\}$

**Total MST Weight:**

$$W_{\text{MST}} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 6 + 7 = \mathbf{19}$$

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1((1)) ---|1| N3((3))
    N3 ---|2| N4((4))
    N1 ---|3| N2((2))
    N4 ---|6| N5((5))
    N4 ---|7| N6((6))
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the **Maximum Flow** from source $S$ to sink $T$ in the network below using the **Ford–Fulkerson Algorithm**. Show all augmenting paths and verify using the Min-Cut Max-Flow theorem.

**Arc Capacities:**
- $S \to 1 = 4$, $S \to 2 = 6$
- $1 \to 3 = 3$, $1 \to 2 = 2$
- $2 \to 1 = 1$, $2 \to 3 = 4$, $2 \to 4 = 5$
- $3 \to T = 5$, $4 \to 3 = 2$, $4 \to T = 7$

**Model Solution:**

**Initialisation:** All flows $f = 0$.

**Augmenting Path 1: $S \to 1 \to 3 \to T$**
Bottleneck = $\min(4, 3, 5) = 3$. Update: $f_{S1}=3,\ f_{13}=3,\ f_{3T}=3$. **Total flow = 3**.

**Augmenting Path 2: $S \to 2 \to 3 \to T$**
Residual capacities: $S \to 2 = 6$, $2 \to 3 = 4$, $3 \to T = 5 - 3 = 2$.
Bottleneck = $\min(6, 4, 2) = 2$. Update: $f_{S2}=2,\ f_{23}=2,\ f_{3T}=3+2=5$. **Total flow = 5**.

**Augmenting Path 3: $S \to 2 \to 4 \to T$**
Residual capacities: $S \to 2 = 6 - 2 = 4$, $2 \to 4 = 5$, $4 \to T = 7$.
Bottleneck = $\min(4, 5, 7) = 4$. Update: $f_{S2}=2+4=6,\ f_{24}=4,\ f_{4T}=4$. **Total flow = 9**.

**Augmenting Path 4: $S \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to T$ — but $4 \to 3$ has capacity 2, and $3 \to T$ is saturated (residual = 0).** We need a backward path. Consider the path: $S \to 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to T$ using backward edge $1 \to 2$:

Check: $S \to 1$ residual = $4 - 3 = 1$. Backward edge $2 \to 1$ exists with residual $= f_{12} = 0$ (no flow yet, so no backward). Hmm, backward edge $1 \to 2$ in the residual network has capacity $f_{12} = 0$. So this path fails.

Try: $S \to 2$ is saturated (residual 0), so no path from $S$ exists through 2.

Remaining: $S \to 1$ residual = 1. From 1, neighbours are 3 (saturated, residual = 0) and 2 (residual = 2 unused).

So path: $S \to 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to T$:
- $S \to 1$: residual 1
- $1 \to 2$: residual 2
- $2 \to 4$: residual $5 - 4 = 1$
- $4 \to 3$: residual 2
- $3 \to T$: residual 0 ✗

Still blocked at $3 \to T$. Algorithm terminates because the only path to $T$ goes through arc $3 \to T$ or $4 \to T$, and $4 \to T$ requires reaching node 4 which requires $S \to 2$ (saturated) or $S \to 1 \to 2 \to 4$ (but $2 \to 4$ is saturated) or $S \to 1 \to 2 \to 4$ via backward — but residual $2 \to 4 = 1$, so we *can* go: $S \to 1$ (res 1) $\to 2$ (res 2) $\to 4$ (res 1) $\to T$ (res $7-4=3$).

**Augmenting Path 4: $S \to 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to T$**
Bottleneck = $\min(1, 2, 1, 3) = 1$. Update: $f_{S1}=3+1=4,\ f_{12}=1,\ f_{24}=4+1=5,\ f_{4T}=4+1=5$. **Total flow = 10**.

**Final check:** $S \to 1$ saturated, $S \to 2$ saturated. No augmenting path exists.

**Final Maximum Flow:**

$$\boxed{|F_{\max}| = 10}$$

**Min-Cut Verification:**
Take $S = \{S, 1, 2, 4\}$, $T = \{3, T\}$. Cut capacity:

$$C(S,T) = c_{13} + c_{4T} + c_{4,3} = 3 + 7 + 2 = 12 \quad (\text{upper bound})$$

Try $S = \{S, 1, 2\}$, $T = \{3, 4, T\}$:

$$C(S,T) = c_{13} + c_{23} + c_{24} = 3 + 4 + 5 = 12 \quad (\text{still upper})$$

Try $S = \{S, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$, $T = \{T\}$:

$$C(S,T) = c_{3T} + c_{4T} = 5 + 7 = 12$$

Try $S = \{S\}$, $T = \{1, 2, 3, 4, T\}$:

$$C(S,T) = c_{S1} + c_{S2} = 4 + 6 = 10 \checkmark$$

This is the minimum cut, equal to the maximum flow of 10. ✓

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a **greedy** single-source shortest path algorithm; it requires **non-negative** edge weights and runs in $O(|E| \log |V|)$ with a min-heap.
- **Dijkstra fails on negative edges** because the greedy "finalise nearest vertex" rule can be invalidated by a later, shorter path through a negative edge.
- The **Shortest Path Tree** is reconstructed by tracing the **predecessor array $\pi$** backwards from each destination.
- A **Minimum Spanning Tree** has exactly $|V| - 1$ edges and contains **no cycles**.
- **Prim's Algorithm** grows the MST from a single root; **Kruskal's Algorithm** builds it by globally picking the smallest edge that doesn't form a cycle (uses **Union-Find**).
- The **Max Flow = Min Cut** theorem is the cornerstone duality result that justifies the Ford–Fulkerson termination criterion.
- **Ford–Fulkerson** iteratively augments flow along paths in the **residual network**; the bottleneck is the **minimum residual capacity** along the augmenting path.
- Always check for **backward residual arcs** — a non-greedy augmenting path can yield a higher final flow than the first greedy one.
- **Cut capacity** = sum of capacities of arcs going from set $S$ to set $T$ (NOT both directions) where $s \in S$ and $t \in T$.
- In KTU exams, the most common loss-of-marks errors are: forgetting $\pi[v]$ updates, not verifying with the cut theorem, and not considering backward augmenting paths in Max Flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm – The Minimum Cost Flow Problem – The Network Simplex Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT523 - OPERATIONAL RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm – The Minimum Cost Flow Problem – The Network Simplex Method # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm)** > The **Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm** is a greedy, iterative method used to compute the **maximum feasible flow** in a single-commodity, single-source–single-sink flow network. It systematically identifies *augmenting paths* from the source node $s$ to the sink node $t$ through the **residual network** $G_f(x)$ and increases the flow along each such path by the minimum residual capacity $\delta$ of its arcs, until no augmenting path exists. Let $G = (N, A)$ be a directed network where: - $N$ is the set of nodes (vertices) - $A$ is the set of arcs (directed edges) - Each arc $(i, j) \in A$ has a **capacity** $u_{ij} \geq 0$ and a **cost** $c_{ij}$ - A **source** $s \in N$ supplies flow, and a **sink** $t \in N$ demands flow - $b_i$ denotes the supply (if $b_i > 0$) or demand (if $b_i < 0$) at node $i$ ### The Three Foundational Constructs > [!NOTE] > **1. Flow Conservation Constraint** > For every intermediate node $k \in N \setminus \{s, t\}$: > $$\sum_{j : (k,j) \in A} x_{kj} - \sum_{i : (i,k) \in A} x_{ik} = 0$$ > *(What flows in must equal what flows out.)* > [!NOTE] > **2. Capacity Constraint** > For every arc $(i, j) \in A$: > $$0 \leq x_{ij} \leq u_{ij}$$ > [!NOTE] > **3. Objective (Max-Flow)** > Maximize the total flow leaving source $s$ (or equivalently entering sink $t$): > $$\max v \quad \text{where} \quad v = \sum_{j} x_{sj} = \sum_{i} x_{it}$$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River Network 🏞️ > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The River & Dams System** > Imagine a water distribution network where: > - A **reservoir** is the **source** $s$ > - A **city** is the **sink** $t$ > - **Pipes** are the **arcs** with maximum diameter = **capacity** $u_{ij}$ > - **Valves** are control points = **decision variables** $x_{ij}$ > > **Ford-Fulkerson's logic** is like a hydrologist who: > 1. **Finds ANY path** from reservoir to city through open pipes > 2. **Pushes as much water as possible** through the narrowest pipe on that path (the *bottleneck* = $\delta$) > 3. **Marks used capacity**, then looks for another path using *reverse* pipes to *redirect* water when needed > 4. **Stops** only when NO path exists — the **min-cut** acts like a dam wall whose total width equals the maximum flow! This is precisely the **Min-Cut Max-Flow Theorem**: the maximum flow equals the minimum cut capacity. --- ## 1.3 Minimum Cost Flow Problem — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Minimum Cost Flow — MCF)** > The **Minimum Cost Flow Problem** generalizes both the *transshipment* and *assignment* problems. It seeks a flow $x_{ij}$ through a capacitated network that **satisfies all node supply/demand requirements** at the **minimum total transportation cost**. > $$\min Z = \sum_{(i,j) \in A} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}$$ The MCF Problem (Mincost Flow Formulation): $$ \begin{aligned} \min \quad & Z = \sum_{(i,j) \in A} c_{ij} x_{ij} \\[4pt] \text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{j:(i,j)\in A} x_{ij} - \sum_{j:(j,i)\in A} x_{ji} = b_i \quad \forall\, i \in N \\[4pt] & l_{ij} \leq x_{ij} \leq u_{ij} \quad \forall\, (i,j) \in A \end{aligned} $$ where $b_i > 0$ for a **supply node**, $b_i < 0$ for a **demand node**, and $b_i = 0$ for a **transshipment node**. ### Special Cases of MCF (KTU-Favorite Question Topic!) | Special Case | Condition | Reduces To | |--------------|-----------|------------| | **Transportation Problem** | All $b_i$ integer, bipartite bipartite structure | Classical TP | | **Assignment Problem** | All $b_i = \pm 1$ | Hungarian Method | | **Transshipment Problem** | Mixed $b_i$ signs, intermediate transfers allowed | Transshipment TP | | **Shortest Path** | $s$ supplies 1 unit, $t$ demands 1 unit | Label Correcting / Dijkstra | | **Max-Flow** | Maximize $v$ at zero cost on capacity | Ford-Fulkerson | --- ## 1.4 Network Simplex Method — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Network Simplex Method — NSM)** > The **Network Simplex Method** is a specialized version of the **Simplex Method** that exploits the **network structure** of a flow problem. It maintains a **spanning tree basis** (rooted at node 1 or a reference node) of $n-1$ arcs (where $n = \vert N \vert$). Each iteration pivots one non-tree arc **into the basis** and one tree arc **out of the basis**, updating flows using a simple **inverse-basis update** rather than the full Simplex tableau. Key advantages over standard LP Simplex: - **Storage**: $O(n)$ instead of $O(m^2)$ - **Per-iteration cost**: $O(n)$ using spanning tree potentials - **Numerical stability**: Improved because bases are triangular - **Integers preserved**: If all $b_i, l_{ij}, u_{ij}$ are integers, so is the optimal flow # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Ford-Fulkerson — Algorithmic Logic in 5 Steps > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 0: Initialization** > Set $x_{ij} = 0$ for all $(i,j) \in A$. Let $v = 0$ (total flow value). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1: Construct the Residual Network $G_f(x)$** > For every arc $(i,j)$ with current flow $x_{ij}$ and capacity $u_{ij}$: > - **Forward residual capacity** (unused): $r_{ij} = u_{ij} - x_{ij}$ > - **Backward residual capacity** (flow that can be *undone*): $r_{ji} = x_{ij}$ > - Arc $(i,j)$ exists in $G_f(x)$ iff $r_{ij} > 0$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 2: Find an Augmenting Path $P$ from $s$ to $t$ in $G_f(x)$** > Use BFS/DFS. If none exists → STOP (optimal). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 3: Compute Bottleneck Capacity $\delta$** > $$\delta = \min_{(i,j) \in P} r_{ij}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 4: Augment Flow Along $P$ by $\delta$** > For each $(i,j) \in P$: > - If $(i,j) \in A$: $x_{ij} \leftarrow x_{ij} + \delta$ > - If $(j,i) \in A$ (i.e., reverse arc): $x_{ji} \leftarrow x_{ji} - \delta$ > - Update $v \leftarrow v + \delta$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 5: Go to Step 1** ### Complexity Note - **Basic Ford-Fulkerson**: $O(E \cdot v_{\max})$ — can be exponential in worst case - **Edmonds-Karp variant** (using BFS): $O(V \cdot E^2)$ — polynomial --- ## 2.2 Min-Cut Max-Flow Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (Menger, Ford-Fulkerson, 1956)** > The maximum value of a flow from $s$ to $t$ equals the minimum capacity of an $s$–$t$ cut. An **$s$–$t$ cut** $(S, \bar{S})$ partitions $N$ such that $s \in S$, $t \in \bar{S}$. Its capacity: $$C(S, \bar{S}) = \sum_{i \in S,\, j \in \bar{S}} u_{ij}$$ The cut found by FF when it terminates satisfies: $$v^* = C(S^*, \bar{S}^*) = \sum_{i \in S^*, j \notin S^*} u_{ij}$$ --- ## 2.3 Optimality Conditions for the Minimum Cost Flow Problem The MCF problem has three equivalent characterizations of optimality — **all three are KTU high-yield**: ### Condition A: Cycle Optimality > [!NOTE] > A feasible flow $x^*$ is optimal for the MCF problem **if and only if** the reduced cost $c_{ij}^{\pi}$ is **non-negative on every residual cycle** in $G_f(x^*)$, where: > $$c_{ij}^{\pi} = c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j$$ ### Condition B: Node Potential (Spanning Tree) Optimality > [!NOTE] > There exist **node potentials** $\pi_i$ such that for **every non-tree arc** $(i,j)$ at optimality: > - If $x_{ij} = l_{ij}$: $c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j \geq 0$ > - If $x_{ij} = u_{ij}$: $c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j \leq 0$ > - If $l_{ij} < x_{ij} < u_{ij}$: $c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j = 0$ ### Condition C: Spanning Tree Reduced Cost > [!NOTE] > For all **tree arcs** $(i,j)$: $c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j = 0$ (used to solve for $\pi$) > This gives a unique $\pi$ since the tree has $n-1$ arcs and $n$ unknowns. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition | |--------|---------|---------------------| | $G = (N, A)$ | Directed network | $N$ = nodes, $A$ = arcs | | $u_{ij}$ | Arc capacity | $0 \leq x_{ij} \leq u_{ij}$ | | $c_{ij}$ | Arc cost per unit flow | Objective coefficient | | $b_i$ | Supply (positive) / demand (negative) | $\sum_i b_i = 0$ required | | $x_{ij}$ | Flow on arc $(i,j)$ | Decision variable | | $r_{ij}$ | Residual capacity (forward) | $u_{ij} - x_{ij}$ | | $r_{ji}$ | Residual capacity (backward) | $x_{ij}$ | | $\delta$ | Bottleneck of augmenting path | $\min_{(i,j) \in P} r_{ij}$ | | $v$ | Total flow value (FF) | $\sum_j x_{sj}$ | | $C(S, \bar{S})$ | Cut capacity | $\sum_{i \in S, j \notin S} u_{ij}$ | | $\pi_i$ | Node potential | $c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j = 0$ on tree | | $c_{ij}^{\pi}$ | Reduced cost | $c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j$ | | $\theta$ | Min ratio in NSM | $\min\{$ ratios along cycle $\}$ | | $B$ | Basis (spanning tree) | $\vert B \vert = n - 1$ | | $L$ | Non-tree arc set | $\vert L \vert = m - (n-1)$ | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where these algorithms are deployed in industry:** > - **Telecommunications**: Routing bandwidth through fiber networks, maximizing packet flow > - **Logistics & Supply Chain**: Min-cost shipment of goods from factories (sources) to warehouses (sinks) — used by **Amazon, FedEx, UPS** daily > - **Power Grids**: Optimal power flow with minimum transmission loss > - **Airline Scheduling**: Crew routing and aircraft assignment (NSM solves these in seconds vs hours for full LP) > - **Water Resource Engineering**: Pipeline capacity design and reservoir distribution > - **VLSI Chip Design**: Wire routing on circuit boards # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU Standard Format):** Consider the network below with source $s = 1$, sink $t = 4$. Arc capacities are: > - $(1,2)$: 7, $(1,3)$: 5, $(2,3)$: 3, $(2,4)$: 4, $(3,4)$: 6 > Find the maximum flow using Ford-Fulkerson. ### Iteration 1: First Augmenting Path **Path selected (using BFS):** $1 \to 2 \to 4$ - $r_{12} = 7$, $r_{24} = 4$ - $\delta_1 = \min(7, 4) = 4$ **Flow update:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{12} &\leftarrow 0 + 4 = 4 \\ x_{24} &\leftarrow 0 + 4 = 4 \\ v &\leftarrow 0 + 4 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ ### Iteration 2: Second Augmenting Path **Path selected:** $1 \to 3 \to 4$ - $r_{13} = 5$, $r_{34} = 6$ - $\delta_2 = \min(5, 6) = 5$ **Flow update:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{13} &\leftarrow 0 + 5 = 5 \\ x_{34} &\leftarrow 0 + 5 = 5 \\ v &\leftarrow 4 + 5 = 9 \end{aligned} $$ ### Iteration 3: Third Augmenting Path (Requires Backward Arc!) Now arcs $(1,2), (2,4), (1,3), (3,4)$ are all *saturated* or partially used. Build residual network: - $r_{12} = 7 - 4 = 3$ (forward, available) - $r_{13} = 5 - 5 = 0$ (forward, none) - $r_{21} = 4$ (backward, can undo) - $r_{24} = 4 - 4 = 0$ (forward, none) - $r_{42} = 4$ (backward, can undo) - $r_{34} = 6 - 5 = 1$ (forward, available) **Path selected:** $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$ (using arc $(2,3)$ forward) - $r_{12} = 3$, $r_{23} = 3$, $r_{34} = 1$ - $\delta_3 = \min(3, 3, 1) = 1$ **Flow update:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{12} &\leftarrow 4 + 1 = 5 \\ x_{23} &\leftarrow 0 + 1 = 1 \\ x_{34} &\leftarrow 5 + 1 = 6 \\ v &\leftarrow 9 + 1 = 10 \end{aligned} $$ ### Iteration 4: Termination Check Now all forward arcs from $s=1$ are exhausted: $r_{12} = 2$, $r_{13} = 0$. **No augmenting path from 1 to 4 exists.** STOP. ### Final Max-Flow Result $$ \boxed{v^* = 10} $$ ### Min-Cut Identification The set $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ separates source from sink. Cut arcs from $S$ to $\bar{S} = \{4\}$: - $(2, 4)$: $u_{24} = 4$ (saturated, $x_{24} = 4$) - $(3, 4)$: $u_{34} = 6$ (saturated, $x_{34} = 6$) $$ C(S, \bar{S}) = 4 + 6 = 10 = v^* \quad \checkmark \text{ (Min-Cut Max-Flow Theorem verified)} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Network Simplex Method (NSM) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Solve the following MCF problem to optimality using Network Simplex: > - **Nodes**: 1, 2, 3, 4 with supplies/demands: $b_1 = 4, b_2 = 6, b_3 = -4, b_4 = -6$ > - **Arcs** (cost, capacity, lower bound): > - $(1,3)$: cost 5, $0 \leq x \leq 10$ > - $(1,4)$: cost 3, $0 \leq x \leq 10$ > - $(2,3)$: cost 2, $0 \leq x \leq 8$ > - $(2,4)$: cost 6, $0 \leq x \leq 12$ ### Step 1: Build Initial Spanning Tree Basis Choose a starting spanning tree with **root at node 1**. A valid initial basis $B = \{(1,3), (1,4), (2,3)\}$ contains $n - 1 = 3$ arcs forming a tree on 4 nodes. > [!NOTE] > **Feasibility check of tree basis**: We need to solve the flow conservation equations using the tree arcs. Since the basis must allow computation of basic flows, we add an **artificial arc** at node 1 with infinite cost if needed. For this example, the chosen tree is feasible. ### Step 2: Compute Node Potentials $\pi$ For tree arcs, set $c_{ij} - \pi_i + \pi_j = 0$. With $\pi_1 = 0$ (reference): **From arc $(1,3)$:** $5 - 0 + \pi_3 = 0 \Rightarrow \pi_3 = -5$ **From arc $(1,4)$:** $3 - 0 + \pi_4 = 0 \Rightarrow \pi_4 = -3$ **From arc $(2,3)$:** $2 - \pi_2 + \pi_3 = 0 \Rightarrow \pi_2 = 2 + \pi_3 = 2 + (-5) = -3$ So potentials: $\pi_1 = 0, \pi_2 = -3, \pi_3 = -5, \pi_4 = -3$. ### Step 3: Check Optimality of Non-Tree Arcs The only non-tree arc is $(2,4)$. Compute its reduced cost: $$ c_{24}^{\pi} = c_{24} - \pi_2 + \pi_4 = 6 - (-3) + (-3) = 6 $$ Since $c_{24}^{\pi} = 6 > 0$ and lower bound is 0 (so we cannot decrease flow on it), **and it is not in basis**, the current solution is... wait — we must check the optimality condition for bringing arc **into** the basis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule:** If $c_{ij}^{\pi} < 0$, arc $(i,j)$ is eligible to **increase** flow (enter basis). If $c_{ij}^{\pi} > 0$, arc $(j,i)$ is eligible (enter basis by increasing flow on reverse, i.e., decreasing $(i,j)$). For our non-tree arc $(2,4)$: $c_{24}^{\pi} = 6 > 0$, so we look at the **reverse direction** $(4,2)$ with $c_{42}^{\pi} = -c_{24}^{\pi} = -6 < 0$. This means we can **enter arc $(4,2)$** (i.e., push flow from 4 back to 2), or equivalently, **decrease flow on $(2,4)$** from 0 to a negative value — but $(2,4)$ is currently 0. So we consider increasing flow on the *forward* direction $(2,4)$ only if $c_{24}^{\pi} \leq 0$. Since $c_{24}^{\pi} = 6 > 0$, we cannot improve by increasing $(2,4)$. **Conclusion:** No improving non-tree arc → **Current solution is optimal**! 🎯 ### Step 4: Recover Optimal Flows For tree arc $(1,3)$: $x_{13} = ?$. Solve using flow conservation. Since this is the simple case, the optimal flows are: - $x_{13} = 4$ (covers demand at node 3 partially) - $x_{14} = 0$ (no flow needed) - $x_{23} = 0$ (no flow needed) - $x_{24} = 6$ (covers demand at node 4) > [!NOTE] > **Verification of flow conservation at every node:** > - **Node 1**: out = $x_{13} + x_{14} = 4 + 0 = 4 = b_1$ ✓ > - **Node 2**: out = $x_{23} + x_{24} = 0 + 6 = 6 = b_2$ ✓ > - **Node 3**: in = $x_{13} + x_{23} = 4 + 0 = 4$, demand = 4 ✓ > - **Node 4**: in = $x_{14} + x_{24} = 0 + 6 = 6$, demand = 6 ✓ ### Step 5: Compute Optimal Cost $$ Z^* = 5(4) + 3(0) + 2(0) + 6(6) = 20 + 0 + 0 + 36 = \boxed{56} $$ --- ## 3.3 Entering Arc and Min-Ratio Test (General Procedure) When an improving non-tree arc $(p, q)$ with $c_{pq}^{\pi} < 0$ is found: > [!IMPORTANT] > **1. Form the fundamental cycle** $W$ by adding $(p, q)$ to the current tree $T$. This creates exactly one cycle. > > **2. Traverse the cycle** starting at $p$: > - Forward arcs (in tree direction matching $p \to q$): let their flow be $x_f$ > - Backward arcs: let their flow be $x_b$ > > **3. Compute $\theta^*$ — the minimum ratio:** > $$\theta^* = \min \left[ \min_{\text{forward } f} (u_f - x_f), \ \min_{\text{backward } b} (x_b - l_b) \right]$$ > > **4. Update flows** by adding $\theta^*$ along forward direction and subtracting from backward direction. > > **5. The arc that achieves the minimum** becomes the **leaving arc** — the new basis is the cycle minus the leaving arc plus $(p, q)$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the **lower bound** $l_{ij}$ in the min-ratio test. The denominator for backward arcs is $x_b - l_b$, not just $x_b$. Marks are deducted for this. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Step | Ford-Fulkerson | Network Simplex Method | |------|----------------|------------------------| | **Problem type** | Max flow | Min-cost flow (general) | | **Maintains** | Feasible flow $x$ | Spanning tree basis + feasible flow | | **Improving object** | Augmenting path | Non-tree arc with negative reduced cost | | **Leaves** | N/A (augments, doesn't pivot) | One tree arc (via min-ratio test) | | **Information used** | Bottleneck $\delta$ | Reduced cost $c_{ij}^{\pi}$ and node potentials $\pi$ | | **Termination** | No augmenting path | All $c_{ij}^{\pi} \geq 0$ on eligible arcs | | **Output** | Max flow + min cut | Optimal flows + min cost $Z^*$ | | **Complexity** | $O(VE^2)$ with BFS | $O(n)$ per iteration, $O(m)$ total pivot rule | | **KTU weight** | 7-8 marks typical | 14 marks (full question) | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Ford-Fulkerson Network Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start FF Algorithm]) --> Init[Initialize x_ij = 0
